## 2026-07-06 (batch 311: page_3101-page_3110)

**The "Knotty Stituation" closes with a supplier making good, Derek's mast
takes final shape, and Bill Serjeant posts his first real build-progress
photos** — the trigger for his own dormant build-detail watch item. One boat
page and two reference/person pages extended; no new person or boat pages
created this batch.

- **`boats/enuf.md`** — extended "Finishing the spars" with the saga's
  resolution: Robbins Timber's manager, Gordon Lamb, phoned Derek personally,
  apologized, and sent a free replacement piece (no charge for timber or
  carriage), declining to collect the faulty offcut back. New section
  "Storing and stepping the mast": planing the mast blank revealed the
  plans' side blanks are tapered in thickness as well as shaped fore-and-aft
  (corrected by bandsaw/plane); the mast reached final shape and was
  photographed in position; Alastair's stowage question and 3.25 nm sculling
  report; Derek's reply (storing the mast inside against trailer-theft worry
  at motorway services; discovering and fixing a missing halyard cutout in
  the sail's head sleeve). Cites [115]-[119], 119 total, no gaps.
- **`people/bill-serjeant.md`** — new paragraph recording his first actual
  build-progress report: photos of deck beams, cabin beams, and hatch beams
  posted to a freshly updated `paradox-uk.co.uk` and mirrored to
  `paradoxbuilders2`'s photo section, plus his request for other UK
  builders' details for the site's registry. This is the trigger event for

## 2026-07-06 (batch 310: page_3091-page_3100)

**"Derek's Knotty Stituation" — the boom-knot saga runs to its conclusion,**
with a supplier complaint, a running list joke, and the underlying wood-grain
physics behind why knots weaken spars. One boat page and two person pages
extended; no new person or boat pages created this batch.

- **`boats/enuf.md`** — extended the existing "Finishing the spars" section:
  too late to drill/plug the boom's knot once shaped (Wayne); Derek's own
  diagnosis (one end of the knot shrunk from the wood) and hypodermic-needle
  epoxy-injection plan, plus the practical lesson to examine timber more
  carefully in the rough and the table saw "Enuf" justified buying; Jeff
  (UK)'s suggestion to contact the timber supplier, Robbins Timber of
  Bristol, directly; Derek's phone call and follow-up written complaint with
  photos; Chris Ostlind's running joke that named the thread; Bill
  Serjeant's own note about checking his newly ordered Robbins timber for
  knots; and Bruce Hallman's grain-runout physics (a scarf joint above 8:1
  runout is the real fix, not epoxy alone), endorsed and extended by Jack
  Gardiner (a repaired/knotted section won't flex evenly with the rest of a
  loaded spar). Cites [106]-[114], 114 total, no gaps.
- **`people/jack-gardiner.md`** — narrate-once cross-link addendum for his
  grain-runout-repair agreement, linking to `boats/enuf.md`'s section. Cite
  [190], 189 total/190 numeric max (pre-existing +1 gap preserved).
- **`people/bill-serjeant.md`** — new short paragraph under "Contributions to
  design discussion" for his Robbins-timber note, cross-linked to
  `boats/enuf.md`. Cite [13], 13 total/13 max, no gaps.
- **Backlog:** no Open item's trigger condition was met this batch (Jeff-UK,
  botebilder/Ernie Fossel, Jason Stancil, below-threshold Larry, and the full
  watch-list all checked, none resurfaced or newly triggered); David
  Platten's dormant promotion watch and Bill Serjeant's own further-build-
  detail watch remain Open/dormant — Bill's appearance this batch was a
  passing remark, not new build-progress detail. No new below-threshold
  correspondents — Wayne and Bruce Hallman are the only non-standing names,
  both one-off factual-answer correspondents with no boat/build of their own.
- **Identity/attribution checks:** Derek Clark, Jack Gardiner ("John
  Gardiner"), Jeff (UK, unconfirmed vs. `doryman_uk`), Chris Ostlind, Bruce
  Hallman, and William/Bill Serjeant all confirmed via headers against their

## 2026-07-06 (batch 307: page_3061-page_3070)

**Derek Clark's "Enuf" bottom finished (hard and shiny, after an exothermic
epoxy scare), plus the tiller thru-hull seal/ventilation trade-off and a
toe-rail question — both settled with real firsthand data from David Beard
and Jack Gardiner.** One boat page and three concept/person pages extended;
no new person or boat pages created this batch.

- **`boats/enuf.md`** — three new sections: "The bottom finished: an
  exothermic epoxy scare, and turning the hull upright" (a large 1.5 litre
  batch overheated in the mixing pot mid-application; bottom completed and
  hull turned upright the same week), "Sealing the tiller thru-hull, and the
  ventilation trade-off it exposes" (bug-tight sealing vs. airflow,
  including a winter-sailing plug-the-holes point), and "Toe rail: is it
  needed, and where to source one in the UK" (settled by David Beard's
  firsthand season-without-one experience), plus a short "Mast blanks"
  note marking where Derek's build stood as of this batch. Cites [79]-[86],
  86 total, no gaps.
- **`construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md`** — new section "An exothermic
  runaway in the mixing pot": the durable lesson that a large batch left
  sitting in one container concentrates cure heat far more than the same
  volume spread thin, so batches should be kept small and spread promptly.
  Cite [83], 84 total/83 numeric max (pre-existing [29a] suffix unaffected).
- **`construction/transom-deck-plate.md`** — extended the existing "vent
  baffle" section with two builders' wetsuit-neoprene and rubber-membrane
  tiller-seal techniques (both aimed at keeping out bugs while trailered,
  both reintroducing the ventilation trade-off) and Jack Gardiner's
  vent-trunk 12V cooling-fan idea. Cites [37]-[40], 40 total, no gaps.
- **`people/david-beard.md`** — narrate-once cross-link addendum: his own
  wetsuit-neoprene tiller seal, winter-ventilation plug-the-holes point, and
  toe-rail experience, folded into the existing "Other durable data points"
  paragraph rather than re-narrated. Cites [245]-[246], 246 total, no gaps.
- **Backlog:** no Open item's trigger condition was met by these ten pages
  (David Platten and Bill Serjeant items untouched — neither appeared this
  batch); no new below-threshold correspondent surfaced (Bill/`bllfsh666`,
  a one-off neoprene-sourcing reply, folded directly into `boats/enuf.md`'s
  citations rather than logged as a watch item, since he contributed one
  practical tip and nothing suggesting a recurring presence).

## 2026-07-06 (batch 305: page_3041-page_3050)

**The second "Enuf" epoxy failure closes out — a new mat-and-squeegee rescue
technique after neat hardener fails to repeat, plus a closed mainsheet
question and two new correspondents.** No new files created; five existing
files extended.

- **`design/rig.md`** — extended the frozen "Mainsheet fouling on the rudder
  and rear deck with an outboard fitted" heading: David Beard finally
  confirms (a batch after Derek's follow-up question) his 2 hp Honda is the
  **short-shaft** model, and explains why he prefers it over a long-shaft
  Yamaha he also owns — the motor's lowest point stays above the hull's when
  fully lowered, so the hull grounds first. Cite [227], 229 total/227
  numeric max (pre-existing [7a]/[7b] pair unaffected).
- **`boats/enuf.md`** — fixed a citation mismatch in "Mainsheet, mast, and
  outboard" (the prior sentence cited Derek's *question*, not David's
  answer) and added the real answer with its own citation. Extended "A
  second epoxy failure: a faulty dispensing pump" with the saga's close:
  David Platten's neat-hardener rescue (from batch 304) did **not** repeat
  for Derek this time — a test patch stayed unset — but laying fiberglass
  mat over the tacky coating and squeegeeing a fresh mix through it set
  solid on the rudder blade/stock, while the hull bottom itself was
  stripped back to bare cloth with a scraper; Platten also suggested
  metering by veterinary syringe instead of pumps, and Derek settled for
  graduated measuring jugs. Closes with Platten introducing himself to the
  list (sails a Breton lugger, weighing a Paradox as boat "number nine")
  and David Beard's welcome. Cites [71]-[76], 76 total, no gaps.
- **`construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md`** — extended "Diagnosing and
  rescuing a coat that will not cure" with the mat-and-squeegee rescue
  technique (for when neat hardener alone doesn't retake), the scraper
  fallback, and the veterinary-syringe metering alternative to dispensing
  pumps. Cites [81]-[82], 83 total/82 numeric max (pre-existing [29a]
  suffix unaffected).
- **`people/david-beard.md`** — narrate-once cross-link for his short-shaft
  confirmation and reasoning. Cite [244], 244 total, no gaps.
- **`construction/deck-and-cabin-beams.md`** — new section "Practicing the
  lamination process, and the case for patience during cure": a new
  correspondent, William "Bill" Serjeant, starts his own deck beams after a
  practice run, with Bill's own patience advice to Derek Clark ("take the
  rest, it will pay dividends") folded in alongside Derek's 24-hour
  clamp-cure discipline. Cite [39], 39 total, no gaps.

  total.

  Wayne ("In the Swamp") suggests patience and moderate heat; Chris Ostlind
  gives the fullest post-cure recipe on record (sun plus black plastic
  sheeting, fingernail/screwdriver-tip resistance test, up to 200°F for two
  hours); Jack Gardiner and Glen Maxwell debate whether more hardener can
  rescue an already-mismixed coat — Glen's molecular-bonding-versus-polyester
  explanation settles it (no, except where only resin with no hardener ever
  reached the wood). Also extended the "Mainsheet, mast, and outboard"
  section with Derek's follow-up to David Beard on the transom
  travelling-block idea, and closed the "photo-folder mystery" section with
  Derek's own "Molduc"→"Bolduc" self-correction. Cites [36]-[43], 43 total.
- **`design/rig.md`** — resolved the standing "remains an open question" note
  under "Mainsheet fouling on the rudder and rear deck with an outboard
  fitted": David Beard confirms, over a year after his transom-rod fix, that
  he never added a travelling block (never enough of a problem to justify
  it) and still runs the same 2 hp Honda. Cites [218]-[219], 219 total.
- **`construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md`** — new section "Diagnosing and
  rescuing a coat that will not cure," generalizing the Enuf epoxy-cure
  failure into a durable technique (sun/heat post-cure, scrap-test to isolate
  mix-ratio/contamination/bad-hardener causes, why extra hardener can't
  rescue a bad mix, when to scrape and restart), cross-linked to the full
  story on `boats/enuf.md`. Cite [76], 76 total.
- **`people/david-beard.md`** — added David's answer on the travelling block
  and 2 hp Honda, cross-linked to `design/rig.md`. Cite [243], 243 total
  (renumbered up from an initial [240] draft after discovering the file's
  citation list is not in strict ascending order past [239] — true max was
  242, not 239; no pre-existing citation touched).
- **`people/jack-gardiner.md`** — citation-only pointer for his three
  epoxy-troubleshooting replies, per the file's standing "full text and
  citation at" convention for Enuf-related content. Cite [184], 184 total
  (pre-existing [53] gap unaffected).
- **`people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`** — citation-only pointer for
  Glen's epoxy-chemistry explanation. Cite [240], 240 total.
- **`people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`** — one-sentence
  addendum to the existing "Enuf photo-folder mystery" section closing
  paragraph for Derek's "Molduc"/"Bolduc" correction. Cite [107], 107 total.

No genuinely new correspondents — all ten messages from four already-standing
identities (Derek Clark/`dclark52001`, David Beard/`buster38801`, Jack
Gardiner/`alex29`/`tidybowlmann` posting as "John Gardiner", Glen Maxwell/
`ifida2000`) plus two established below-threshold commentators (Wayne/
`wtorry`/`wayneski812`, "In the Swamp"; Chris Ostlind/`wedgesail`). Derek
Clark authored 3 of 10 messages; his Enuf-build content continues to land on
the now-promoted `boats/enuf.md` + `people/derek-clark.md` pair per the
post-3000 review, not scattered. Attribution checks: David Beard (`dlb@`/
`buster38801`) confirmed distinct from David LeBlanc throughout; Jack
Gardiner confirmed distinct from John Hall; no two-UK-Jeffs/three-Larrys/
two-Roberts-Rons collisions triggered. Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and
the Jeff-UK/botebilder identity items remain open, untouched this batch.
msgnum run 3032-3041 confirmed distinct and consecutive, continuing from
batch 300's last msgnum 3031; standing archive gaps at 2122/2190/2229/2385/
2611/2614/2753 unaffected, no new gaps. All ten source byte sizes matched
the dispatch table exactly. Archive timeline reached 2 August 2005. Anchor
checker: 0 broken across 2,307 links. Cursor set to 3010.

## 2026-07-05 (user-directed: Andrew Layden site ingested; Mini Paradox = Enigma, page merged & deleted)

**Ingested Andy Layden's BGSU site** as a new Reference concept
`references/andrew-layden-website.md` (linked from `references/index.md`), with
back-links from `matt-layden/{enigma,mini-paradox,biography}.md`. Documents the
brother relationship (Andy's own words), the first-person build logs (a second
source of Matt's own voice beyond the one mailing-list post), and what the
construction photos teach (Rhino 3D confirmed on screen; chine-runner build
photographed; stitch-and-glue sequence; finished Enigma with fold-down soft
cabin; grey Rob Roy-oid).

**User adjudication — "Mini Paradox" is not a separate boat:** it was the
`paradoxbuilders` list's informal 2005 working name for **Enigma** during her
construction. microcruising.com (the authority on Matt's designs) documents the
same 2005 boat only as Enigma and never uses "Mini Paradox"; convergent primary
detail confirms it (same year, "V" bottom at the bow, "shorter and wider" chine
runners, cartop-light, sailed two-up by Matt + Karen, same brother's-site build
photos).

- **Deleted `matt-layden/mini-paradox.md`.** Its full content and all 16 archive
  citations (the June 2005 "Mini Paradox" thread, page_2758–page_2825) were
  folded into `matt-layden/enigma.md` under a new section, "The 'Mini Paradox'
  working name and the 2005 construction-era discussion" (citations [13]–[28]).
- **Repointed every inbound link** off the deleted page: `matt-layden/index.md`
  (dropped the Mini Paradox design bullet; noted the alias on the Enigma bullet),
  `matt-layden/biography.md` (2 spots), `references/andrew-layden-website.md`
  (2 links), `people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md` (section reframed to
  "Sailing aboard Enigma (Matt Layden's 'Mini Paradox')" + 4 citation pointers).
  Historical batch-narration links to `mini-paradox.md` earlier in this log are
  left as-is (they record the file as it existed then).

## 2026-07-05 (holistic review @ 3000 — FP3: Matt Layden bio re-voice)

**Third of six fix passes from the holistic review triggered at cursor
3000.** Scope: `matt-layden/biography.md` only. Full rationale and outline in
`.okf-progress/review-3000/A4-layden-dossier.md`; verdict there was
**enrich, not rebuild** — the page was already the best-sourced in the
bundle (56 citations), so this pass re-voiced it from a chronological
evidence ledger into a flowing biographical narrative, without any new
research or re-verification of citations.

**Restructured** around the dossier's 10-point arc: lede; origins and
formation (New England sailing family, cabinet-shop apprenticeship, design
philosophy); the decade of boats (Terrapin's knockdown through Paradox);
Karen, the Balboa 20, and settling down; Paradox and the 2003 Everglades
Challenge (eyewitness account, the race, the win, after-the-win, racing
beyond Paradox); the chine runner (summarized and cross-linked rather than
duplicated, plus Matt's own rigorous self-testing); the Watertribe years
(Enigma, Sand Flea, Elusion, plus "Big Cruiser"/"Mini Paradox"/"Lugger #2");
the man himself (Cameron's 2009 profile, the Bolducs' and Cameron's character
portraits, a new subsection sharpening firsthand vs. secondhand vs.
journalist-narration sourcing); the "Mattsplatt" identity (still
unconfirmed); legacy; and a new closing **"What the record doesn't tell
us"** section owning the documented gaps (no first-person account of the
2003 EC win, an almost wholly undocumented mega-yacht career, nothing after
2012, no verified full name/birthdate, only one verbatim Matt quote in the
whole archive). Every existing factual claim kept its original `[n]`
citation marker; the Citations block itself was left untouched.

**Anchor safety:** of the original headings, 5 have inbound cross-file
anchors (`#the-14-ft-rob-roy-based-canoe-and-the-2005-everglades-challenge`,
`#other-craft-and-events`, `#asides-and-footnotes`,
`#the-2003-everglades-challenge-an-eyewitness-account`, `#the-win`) and were
kept verbatim as short pointer stubs into their new narrative homes so nothing
broke; one duplicate `## The win` stub (created when a pointer section
temporarily reused an anchored heading) was caught and removed before
finalizing. All other headings were freely restructured. Verified by
repo-wide grep: no dangling `biography.md#...` inbound link remains.

**Also fixed:** the file's own three links to the moved
`/design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md` stub were repointed to
`/chine-runners/how-they-work.md` (mechanism/philosophy summary) and
`/chine-runners/windward-performance.md` (the 2001 quantified sea trial),
per context; a third mention (Swamp Thing's first use of the chine runner)
now points to `/chine-runners/index.md`. The frontmatter `description` was
tightened to one concise present-tense sentence.

## 2026-07-05 (holistic review @ 3000 — FP2: New nodes)

**Second of six fix passes from the holistic review triggered at cursor
3000.** Scope: create the three boat nodes and one person node the process
itself deferred to this exact review, narrating each build/launch story once
on its own boat page and cross-linking rather than duplicating. Full
rationale in `.okf-progress/review-3000/A2-missing-boats.md` and
`.okf-progress/review-3000/A3-missing-people.md`; task list in
`.okf-progress/review-3000/MASTER-WORKLIST.md` (FP2).

**Created (4 pages):**
- `boats/jill.md` — Jack Gardiner's Rhode Island Paradox, launched at Gaspee
  Point, Narragansett Bay, 11 September 2004. Consolidates the launch
  narrative, the small-craft-advisory sail with the rudder-blade trailer
  rescue, the windward-performance "check the tides" trial, and the full
  "paradox mpeg movie" BitTorrent-video saga (two days in 20-35 mph wind,
  two adults plus tool ballast) — all previously narrated only on
  `people/jack-gardiner.md`.
- `boats/enuf.md` — Derek Clark's UK Paradox, renamed from an early working
  name "So-So" on his wife's suggestion. Consolidates the fullest single
  ballast layout in the archive, the hull-turning/bottom-sheathing void
  repair, the tack-strut/boom-axle pivot-joint fabrication (galvanised pipe
  vs. brass, settling on an epoxy-coating fix), the "three-tone green with a
  yellow bottom" paint-scheme thread, and the "Enuf" photo-folder mystery
  with Derek's closing thank-you to the list — previously scattered across
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`,
  `design/weight-and-ballast.md`, `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md`,
  `construction/topside-painting.md`, and `design/rig.md`.
- `people/derek-clark.md` — companion person page (removed from the
  below-threshold watch-list after 15+ batches "STRENGTHENED but not
  promoted"); short biography, community role in the mid-2005 Don Elliott
  ban revisiting, and non-boat contributions (mast/mainsheet questions),
  with the full build narrative cross-linked to `boats/enuf.md`.
- `boats/swash.md` — DC's Columbia River-area Paradox, built strictly to the
  plans. Consolidates the name origin ("Introducing Swash"), the "Owning
  Swash" home-waters/ownership-philosophy passage, and the July 2004
  for-sale-during-a-divorce episode (kept and finished rather than sold) —
  previously on `people/dc.md` and `operations/secondhand-market.md`.

**Trimmed/cross-linked on source pages:**
- `people/jack-gardiner.md` — "Launch: 'Jill'" section (previously the full
  narrative) reduced to a short summary + link to `boats/jill.md`; the
  "paradox mpeg movie" firsthand account and the "Torrent support for the
  'beaching' video" section's boat-specific content likewise reduced to
  summaries + links, keeping the BitTorrent/file-sharing mechanics (not
  boat-specific) in place. Both headings — `#launch-jill` and
  `#torrent-support-for-the-beaching-video-and-a-canadian-toast-interlude`
  — **kept** rather than removed or renamed, since both have inbound anchors

  `construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md`.
- `people/dc.md` — "Owning 'Swash'" paragraph reduced to a short summary +
  link to `boats/swash.md`.
- `operations/secondhand-market.md` — added a one-line pointer to
  `boats/swash.md` at the top of the existing "An unfinished hull for sale"
  section; the section itself was left in place (topical home for the
  secondhand-market angle, and its heading has inbound anchors from
  `log.md`, `people/dc.md`, and `people/nels.md`).
- `design/weight-and-ballast.md`, `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md`,
  `construction/topside-painting.md`, `design/rig.md`,
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` — left as-is (topical
  content that also belongs on those pages per the task brief); no
  duplicated prose was removed from them, only reused/cited on the new
  boat pages.

**Index updates:** `boats/index.md` gained three one-line entries (Jill,
Enuf, Swash); `people/index.md` gained one entry (Derek Clark).

**Verification:** grepped `bundle/` to confirm all four new pages are
reachable from their respective indexes, that `jack-gardiner.md` and
`dc.md` both cross-link to their new boat pages, and that no heading
trimmed on a source page had an inbound anchor it would have broken.

Verified via grep across `bundle/` that no surviving page's body prose links
to any of the 4 deleted files; a handful of frozen `# Citations`-block
references into the deleted `off-topic-cross-posts.md` remain by design
(low-impact per the prune rules) in `matt-layden/biography.md`,
`people/dc.md`, `people/jeff-doryman.md`, `people/william-longyard.md`, and
`people/nels.md`. `log.md`'s own historical references to the deleted files
(this changelog) were left as-is, being a historical record rather than
reader-facing navigation.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 300 — RECOVERY of a partially-applied batch)

**This batch was a recovery.** A prior batch-300 agent died mid-run after
partially applying pages 2991-3000 (msgnum 3022-3031) and left the tree in an
inconsistent state: citations **[99]** and **[100]** had already been added
correctly to
[people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md)
(page_2991/msgnum 3022, page_2992/msgnum 3023), and citation **[113]** had
been added correctly to
[design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) (page_2993/msgnum
3024) — both verified against the source `.eml`s and found complete and
internally consistent, so neither was touched again. However,
[construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
was left **broken**: an inline `[75]` marker had been inserted mid-sentence
("...skid over obstructions more easily than a plain epoxy finish[75]") with
no matching `[75]` entry in the Citations block, which still ended at [74].

**Reconciliation.** The dangling `[75]` was traced to page_2995 (msgnum 3026,
Gene T./`tehansky`, "Re: Enuff Update"), replying to Derek Clark's paint-scheme
post to say he likes graphite mixed into the final epoxy coats on the
bottom — hard to gouge, and it skids over obstructions more easily, "so they
say." This is a genuine, well-grounded fact from an already-standing
below-threshold correspondent, so it was completed rather than stripped out:
a proper `[75]` Citations entry was added (matching the existing, unchanged
inline clause). `epoxy-coating-technique.md` is disposed as of page_2995 and
was not reprocessed.

0 files created, 1 file extended
([people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md)),
1 file recovery-completed
([construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)),
plus the already-complete dead-agent edit to
[design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) left untouched.

**The remaining six pages (2994, 2996, 2997, 2998, 2999, 3000)** continue the
same thread as pages 2991/2992/2995 — nominally "Jack gardiner's Tack Strut &
Boom Axle," but actually a short, self-contained story about Derek Clark's
"Enuf" build: his own group Photos folder for "Enuf" unexpectedly emptied,
with the pictures turning up instead on Don Elliott's rival group. Jack
Gardiner, confused, could still see four photos in the group's own folder and
offered to host a set on his personal website regardless; Derek, as much in
the dark as everyone else ("it may have been an act of God"), confirmed the
photos were back and more were coming once the hull's bottom was finished.
Derek then asked Jack for his website link, which Jack supplied
(`http://members.cox.net/alex29/`) — one of only a handful of individual
builder websites recorded in the archive alongside Alastair's and Bill
Serjeant's. Derek closed the thread with an unprompted thank-you to the list
as a whole — naming Nels, Chris Ostlind, Jack Gardiner, David Beard, and Dave
Bolduc among those whose knowledge he had drawn on as his own build neared
launch — plus a lighthearted aside about the range of surnames on the list
(French, Swedish, Norwegian, Anglo-Saxon), wondering in passing whether
"Bolduc" is East European. Glen closed out with a simple "Thanks Derek, she
looks great!" Folded into a new subsection, "The 'Enuf' photo-folder mystery,
and Jack Gardiner's own website," added directly after the existing "Don
Elliott ban, revisited" section in
[people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md)
(cites [101]-[106], 106 total, no gaps, no letter-suffixes invented).

No genuinely new correspondents this batch — all ten messages from four
already-standing identities: Glen Maxwell, Chris Ostlind, Jack Gardiner
(alex29/`tidybowlmann`, posting as "John Gardiner"), Derek Clark
(dclark/`dclark52001`), and Gene T. (`goldranger02-boats`/`tehansky`, a
standing below-threshold correspondent). Derek Clark authored the plurality
of messages (5 of 10) and is reconfirmed **STRENGTHENED but NOT promoted**
per standing instruction — no link to `people/dc.md`, no `boats/enuf.md`
created. David Beard, David LeBlanc, DC, John Hall, both other Pauls, and Jeff
UK did not appear; two-UK-Jeffs/three-Larrys/two-Roberts/two-Rons watches not
triggered. Jason Stancil identity-reconcile, Jack Gardiner "Jill" boat-page
split, Jeff UK vs doryman_uk, and Derek Clark consolidation candidacy remain
OPEN/deferred 3000-review items, untouched this batch.

Post-recovery integrity audit (count vs. max, duplicates, orphan markers) run
clean on all three touched/partially-touched files:
`people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` **106/106** (every inline
[85]-[106] marker matched 1:1 to a Citations entry, no duplicates);
`design/weight-and-ballast.md` **115 total entries, numeric max [113]**
(pre-existing [53]/[53a]/[53b] triad unaffected, [109]-[113] all verified
matched); `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` **76 total entries,
numeric max [75]** (pre-existing [29a] letter-suffix unaffected, the dangling
[75] marker fully resolved, no orphans remain). No new cross-file anchors
introduced; no headings changed, added, or reordered (frozen per
REGISTER-SHIFT-V2). msgnum run 3022-3023-3024-3025-3026-3027-3028-3029-
3030-3031 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive continuing directly from
batch 299's last msgnum 3021 (all ten source `.eml` byte sizes distinct on
disk, ruling out stale-read risk); standing gaps at 2122, 2190, 2229, 2385,
2611, 2614, and 2753 unaffected, not re-flagged.

Archive timeline reached **1 August 2005** (page_3000/msgnum 3031 dated
2005-08-01). Cursor set to **3000**. **This batch completes the first 3000
pages of the archive** (~30% of 10,065 total) — the next holistic tree
review is due; see `.okf-progress/backlog.md` for observations recorded for
that review (Derek Clark/"Enuf" content scatter across five files with no
single home page, a possible length/cohesion check on
`list-founder-and-moderator.md` now at 106 citations, and a citation

## 2026-07-05 (batch 299)

* **Update (batch page_2981-page_2990, msgnum 3012-3013-3014-3015-3016-3017-3018-3019-3020-3021,
  continuing directly from batch 298's last msgnum 3011; no new gap in this
  range)**: 29-31 July 2005. 0 files created, 4 files extended
  (design/weight-and-ballast.md, construction/topside-painting.md,
  construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md, design/rig.md), 2 files given
  narrate-once cross-link additions (people/nels.md, people/jack-gardiner.md).

  **The self-righting/capsize-safety disagreement (opened batch 298)
  reaches a fuller resolution.** Answering the detailed pushback that "nil"
  odds of staying inverted was an overstatement, a further firsthand
  account judged the real risk small in practice: a Paradox would spill
  wind and settle rather than staying pinned the way a multihull's mast
  jabs into the bottom when turtled, and rigging tangled on an obstruction
  is something to cut free rather than fear. Matt Layden's own boat has
  needed a crew member's help before — Matt himself was dislodged from the
  hull while helping her right herself after a knockdown, by grabbing the
  cabin-top rail — and the mast is built to slide out of its step under
  load in any case. Fitting the foam flotation as designed was reiterated
  as the real safeguard, so a capsized hull works with the crew toward
  self-righting rather than against them; a 72-hour overboard bag with a
  flare gun was offered as further personal insurance, alongside a
  reminder that groundings and mishaps (illustrated by a 30 ft powerboat
  once stuck a full tide cycle on a sandbar) are not unique to a small,
  shoal-draft boat. Folded into the existing "Self-righting range, and
  bailing without opening the hatch" section of
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#self-righting-range-and-bailing-without-opening-the-hatch)
  (cite [109], 114 total incl. the pre-existing [53]/[53a]/[53b] triad, no
  new gaps; the pre-existing heading was not touched). A narrate-once
  cross-link was added to
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#torrent-support-for-the-beaching-video-and-a-canadian-toast-interlude)
  (cite [181], 183 total, known pre-existing [53] gap unaffected).

  **A full worked ballast layout, and the practical load-then-trim method
  for sizing and placing it, are now on record.** One builder's complete
  centerline ballast plan — 11 kg lead pigs balancing a 12v battery at one
  bulkhead, two lidded lead compartments at another, anchors/chain and
  water stowage further aft, food bins, and the crew's own weight, totaling
  roughly 66 kg on the centerline before stores — prompted the archive's
  clearest practical answer to how much ballast a Paradox needs and where:
  launch with an empty hull, load all cruising gear, add lead until she
  balances to her designed waterline, then mount it permanently — most
  builders start light, which usefully shows the chine runners' real
  performance before the boat reaches her final trim. Folded into the
  existing "Ballast placement: outboard vs. centered" section of
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#ballast-placement-outboard-vs-centered)
  (cites [110]-[112], 114 total incl. the pre-existing [53]/[53a]/[53b]
  triad, no new gaps; the pre-existing heading was not touched). A
  narrate-once cross-link was added to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md#other-contributions) for the practical
  advice (cite [138], 138 total, no gaps), which also picks up his closing
  paint-scheme banter (cite [137]).

  **The "Enuff Update" paint-scheme thread closes out on a joke.** The
  finished scheme was summed up as "three-tone green with a yellow
  bottom," with the word "HELP!" in International Red floated,
  tongue-in-cheek, as a finishing touch — closing the thread carried over
  from batch 298. Folded into the existing "Choosing a hull colour, and
  why the epoxy's UV protection matters more than the shade" section of
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md#choosing-a-hull-colour-and-why-the-epoxys-uv-protection-matters-more-than-the-shade)
  (cite [49], 49 total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing heading
  was not touched).

  **The hull-turning narrative (opened batch 297) advances to bottom
  sheathing, with a real repair technique added to the record.** With the
  hull upside down, the bottom was smoothed and sheathed in the same
  biaxial cloth used on the sides, carried about an inch up the sides all
  round to protect the bottom edge and chine runners (the chine having
  been radiused beforehand to let the cloth wrap the corner cleanly). Small
  voids ("white spots") can appear where the sheathing fails to bond fully
  into the chine-runner/side angle — the fix is to **inject epoxy into the
  void rather than cut it out**, since cutting sacrifices the fiberglass
  cloth's own structural integrity at that spot. Folded into the existing
  "Turning the hull over" section of
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md#turning-the-hull-over)
  (cites [53]-[54], 54 total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing
  heading was not touched). A narrate-once cross-link was added to
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#torrent-support-for-the-beaching-video-and-a-canadian-toast-interlude)
  (cite [182], 183 total, known pre-existing [53] gap unaffected).

  **The tack-strut/boom-axle pivot-joint thread (running since batch 291)
  reaches its fullest fabrication detail yet.** A complete build write-up
  of a finished pivot joint was posted: a furling-rope sheave mounted on a
  hose-clamp-seized plate; a sleeve, made from the same pipe stock, that
  enlarges the axle's effective diameter and spins freely so the fitting
  finds its own angle without binding; interim hose-clamp stops with a
  planned upgrade to formal, drilled-and-tapped, set-screw-adjustable
  stops (brazing on a nut first if the pipe wall proves too thin to hold
  threads); a worn retaining bolt due for a locking nut and a larger
  replacement size; and a fiber-disk cutoff wheel used to cut the pipe's
  end notches (drilling and hacksawing, then sanding, noted as a workable
  alternative). Folded into the existing "The tack strut and boom axle's
  pivot joint: why a 'V,' and alternatives" section of
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#the-tack-strut-and-boom-axles-pivot-joint-why-a-v-and-alternatives)
  (cites [216]-[217], 219 total incl. the pre-existing [7a]/[7b] pair, no
  new gaps; the pre-existing heading was not touched). A narrate-once
  cross-link was added to
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#torrent-support-for-the-beaching-video-and-a-canadian-toast-interlude)
  (cite [183], 183 total, known pre-existing [53] gap unaffected).

  **Standing-watch checks.** No genuinely new correspondents this batch —
  all ten messages came from three already-standing identities: Nels
  (`arvent@...`/`recree8`), Jack Gardiner (`alex29@...`/`tidybowlmann`,
  posting under the "John Gardiner" display name, confirmed distinct from
  John Hall/`jdmhall`), and Derek Clark (`dclark@...`/`dclark52001`, boat
  "Enuf"), who authored the plurality of messages (five of ten). Derek
  Clark is reconfirmed STRENGTHENED — supplying his fullest single-batch
  ballast-layout detail yet plus the tack-strut photo upload and biaxial-
  sheathing/void report — but deliberately left NOT promoted per standing
  instruction, with no link to [people/dc.md](/people/dc.md); the
  `boats/enuf.md`/`people/derek-clark.md` consolidation candidacy is
  reiterated but untouched. David Beard, David LeBlanc, DC, John Hall, and
  both other Pauls (kayaker37, Paul V.) did not appear this batch. The
  two-distinct-UK-Jeffs, three-distinct-Larrys, and two-distinct-
  Roberts/Rons watches were not triggered (no traffic from any of those
  identities). Jason Stancil identity-reconcile, Jack Gardiner's "Jill"
  boat-page-split, the Jeff UK vs. doryman_uk identity item, and the Derek
  Clark consolidation candidacy all remain OPEN/deferred items, none
  touched this batch. `people/jeff-doryman.md` was NOT touched this batch,
  so the standing FIX-LATER date-range fix (from batch 287) remains
  deferred. The batch-296 correction (`alfswatzky` = standing Alf Sawatzky,
  not a new person) is unaffected — he did not appear this batch. No new
  archive gap: msgnum run 3012-3013-3014-3015-3016-3017-3018-3019-3020-3021
  confirmed distinct and fully consecutive across all ten pages via direct
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` header read, continuing directly from batch 298's last
  msgnum 3011. Standing gaps at 2122, 2190, 2229, 2385, 2611, 2614, and
  2753 unaffected, not re-flagged. All ten source pages matched the
  dispatch byte sizes exactly (1610/7588/3131/1919/2173/1500/2024/2117/
  4681/5285 bytes for page_2981 through page_2990 respectively) — no
  stale-read issues. Citation-collision check clean across all six
  touched/extended files (count equals max, zero new gaps or duplicates):
  design/weight-and-ballast.md 114/114 (pre-existing [53]/[53a]/[53b]
  triad unaffected), construction/topside-painting.md 49/49,
  construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md 54/54, design/rig.md 219/219

  No letter-suffixed citation numbers were invented; no new cross-file

## 2026-07-05 (batch 298)

* **Update (batch page_2971-page_2980, msgnum 3002-3003-3004-3005-3006-3007-3008-3009-3010-3011,
  continuing directly from batch 297's last msgnum 3001; no new gap in this
  range)**: 28-29 July 2005. 0 files created, 3 files extended
  (construction/topside-painting.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md,
  references/building-plans.md), 2 files given narrate-once cross-link
  additions (people/nels.md, people/jack-gardiner.md).

  **The "Enuff Update" paint-scheme thread (opened batch 297) runs a full
  further round, testing and refining the red-bottom/green-deck plan.** The
  red-for-visibility half of the plan was directly challenged: independent
  testing of colour visibility over distance has found bright yellow, not
  red, to be the most visible from far away, with red actually ranking among
  the worst — contrary to the common assumption that red would win, and the
  reason most safety devices are made yellow. The builder took the point,
  dropping distance-visibility as one of his reasons for red, though the
  separate scratch/score-visibility rationale for a red bottom stood on its
  own. A related idea — painting an international distress symbol on the
  hull's (sufficiently flat) bottom, for use if the boat were ever found
  capsized — was set aside in favour of carrying flares, judged more useful
  over a distance given the same yellow-over-red point. The pigment-coat
  mechanics were also spelled out in full: mixed into the final epoxy coat
  and applied with a wide spatula, dragged out to a smooth shiny or matt
  finish; a textured, less-painful-on-bare-feet deck surface can be produced
  the same way by sprinkling on sugar and washing it off with hot water a
  day later; the coat cures hard enough to walk on in about four hours; and
  since one batch may not stretch to cover the whole boat, the job is best
  split across two or three sessions, with any resulting shade mismatch
  between sessions judged unlikely to matter (or blamable on the light).
  Folded into the existing "Choosing a hull colour, and why the epoxy's UV
  protection matters more than the shade" section of
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md#choosing-a-hull-colour-and-why-the-epoxys-uv-protection-matters-more-than-the-shade)
  (cites [43]-[48], 48 total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing
  heading was not touched). A narrate-once cross-link was added to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md#other-contributions) for his
  pigment-application questions (cite [136], 136 total, count equals max, no
  gaps).

  **A parallel exchange on the same "Enuff Update" thread reopened the
  self-righting/capsize-safety question with a real disagreement.**
  Reassuring the builder that properly secured ballast makes turning turtle
  and staying that way effectively nil, one correspondent's confidence drew
  a detailed rebuttal: judging "nil" an overstatement, since plenty of
  real-world conditions can defeat any boat's self-righting design — a
  turtled multihull's mast can jab into the bottom and hold it inverted,
  recoverable only with a powerboat's help or a lucky wave; a Paradox's
  ability to sail in very thin water means strong wind and breaking waves
  over a soft, muddy bottom could produce the same "stick in the bottom"
  effect; and a knockdown with tangled halyards/sheets plus a leaking hatch
  and a filling hull is another route to the same result. Folded into the
  existing "Self-righting range, and bailing without opening the hatch"
  section of
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#self-righting-range-and-bailing-without-opening-the-hatch)
  (cites [106]-[108], 108 total, count equals max, no gaps except the
  pre-existing [53]/[53a]/[53b] triad, which is unaffected; the
  pre-existing heading was not touched). A narrate-once cross-link plus a
  short body mention was added to
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#torrent-support-for-the-beaching-video-and-a-canadian-toast-interlude)
  (cite [180], 180 total, count equals max, known pre-existing [53] gap
  unaffected).

  **A minor, unrelated item: a plan set changes hands.** A standing
  correspondent, having decided to buy a used boat rather than build,
  offered his own Paradox plan set (with its bonus CD) plus his copy of
  Don Elliott's build manual for sale as a package for $52 — folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#source-and-cost)
  as a brief new paragraph (cite [140], 140 total, count equals max, no
  gaps; the pre-existing heading was not touched).

  **Standing-watch checks.** One genuinely new below-threshold correspondent
  this batch: a first-time poster (`rube2112us`) who supplied the
  yellow-vs-red visibility data point, with no boat or build of his own on
  record. All other correspondents — Paul Reppeto (`ldjonerik`), Brent
  Geery (`brentgeery`), Nels (`recree8`), Chris Ostlind (`wedgesail`), and
  Jack Gardiner (`alex29`/`tidybowlmann`, posting under the "John Gardiner"
  display name) — were reconfirmed already-standing identities, none newly
  added. Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`) authored the plurality of
  this batch's messages (the paint-visibility reply, the distress-flare
  reply, the ballast-confidence reply, and the pigment-mechanics reply),
  confirmed via headers each time, reconfirmed STRENGTHENED but
  deliberately left NOT promoted per standing instruction, with no link to
  [people/dc.md](/people/dc.md); the `boats/enuf.md`/
  `people/derek-clark.md` consolidation candidacy is reiterated but
  untouched. The batch-296 correction (`alfswatzky` = standing Alf
  Sawatzky, not new) is unaffected — he did not appear this batch. David
  Beard, David LeBlanc, DC, and John Hall did not appear. Two-distinct-UK-
  Jeffs, three-distinct-Larrys, and two-distinct-Roberts/Rons watches were
  not triggered. Jason Stancil identity-reconcile, Jack Gardiner's "Jill"
  boat-page-split, the Jeff UK vs. doryman_uk identity item, and the Derek
  Clark consolidation candidacy all remain OPEN/deferred items, none
  touched this batch; `people/jeff-doryman.md` was not touched, so the
  standing FIX-LATER date-range fix (from batch 287) remains deferred. No
  new archive gap: msgnum run 3002-3011 confirmed distinct and fully
  consecutive across all ten pages via direct `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` header read,
  continuing directly from batch 297's last msgnum 3001; standing gaps at
  2122, 2190, 2229, 2385, 2611, 2614, and 2753 unaffected, not re-flagged.
  All ten source pages matched the dispatch byte sizes exactly (no
  stale-read issues). Citation-collision check clean across all five
  touched/extended files via a sorted-dedup pass (count equals max in each,
  zero new gaps or duplicates): topside-painting.md 48/48,
  weight-and-ballast.md 108/108 (pre-existing [53]/[53a]/[53b] triad
  unaffected), building-plans.md 140/140, jack-gardiner.md 180/180
  (pre-existing [53] gap unaffected), nels.md 136/136. No letter-suffixed

## 2026-07-05 (batch 297)

* **Update (batch page_2961-page_2970, msgnum 2992-2993-2994-2995-2996-2997-2998-2999-3000-3001,
  continuing directly from batch 296's last msgnum 2991; no new gap in this
  range)**: 24-28 July 2005. 0 files created, 4 files extended
  (people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md,
  references/paradox-uk-website.md, construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md,
  construction/topside-painting.md).

  **The "Don Elliott ban, revisited" thread closes with a run of ban-story
  banter.** The member who had recounted Don Elliott's Wisconsin boat-show
  invitation drew a laugh for dubbing him "The Donald, jr." The below-threshold
  correspondent who had earlier joked about earning his own ban proposed,
  half-seriously, starting a dedicated group for everyone Don has banned; the
  "select group" member's deadpan reply was "There you go," and the
  co-moderator confirmed the joke was not entirely a joke — "you are number
  one on our list." Told he had apparently already earned a spot, the same
  member replied "My God, what have I done? My wife says that whatever it is
  I probably deserve it," closed out by the co-moderator judging his wife "a
  very wise lady." Folded into the existing "The Don Elliott ban, revisited
  nearly two years on" section of
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#the-don-elliott-ban-revisited-nearly-two-years-on)
  (cites [93]-[98], 98 total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing
  heading was not touched).

  **The UK Jordan Boats kit venture, tracked since January 2005, concludes in
  failure.** Despite working hard to make every kit part fit, the design and
  cutting process for the panels and components needed more research than
  could be resolved in time; the kit-Paradox builder learned he would not
  receive a kit after all, and the supplier — who had not abandoned the idea
  of eventually offering a production-quality kit — instead supplied the
  plywood that would have gone into it. The builder chose to start from
  Matt Layden's original plans rather than wait further, thanking both the
  supplier and the designer for their collaborative effort; a fellow
  correspondent (confirmed via headers as the same standing Rhode Island
  builder posting under a "John Gardiner" display name, distinct from John
  Hall) wished him well. Folded into
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-kit-built-option-jordan-boats)
  (cites [41]-[42], 42 total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing
  heading was not touched).

  **A standing below-threshold builder's "Enuf" progress reports add two
  concrete techniques.** Wet-laying fiberglass sheathing on each side of a
  hull, prepared over several preceding days, took about two hours per side
  for one builder working alone — folded into
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md#turning-the-hull-over)
  alongside a full account of turning a hull solo from a garage onto a
  driveway without a crane: sliding it out on round fence-pole rollers,
  flipping it onto its side on long timbers and scrap plywood, flipping again
  onto its deck, and rolling it back in — done with four strong helpers,
  though the builder judged three could have managed it (cites [51]-[52], 52
  total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing heading was not
  touched). The same update gave a paint-scheme rationale — a bright red
  bottom so scratches and scores show through and a downed boat is easy for a
  rescue helicopter to spot, paired with a green pigment mixed into the final
  finish coat for the sides and deck, a supplier's suggestion — folded into
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md#choosing-a-hull-colour-and-why-the-epoxys-uv-protection-matters-more-than-the-shade)
  (cite [42], 42 total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing heading
  was not touched), cross-linked both ways with the hull-turning account
  above since both facts come from the same source message.

  **Standing-watch checks.** No genuinely new correspondents this batch — all
  ten messages came from already-standing identities. The batch's Alf

  per the batch-296 correction, this is reconfirmed as the same standing
  person (`alfswatzky`), not treated as new. Derek Clark (`dclark@`/
  `dclark52001`) authored five of the ten messages, his fullest single-batch

  `people/derek-clark.md` consolidation candidacy is reiterated but
  untouched. Jack Gardiner (`alex29@...`/`tidybowlmann`) authored one message
  under the "John Gardiner" display name, confirmed via headers as the same
  standing Rhode Island correspondent, correctly distinct from John Hall.
  David Beard, David LeBlanc, DC, John Hall, both Pauls, Paul Reppeto, and
  Jeff UK did not appear this batch. Two-distinct-UK-Jeffs,
  three-distinct-Larrys, and two-distinct-Roberts/Rons watches were not

## 2026-07-05 (batch 296)

* **Update (batch page_2951-page_2960, msgnum 2982-2983-2984-2985-2986-2987-2988-2989-2990-2991,
  continuing directly from batch 295's last msgnum 2981; no new gap in this
  range)**: 23-24 July 2005. 0 files created, 3 files extended
  (people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md,
  construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md, construction/build-effort.md),
  2 files lightly cross-linked with no new citation (people/don-elliott.md,
  people/nels.md).

  **The "Don Elliott ban, revisited" thread (opened batch 295) runs a full
  further round and widens into a small chorus of ban stories.** Glen Maxwell
  answered Derek Clark's free-speech challenge directly: the 2003 ban was
  never about free speech, but about Don's personal attacks on members, which
  had drawn enough off-list complaints (including threats to leave) that
  action was necessary; Don remains reachable elsewhere for anyone still
  wanting his counsel. Nels then drew a sharper line between Don and "Nutty
  Boats": in his view Don banned himself through direct personal attacks and
  flaming, a pattern he distinguished from the more even-tempered "T. Lee" of
  Nutty Boats (opinionated but not abusive) — and disclosed being banned from
  one of Don's own groups without ever having posted there, over an unrelated
  post Don had taken offense to elsewhere. Derek Clark accepted the
  clarification gracefully, conceded he'd arrived at "the tail end" of the
  original affair, and joked about earning his own Don Elliott ban as a badge
  of honor once his boat, "Enuf," is finished. A second, parallel sub-thread
  ("Welcome to the Club, Brother Nels!") gathered two more firsthand
  no-post-required ban stories: one member banned for what he intended as a
  positive design comment, and — the batch's fullest single account —
  **Chris Ostlind**'s own history with Don: personally invited to join a Don
  Elliott group after showing a sailing sea kayak at a Wisconsin boat show,
  an invitation that "never got that far," followed by Chris later banning
  himself from Don's subsequent "SmallBoats" group over the moderator's own
  conduct. Chris characterized Don as a talented illustrator but poorly
  socially adjusted and closed to any idea but his own, and stated plainly
  that Paradox is easy enough to build from Matt Layden's original plans
  without needing Don's manual at all. All folded into the existing "Don
  Elliott ban, revisited nearly two years on" section of
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#the-don-elliott-ban-revisited-nearly-two-years-on)
  (cites [87]-[92], 92 total, count equals max, no gaps; the pre-existing
  heading was not touched). Cross-link-only additions (no new citations) were
  made to [people/don-elliott.md](/people/don-elliott.md#a-rival-group-and-a-dispute-over-who-founded-and-moderates-the-list)
  (42/42 unchanged) and [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md#other-contributions)
  (135/135 unchanged), pointing to the revisited-ban section rather than
  restating it.

  **A second, unrelated exchange settled a standing open question about
  brand-name epoxy.** Answering a member's question about who/what "FGCI"
  (an epoxy source named the previous batch) actually is, Glen Maxwell
  identified Fiber Glass Coatings Inc. of St. Petersburg, FL, and reported
  using their 5:1 mix for about 12 years, finding it nearly indistinguishable
  from WEST System once mixed beyond a somewhat thicker hardener — a direct,
  firsthand answer to the "does a name-brand epoxy perform any differently
  from a cheaper equivalent" question left explicitly open in
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#brand-name-epoxy-versus-cheaper-equivalents-and-masonry-sealant).
  Extended that section (cite [74], 74 total, count equals max, no gaps; the
  pre-existing heading was not touched).

  **A third, minor item extended the standing Derek Clark/"Enuf" build-effort
  record.** Preparing one side of the hull for glassing while it sat roughly
  10 in off the floor on baulks of timber, Derek Clark found a simpler
  alternative to cutting long wedges to correct a side sloping inward: nail a
  pair of short stops to one end of the baulks to stop the boat sliding away,
  then lift the opposite side on a trolley jack. Folded into
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#workspace)'s
  existing "Workspace" section, alongside the same builder's earlier
  trolley/wheeled-cradle moving methods (cite [85], 85 total, count equals
  max, no gaps).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 295)

* **Update (batch page_2941-page_2950, msgnum 2972-2973-2974-2975-2976-2977-2978-2979-2980-2981,
  continuing directly from batch 294's last msgnum 2971; no new gap in this
  range)**: 23 July 2005. 0 files created, 2 extended
  (references/building-plans.md,
  people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md), 1 file lightly
  cross-linked with no new citation (people/nels.md).

  **The "please help!!!" plans-request saga (running since batch 293) reaches
  full resolution.** Wayne, "In the Swamp," gave the correspondent concrete
  plywood suppliers — M.L. Condon of White Plains, NY (judged, on balance,
  the best plywood available in North America, budget allowing), Harbor
  Sales of Maryland, and, for New Jersey specifically, either M.L. Condon or
  South Jersey Lumberman's — plus Douglas fir for structural lumber and FGCI
  as the best epoxy price found, $35/gal. "Jeff UK" reacted to the epoxy
  price (UK buyers pay roughly double; MAS is the cheapest he has found on
  that side of the Atlantic) and asked directly whether the correspondent had
  looked at an actual set of plans yet. The correspondent thanked both,
  confirmed South Jersey Lumberman's was the likelier local source, and
  reported his email trouble persisted but he now had a phone number and
  postal address in hand. Nels then offered a faster alternative route into
  the design while the plans order remained stalled:
  [Don Elliott](/people/don-elliott.md)'s own build manual, sold on CD for
  about $20 through Don's rival Yahoo group, containing a full
  materials/tools list, step-by-step illustrated instructions down to
  puttying and glassing the hull, a reduced-scale set of plans, and complete
  plans for a mini folding dinghy as a practice project. A below-threshold
  correspondent, "Andy," confirmed Don delivers the manual by e-mail as a
  PDF for near-instant access, and separately made a one-off dry joke about
  the correspondent's self-described New Jersey impatience. Wayne closed with
  a full supplier contact list (addresses, phone/fax numbers, and URLs for
  M.L. Condon, South Jersey Lumberman's, Harbor Sales, FGCI, and Hamilton
  Marine). The thread's actual resolution came from the correspondent
  himself: Dave Bolduc had, after all, replied to one of his e-mails with
  pricing, and he reported he was off to mail payment that day. Extended
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#source-and-cost)
  (cites [132]-[139], 139 total, count equals max, no gaps), with a
  no-new-citation cross-link addition to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md#other-contributions) (135/135 unchanged)
  for his manual-alternative suggestion.

  **A new, distinct thread revisits the November 2003 Don Elliott ban nearly
  two years later.** Derek Clark questioned whether the ban sat well with
  free-speech values and floated inviting Don back, having found Don's advice
  helpful once its reasoning was explained to him — while noting he
  personally found a site called "Nutty Boats" (carrying Don's posts) hard to
  stomach, though some who reply to Don there are worth reading for their
  turn of phrase. Brent Geery answered that whatever one thinks of banning
  people, the ban had done little in practice to curb Don's ability to voice
  his opinion, since Don has his own group, `paradoxboatbuilders`, to post to
  regardless. Folded as a new thematic section, "The Don Elliott ban,
  revisited nearly two years on," appended to the end of
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#the-don-elliott-ban-revisited-nearly-two-years-on)
  (cites [85]-[86], 86 total, count equals max, no gaps). The source text is
  ambiguous on whether "Nutty Boats" is literally Don Elliott's own site or
  merely a site where his posts circulate; both body and citation were kept
  deliberately hedged rather than asserting outright ownership, per the
  "do not invent facts" rule — this is the archive's first mention of "Nutty
  Boats" anywhere in the tree.

  **Standing-watch checks.** Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`, boat

## 2026-07-05 (batch 294)

* **Update (batch page_2931-page_2940, msgnum 2962-2963-2964-2965-2966-2967-2968-2969-2970-2971,
  continuing directly from batch 293's last msgnum 2961; no new gap in this
  range)**: 21-23 July 2005. 0 files created, 4 extended
  (references/building-plans.md, design/rig.md, people/alastair.md,
  people/jack-gardiner.md), 1 file lightly cross-linked with no new citation
  (construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md).

  **The "please help!!!" plans-request saga (opened batch 293) runs to its
  full conclusion — bounced e-mail, a photocopy workaround, tough love, and
  finally a reframe from plans to materials.** The newcomer who had already
  been pointed to Dave Bolduc and Matt Layden's e-mail addresses found his
  own follow-up to the Bolducs bounced as an invalid address, leaving him
  unsure the plans were still obtainable at all. A UK correspondent signing
  "Jeff UK" proposed an interim workaround — a builder who already holds a
  set of plans photocopying them and forwarding the copy, with the
  newcomer reimbursing the copying and postage cost on top of the usual fee
  owed to the Bolducs — and re-gave the Micro Cruising Yahoo group and
  `microcruising.com` links, noting Dave's own last post there was already a
  couple of weeks old. The newcomer accepted readily, offering to pay
  outright for a full plans set and construction manual from anyone able to
  supply them. A UK builder's reply was characteristically blunt: this level
  of impatience this early might mean Paradox — "more of a relaxed sail" —
  isn't the right boat, before pointing once more to `microcruising.com` as
  the actual source. The newcomer apologized for his tone, noting the e-mail
  address listed on that site itself wasn't working either, and drew a short
  word of encouragement from a Rhode Island builder: getting past the plans
  hurdle, the boat is worth the effort. The thread's real turning point came
  from an already-standing correspondent signing "Wayne, In the Swamp," who
  reframed the newcomer's urgency directly: boatbuilding in wood is "a labor
  of love" finished "when it gets done and not one minute sooner," and the
  more useful question at this early stage isn't plans but **materials** —
  what plywood the build calls for and where to source suitable stock, since
  ordinary hardware-store CDX plywood won't do and marine- or premium-grade
  MDO/HDO plywood, lumber, epoxy, fasteners, and sail cloth often have to be
  shipped in; "buying plans is easy, sourcing materials can be a challenge."
  The newcomer, based in New Jersey, thanked him and admitted he had no idea
  yet where to find suitable wood, but took the reframing in stride as
  something concrete to start on regardless of how the plans question
  resolved. Extended
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#source-and-cost)
  (cites [124]-[131], 131 total, count equals max, no gaps), with a light,
  no-new-citation cross-link added from
  [construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md#sourcing-fir-marine-plywood-and-the-true-weightcost-tradeoff-against-okoume)'s
  long-open "specific A/B fir plywood source... remains unsourced on record"
  note (count unchanged, 53/53, no gaps — Wayne's advice is general sourcing
  strategy, not a specific supplier, so the open citation itself was left
  untouched). Narrate-once-cross-link additions to
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#design-philosophy-and-community-role)
  (cite [260], 260 total, count equals max, no gaps) and
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#citations) (cite [179],
  178/179, known pre-existing `[53]` gap unaffected).

  **The "Mainsheet and outboard" canal-touring thread (closed out in batch
  293) draws one further, genuinely final reply.** A UK builder's firsthand
  yuloh verdict: excellent as a power source once the wind drops, but hard
  going in any direction but downwind against a real breeze, since the
  yuloh's thrust acts too far aft to easily stop the bow blowing off — fine
  for manoeuvring in a marina, but not a method that could sustain
  long-distance windward progress. Extended
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#mainsheet-fouling-on-the-rudder-and-rear-deck-with-an-outboard-fitted)
  (cite [215], 217 total including the pre-existing `[7a]`/`[7b]` letter-suffix
  pair, count equals max, no new gaps); narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#design-philosophy-and-community-role)
  (cite [259], included in the 260 total above).

  **Standing-watch checks.** Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`, boat
  "Enuf") did not appear this batch at all — no traffic from his address —
  so the standing STRENGTHENED-not-promoted flag and the `boats/enuf.md`
  consolidation candidacy are reiterated but untouched, per explicit
  instruction not to promote him regardless. Alastair (`openboat@`/
  `alopenboat`) authored two of this batch's ten messages (page_2931,
  msgnum 2962, the yuloh verdict; page_2936, msgnum 2967, the tough-love
  reply), confirmed via headers both times, correctly routed to
  design/rig.md and references/building-plans.md respectively with
  narrate-once-cross-link additions to his own person page. "Jeff UK"
  (`apcf14@`/`roverticket`) authored one message (page_2934, msgnum 2965),
  confirmed via headers, correctly kept a separate, unconfirmed identity
  from both the established UK Jeff (`doryman_uk`) and this batch's
  below-threshold "Jeff"/`jeff_hammell` — three separate identities, none
  conflated; `people/jeff-doryman.md` was not touched this batch, so the

  `jeff_hammell` (`stinkyshorts@`) authored five of this batch's ten

## 2026-07-05 (batch 293)

* **Update (batch page_2921-page_2930, msgnum 2952-2953-2954-2955-2956-2957-2958-2959-2960-2961,
  continuing directly from batch 292's last msgnum 2951; no new gap in this
  range)**: 18-21 July 2005. 0 files created, 3 extended
  (design/rig.md, people/nels.md, references/building-plans.md).

  **The revived "Mainsheet and outboard" canal-touring thread (opened batch
  292) closes out: a portage cart, the historical hazards of towpath-hauling,
  and how to reach the European canals at all.** Continuing straight on from
  batch 292's towpath-hauling/canoe-tracking exchange, a portage cart capable
  of supporting 200+ lb was offered as a further practical aid for combining
  paddling and portaging around unopenable locks, illustrated by British
  Columbia's Bowron Lakes canoe circuit, whose portage trails are deliberately
  wood-chip-surfaced in low spots to allow cart use. On the towpath-hauling
  method itself, willow regrowth is a worse obstruction on rivers governed by
  ancient navigation rights, where paths go unmaintained; finding hauling
  teams today is equally impractical, since the historical rope-hauler gangs
  were themselves notorious for drunkenness and brawling, prompting repeated
  but unsuccessful 18th- and 19th-century attempts to disband them — a
  dry-humored aside noted modern towpaths are now disrupted by dirt bikes,
  quads, snowmobiles, and jet skis instead. An air-cooled backup outboard was
  flagged as reportedly noisy, leaving a plain walk along the towpath,
  lodging at inns overnight, as simply the easier course for some. The thread
  closed by widening the question to reaching the continental canal system
  at all: the **English Channel** was judged straightforwardly sailable in
  its own right, "in craft as small as Mirrors and even the odd bathtub,"
  without needing to tow or ferry a Paradox across. Extended
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#mainsheet-fouling-on-the-rudder-and-rear-deck-with-an-outboard-fitted)
  (cites [212]-[214], 216 total, count equals max, no new gaps — pre-existing
  `[7a]`/`[7b]` letter-suffix pair unaffected); narrate-once-cross-link
  addition to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md#other-contributions) (cite [135], 135
  total, count equals max, no gaps).

  **A UK builder's rebuilt tack strut is confirmed sound after a full
  six-day cruise, closing the strut-failure thread first opened in batch
  289.** His rebuilt strut — reported "holding up, fingers crossed" after a
  weekend's sailing at the end of batch 292's predecessor thread — went on to
  withstand a full six-day cruise including force-5 jibes and sailing heavily
  reefed, confirming the original failure had been a matter of an
  under-built strut ("should have followed the plans") rather than any flaw
  in the plans' own design. Extended
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#the-tack-strut-also-carries-compression-not-just-tension)
  (cite [211], 216 total, count equals max, no new gaps).

  **A new correspondent's plans-request struggle surfaces the standard
  answer once more, and prompts an explicit privacy appeal now that Matt
  Layden's own e-mail address has begun circulating.** A newcomer who had
  found and fallen for the Paradox online, and already written off for
  plans, hit the FAQ's usual failure mode when both addresses he tried
  bounced, worrying aloud that the plans might no longer be available or
  that the designer was simply away sailing. The reply supplied both
  standard contacts — Dave Bolduc (plans, "may be off sailing at this time")
  and, for the first time with the address given out plainly in a reply, an
  e-mail address for Matt Layden himself — prompting the list's founder to
  ask the group explicitly to respect Matt's privacy now that his address
  had been made public: Matt does not want to personally field every
  question, especially ones already answered in the group's own archive,
  which is precisely why plans requests are routed elsewhere; he does still
  read the list and occasionally posts himself, as when he published his own
  new sail-plan article, and members were asked to leave it to him to come
  forward on his own terms. The same reply pointed the newcomer to Don
  Elliott's paid construction booklet, with a passing acknowledgment of Don's
  history of dominating the group with unsolicited advice — cross-linked
  (not restated) to the existing full account of Don's ban and rival group,
  per narrate-once-cross-link. Extended
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#source-and-cost)
  (cites [120]-[123], 123 total, count equals max, no gaps), with a

## 2026-07-05 (batch 292)

* **Update (batch page_2911-page_2920, msgnum 2942-2943-2944-2945-2946-2947-2948-2949-2950-2951,
  continuing directly from batch 291's last msgnum 2941; no new gap in this
  range)**: 17-18 July 2005. 0 files created, 3 extended
  (design/rig.md, people/nels.md, people/jack-gardiner.md).

  **The "Tack Strut and Boom Axle" pivot-joint thread (opened in batch 291)
  concludes: sourcing brass tube fails, and epoxy-coating tested pipe wins
  out over every galvanising alternative.** Continuing straight on from
  batch 291's run of proposed rust fixes for a galvanised-steel pivot-joint
  substitute, a UK builder explained local galvanisers' flat minimum charge
  reflects their unwillingness to handle one-off small jobs — cheap,
  disposable scrap-yard galvanised pipe remained the practical fallback —
  while a search for brass tube itself at non-ferrous scrap yards turned up
  only short lengths of thin-wall stock, apparently because factories
  salvage non-ferrous scrap more carefully than ferrous. That left epoxy
  coating as the only remaining practical route to a strong, low-cost tube:
  refined to sanding the pipe before coating and painting over the cured
  epoxy for UV protection, then tested and reported to have "worked fine" —
  "so much simpler" than sourcing or galvanising metal pipe at all. The
  thread closed with the two builders arranging to post reference
  photographs to the group directly (after an earlier attachment mishap)
  and a promise to track down a part number for the tack-line fitting.
  Extended [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#the-tack-strut-and-boom-axles-pivot-joint-why-a-v-and-alternatives)
  (cites [201]-[202] and [205]-[206] and [208]-[209], 210 total, count
  equals max, no gaps); cross-linked (not restated) from
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#torrent-support-for-the-beaching-video-and-a-canadian-toast-interlude)
  per narrate-once-cross-link, since the full exchange and its citations
  live on the rig page.

  **A revived "Mainsheet and outboard" thread (last active May 2005) takes
  up motor-free canal travel: towpath-hauling technique, its river history,
  and a Canadian canoe-country analogy.** A new UK correspondent, wanting to
  reach the sea via the UK canal and river system without a motor, asked
  whether a yuloh could be used all day given frequent lock stops, and
  separately about the historic method of hauling a boat along a towpath.
  The towpath-hauling technique itself: rig a bridle to the bow and stern
  cleats with the towline fixed about two-thirds back from the bow, set the
  rudder to turn the bow out from the bank, and walk at 1.5-2 mph, adjusting
  the bridle and rudder to find the best towing angle. The method has real
  history on the River Severn, where cargo "trows" were hauled upstream from
  Gloucester to Bridgnorth by teams of men on the towpath (the tow rope
  sometimes run to the masthead), and on canals, where horses towed from
  just aft of the bow before motors displaced both. A yuloh was judged too
  slow to cover real distance on a canal over as much as a week (roughly 2
  knots, comparable to a sampan's yuloh speed), though it might serve mainly
  as a steering aid going downstream on a river, or for passing a fishing
  party without disturbing them. The nearest analogy offered was Canadian
  canoe "tracking" (upstream) and "lining" (downstream): a line at each end
  of the boat, fine-tuned to steer around obstructions despite no rudder,
  complicated in practice mainly by streamside willow growth, mud, and
  steep banks — sometimes requiring two haulers with separated lines — with
  a small backup outboard (the Honda 2 hp four-stroke, named specifically),
  walking the towpath and lodging at inns, or a canoe-with-portage-cart
  combination all offered as simpler alternatives, and Ontario's Rideau
  Canal cited as a well-supported example of the mixed paddling-and-
  portaging approach. Extended
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#mainsheet-fouling-on-the-rudder-and-rear-deck-with-an-outboard-fitted)
  (cites [203]-[204], [207], and [210], 210 total, count equals max, no
  gaps — same citation range as the tack-strut extension above, the two
  threads' messages interleaving chronologically over 17-18 July 2005);
  narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md#other-contributions) (cite [134], 134
  total, count equals max, no gaps).

  **Standing-watch checks.** Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`, boat
  "Enuf") authored four of this batch's ten messages (page_2911/2915/2917/2918,
  msgnum 2942/2946/2948/2949), continuing as the tack-strut thread's main
  asker and also opening the revived mainsheet/canal sub-thread with his own
  River Severn/yuloh-speed reply; confirmed via headers each time, correctly
  not conflated with DC (`kd7ixk@`/`betelgeuserdude`), who did not appear
  this batch, and correctly given no link to people/dc.md. Per the
  well-established 15+-batch STRENGTHENED-not-promoted precedent, he was
  again NOT promoted to a dedicated person/boat page this batch; his content

  operations/cabin-heating.md, people/jack-gardiner.md). The standing

  (page_2912/2916/2919, msgnum 2943/2947/2950), confirmed via headers,
  correctly kept distinct from John Hall (`jdmhall`, Tasmania), who did not

  batch (no conflation risk realized). "Jeff UK" (`apcf14@`/`roverticket`)
