## 2026-07-05 (batch 273)

* **Update (batch page_2721–page_2730, msgnum 2751-2752-2754-2755-2756-2757-2758-2759-2760-2761,
  continuing directly from batch 272's 2750; msgnum 2753 is a newly
  identified gap, not previously flagged)**: 3-6 June 2005. No new files,
  6 extended (references/beachcruising-book.md,
  construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md, design/rig.md,
  operations/crew-size-and-fit.md — plus narrate-once-cross-link touches to
  people/nels.md, people/david-beard.md, and
  people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md — 7 files touched in total).

  **(1) Reading-list thread continued.** **Nels** followed up his own
  "Books" recommendations with a pointer to the Pardeys' own website,
  landlpardey.com, for further living-aboard tips. Folded into
  [references/beachcruising-book.md](/references/beachcruising-book.md)
  ("A wider Paradox-sailor reading list", cite [7], 7 total).

  **(2) "Finish" thread closed out.** Continuing directly from batch 272's
  weave-filling discussion, **Derek Clark** agreed filling the weave and
  finishing properly makes sense, and reported a viral infection had set
  his decking back a further week, doubting "Enuf" would reach the water
  this year at all. **Nels** answered with practical finishing guidance:
  a warning about **amine blush** (a water-soluble epoxy cure byproduct in
  moist air, removable with water/vinegar or diluted ammonia) and a
  time-pressed builder's sequence — fill the weave, apply two thinned
  (~10%) paint coats, then go sailing, finishing fully (bright work
  included) the following season if motivated, sanding the sheen off
  between coats with 80-100 grit before moving to finer grits or wet
  sanding much later. Derek closed the thread reporting himself recovered,
  his hull near complete (decks, chine-runner trimming, full glassing, the
  cabin top, and the mast/spars still outstanding), his plan to trust
  Paradox's get-under-cover "Stock" cruising style rather than pack
  traditional foul-weather gear, and a borrowed folding bicycle stowed
  forward for shore trips. Folded into
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
  (extension to "Filling the fiberglass weave before painting...", cites
  [71]-[73], 73 total). Narrate-once-cross-link bullets extended on
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [129]-[130], 130 total).

  **(3) "Heart Pine & Epoxy": a new mast-wood data point.** **David Beard**,
  finishing his second Paradox and starting the mast, asked whether epoxy
  would bond well to old, rock-hard heart pine salvaged from a 1920s house
  remodel. **Alf Sawatzky** confirmed the wood was sound and usable, if the
  builder was prepared to dull blades cutting it; David accepted the
  trade-off. **Larry** (`lorenzojose2000`) gave the fuller technical answer:
  old-growth heart pine's tight grain and lack of knots likely make it
  stronger than pine cut today, with the one real risk being wood that
  overdried in a hot attic rather than a remodeled house — a failure mode
  also seen in old-growth Douglas fir, causing splits when fasteners are
  driven without a pre-drilled pilot hole; recommended testing a piece
  first, with no issue expected using epoxy on it. Folded into
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md) ("Wood species for a scratch-built wooden
  mast", new paragraph, cites [175]-[178], 178 total). Narrate-once-cross-link
  bullet added to [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cites
  [240]-[241], 241 total).

  **(4) "Sleep two in a Paradox?" answered with hard dimensions.** A
  prospective builder (**Ray**, `rhaldridge`/`knobmaker`) asked how skinny
  two people would need to be to share a Paradox cabin overnight, and how
  large the air-mattress space actually is. **Glen Maxwell** answered with
  exact figures — the cabin's clear sleeping space is 930 mm (37.2 in) wide
  by 1900 mm (76 in) long, workable for two 170 lb people "if you love each
  other" — but framed the harder problem as day-to-day cohabitation rather
  than fit: with two aboard, simple tasks like cooking in the rain leave
  no room to get out of each other's way. He and his wife's practice-tested
  answer is to pitch a tent ashore as a base camp and return to the boat,
  rather than try to live in the cabin continuously; solo, Glen has spent
  up to five days aboard without going ashore at all. Folded into
  [operations/crew-size-and-fit.md](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md)
  (extension to "Sleeping two aboard a Paradox-family cabin", cites
  [88]-[89], 89 total — file retains a pre-existing, unrelated numbering
  gap between [53] and [88] predating this batch). Narrate-once-cross-link
  bullet added to
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (cites [236]-[237], 237 total).

  No new person or boat pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Standing-open-items check: Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf", STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate)
  appeared as author twice this batch (page_2722/msgnum 2752, page_2724/
  msgnum 2755) — still NOT promoted per standing instruction, content

  Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill"

  below-threshold identity worth noting: **Alf Sawatzky** (`alfswatzky`,

## 2026-07-05 (batch 272)

* **Update (batch page_2711–page_2720, msgnum 2741-2742-2743-2744-2745-2746-2747-2748-2749-2750,
  continuing directly from batch 271's 2740, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  1-2 June 2005. No new files, 2 extended
  (references/beachcruising-book.md, construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md —
  plus a narrate-once-cross-link touch to people/nels.md — 3 files touched in
  total).

  **(1) "Books" thread closed out.** Continuing directly from batch 271's
  reading-list section, **Derek Clark** replied that "Tilikum" (38 ft) was
  larger and less instructional than he had hoped, naming Shane Acton's
  "Shrimpy" books as closer to what he wanted. **Nels** answered with the
  book's fuller context — Tilikum was a hollow cedar-log Haida Indian dugout
  canoe later decked over and sailed around the world by John Voss, whose
  other boat was only 19 ft; its appendix's storm tactics (trailing warps,
  riding to a sea anchor, staying quartering to the seas, and the older
  practice of streaming oil to blunt breaking seas) still hold value —
  and recommended the Pardeys, Annie Hill's "Voyaging on a Small Income,"
  and Charles Stock's skill at finding storm-sheltering "hidey holes" as
  proof a small boat can be safer than a large one coastal cruising. Folded
  into [references/beachcruising-book.md](/references/beachcruising-book.md)
  ("A wider Paradox-sailor reading list", cite [6], 6 total).

  **(2) "Finish": whether filling the fiberglass weave matters beyond
  looks.** **Derek Clark**, finishing "Enuf" for a hard working life rather
  than a showroom look, asked what fairing compound really does and whether
  leaving the weave unfilled costs anything besides cosmetics. **Nels**
  gave the paint-sequence answer (thinned coats sanded between, fairing
  applied only afterward, bottom paint recommended regardless of water
  type); on Derek's narrower follow-up, **Chris Ostlind** and Nels
  converged on **yes** — an unfilled weave adds real hull drag and should be
  filled with clear or microballoon-thickened epoxy even where the hull
  stays unpainted below the waterline, while an open weave separately
  absorbs moisture/dirt/oil, holds paint worse, and leaves bare epoxy
  without its needed UV protection. The thread closed on Nels's broader
  point: water that gains any way in is very hard to keep out afterward, so
  finishing correctly once at the outset — glass and epoxy being
  fundamentally just a matrix keeping water out of the wood — pays back in
  far less annual upkeep than patching a poorly finished hull later. Folded
  into [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
  as a new section ("Filling the fiberglass weave before painting: fairing
  compound, and the case against leaving it exposed", cites [64]-[70], 70
  total). Narrate-once-cross-link bullets added to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [127]-[128], 128 total).

  No new person or boat pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Standing-open-items check: Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf", STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate)
  appeared as author five times this batch — still NOT promoted per
  standing instruction, content genuinely topical rather than build
  narrative. Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill"
  boat-page-split remain OPEN 3000-review items, neither surfaced this
  batch. No genuinely new below-threshold correspondents (Derek Clark,
  Nels, and Chris Ostlind are all already-standing identities).
  Attribution-trap check clean: David Beard, David LeBlanc, DC, the two
  UK Jeffs, three Larrys, two Roberts, and two Rons did not appear this
  batch; Derek Clark confirmed via headers each time, not conflated with
  DC; Glen Maxwell did not appear at all. Byte sizes cross-checked via
  `wc -c` against dispatch instructions exactly (2024/2080/3462/2949/3031/
  4365/2207/2393/2116/2575 bytes for page_2711 through page_2720
  respectively), no stale-read issues, all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` values

  already documented). Cursor set to 2720.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 271)

* **Update (batch page_2701–page_2710, msgnum 2731-2732-2733-2734-2735-2736-2737-2738-2739-2740,
  continuing directly from batch 270's 2730, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  28 May – 1 June 2005. No new files, 8 extended
  (design/rig.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md, operations/running-lights.md,
  design/blue-water-suitability.md, construction/water-tank-lining.md,
  construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md,
  references/beachcruising-book.md — plus narrate-once-cross-link touches to
  people/kjell-karlsson.md, people/bolduc/dave.md, people/nels.md, and
  people/jack-gardiner.md — 12 files touched in total).

  **(1) Brent Geery's "Introduction" thread, resolved in full.** Continuing
  directly from batch 270's introduction, **Jack Gardiner** welcomed Brent
  with his own finished-build techniques — exterior ply skinned in two
  layers of 2 oz fabric to stop checking, "blue" extruded-foam insulation
  that does not collect water, a sewn rubber-roofing tiller boot, and
  rubber-clad commercial windows in place of stock lexan — folded into
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) with cross-links to
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md) and
  [construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md).
  **Dave Bolduc** then answered Brent's original four questions and several
  follow-ups in full: **1/4 in lexan** windows are plenty strong across
  three of Matt Layden's own boats, with Little Cruiser's own port windows
  surviving a broadside hit from a 17 ft powerboat at ~15 kts intact but for
  a buffed-out rub mark — folded into a new section on
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md)
  ("Window strength: a decades-long 1/4 in lexan track record, and one
  collision", cites [56]-[57], 57 total), alongside Brent's own
  window-reinforcement ideas (3/8 in lexan, wider hanging knees, a
  reinforcing ring at the bolt holes) as cheap insurance for a boat that is
  also the life raft. Little Cruiser and Swamp Thing's own **tiller-boot**
  and **water-tank** answers, plus Brent's plastic-welder tank idea and a
  raised tank-access objection, were folded into
  [construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md)
  (cites [28]-[29], 29 total). **Insulation** — Celotex (polyisocyanurate,
  identified by Brent) versus white polystyrene versus pink Home Depot
  foam, plus an ants-in-the-Celotex anecdote — and **fir marine plywood**
  sourcing/cost tradeoffs against Okoume were folded into
  [construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md)
  (cites [48]-[53], 53 total). **CCFL versus LED masthead lighting** was
  closed out (LEDs now cheap enough at ~$1 apiece that the Bolducs see no
  reason to switch), alongside Dave's own **$6 solar-garden-light anchor
  light** and its year-round performance, tested a few days later by
  **Alan** (`logicaidnz`) with a seasonal-charging caveat — both folded into
  [operations/running-lights.md](/operations/running-lights.md) (cites
  [35]-[36], extending the existing CCFL section). No boat/build of Brent
  Geery's own yet, so still no person page — remains on the standing
  below-threshold correspondent list.

  **(2) Kjell Karlsson's "2 pics 2 questions."** Weighed his "ParaPlastic"
  hull (complete except mast/boom/yard/battery, including its 70 kg bronze
  bottom plate) at **326 kg**, folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) ("Independent
  weight and displacement estimates", cite [102], 102 total); also asked,
  unanswered on record, how other builders route a solar panel's cable into
  the cabin (new section on
  [operations/running-lights.md](/operations/running-lights.md), cite [37],
  37 total) and whether Matt Layden's masthead drawing really does route
  the halyard's deck hole aft of the mast, twisting the halyard a
  quarter-turn (new section on [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md), cite [174],
  174 total).

  **(3) Derek Clark's "Books" thread, closed by Nels.** Derek Clark offered
  his own reading-list starter (Charles Stock's *Sailing Just for Fun*,
  Douglas Alvord's *Beachcruising*, Little and Walsh's *Beachcruising and
  Coastal Camping*, John Mellor's *Pilotage*); **Nels** added a Sven
  Yrvind link and nominated any future writing by the Bolducs or Matt
  Layden as reading-list material in its own right. Folded into a new
  section on
  [references/beachcruising-book.md](/references/beachcruising-book.md)
  ("A wider Paradox-sailor reading list", cites [4]-[5], 5 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 270)

* **Update (batch page_2691–page_2700, msgnum 2721-2722-2723-2724-2725-2726-2727-2728-2729-2730,
  continuing directly from batch 269's 2720, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  19–28 May 2005. No new files, 7 extended
  (construction/build-effort.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md,
  construction/non-skid-deck.md, operations/running-lights.md,
  construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md,
  construction/water-tank-lining.md, references/materials-sourcing-links.md
  — plus narrate-once-cross-link touches to people/mat-man22.md,
  people/david-beard.md, and people/alastair.md — 10 files touched in total).

  **(1) The "PS" thread — a direct continuation of batch 269's hull-weight
  and moving-methods thread.** **Derek Clark**'s own aside about finding
  scrap lead shaped like pipe discarded in a skip, useful as ready-made
  ballast, was folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) (cite [101],
  101 total). His "PS" grew into a wider exchange: **Adrian Dodd**, feeling
  close to a decision, asked whether Derek's moving crew were big or small
  (since he expected to do all the persuading himself), and weighed a
  precut kit against building from scratch — concluding that completing a
  boat from start to finish is part of Paradox's appeal, and that getting a
  boat in the water matters more than the method. **Alastair** reassured
  him that even a tired-out moving crew can safely leave the hull propped
  on rocks partway through a move, since there is "no danger of putting a
  hole" in the 18mm bottom. **Derek Clark** replied in kind — joking about
  his own moving crew (his 20-year-old son's "weedy" rock-band friends) —
  and closed with the observation that Paradox's real case rests on her
  practical track record, since in his view essentially all the design's
  negative criticism comes from theoreticians rather than actual owners.
  Folded into [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md)'s
  existing "Workspace" section (cites [81]-[83], 84 total).

  **(2) The "Non-skid" thread.** **David Beard** asked about roll-on
  truck-bed liner (Rhino-lining) as a possible deck non-skid. **Alastair**
  answered with a much cheaper find: rubberized stair-tread strip from
  Ikea's childcare section, a bargain at £1.50 a roll. **Mat**
  (`mat_man22`) added that workboat operators use similar spray-applied
  industrial coatings (now available in colors, though not cheap), having
  also seen one sprayed onto an old wooden Chris Craft's hull, and pointed
  to `rec.boats.building` for more. Folded into a new section on
  [construction/non-skid-deck.md](/construction/non-skid-deck.md) ("Truck-bed
  and industrial coatings", cites [6]-[8], 8 total), with narrate-once
  cross-link bullets added to people/alastair.md, people/david-beard.md, and
  people/mat-man22.md.

  **(3) A new correspondent's "Introduction," with four build questions.**
  **Brent Geery** (Los Angeles, `fasttimes`/`brentgeery`) introduced
  himself: already owns Don Elliott's construction book, plans just
  arrived, planning a fall build timed to avoid LA's summer heat, building
  as an apartment dweller in the space of a single outdoor parking spot
  (roughly 8 ft x 15 ft). His four questions were each folded into their
  existing topical home: Cold Cathode Fluorescent (CCFL) as a
  higher-efficiency alternative to LED masthead lighting (new section on
  [operations/running-lights.md](/operations/running-lights.md), cite [34],
  34 total); extruded polystyrene (XEPS) over white EPS for insulation,
  since EPS absorbs water
  ([construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md),
  cite [47] in the file's final citation block, which runs 38-47 with no
  gap); a plastic-welded poly-sheet water tank idea
  ([construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md),
  cite [27], 27 total); and marine-plywood sourcing in the Los Angeles area,
  answered by standing correspondent **Chris Ostlind** (`wedgesail`) with a
  supplier recommendation (Allied Plywood/Veneer) — Brent's own follow-up
  found the best-grade stock ran "a little over a grand for 10 sheets."
  Folded into a new section on
  [references/materials-sourcing-links.md](/references/materials-sourcing-links.md)
  ("Marine plywood sourcing in the Los Angeles area", cites [16]-[17], 17
  total). His apartment-build workspace detail was also added to
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md)'s
  "Workspace" section (cite [84]). No boat/build of his own yet, so no
  person page — added to the standing below-threshold correspondent list.

  No new person or boat pages, no prunings. Narrate-once-cross-link

## 2026-07-05 (batch 269)

* **Update (batch page_2681–page_2690, msgnum 2711-2712-2713-2714-2715-2716-2717-2718-2719-2720,
  continuing directly from batch 268's 2710, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  15–19 May 2005. No new files, 3 extended
  (operations/head-and-sanitation.md, operations/provisioning-and-water.md,
  construction/build-effort.md — 3 files touched in total).

  This batch closed out all three newcomer questions batch 268 had left
  unanswered on record, each answered in full within a few days by returning
  correspondents.

  **(1) "A Delicate question when cruising" — the toilet/sanitation thread,
  answered in full.** Daved Ferrell's Puget-Sound toilet question drew a
  five-reply round: **Dave Bolduc**'s short porta-potty answer; **Derek
  Clark**'s two further alternatives (a white-water-rafter's ammunition-box
  and cat-litter method, and a "green" composting-toilet design he judged
  could be scaled down to fit a Paradox); **Glen Maxwell**'s pointer to a
  commercial "WAG Bag" disposable kit; a new correspondent, **Paul Reppeto**
  (Olympia, WA), voting for the porta-potty and linking a "sawdust toilet"
  design; and **Dave Bolduc**'s own field report of using exactly that
  sawdust method at home during a plumbing repair, surprised at how well it
  controlled odor. Folded into the existing canonical home
  [operations/head-and-sanitation.md](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md)
  (cites [13]-[17], 17 total).

  **(2) Water-container question, answered.** Dave Bolduc gave Daved
  Ferrell his own direct answer: one-quart containers that fold flat,
  similar to clear plastic cubes sold at K-Mart and outdoor stores, topped
  off with two 2.5-gallon cubes; his preferred original small cubes
  (Campmor, Adventure 16) are now discontinued, so he expects to switch to
  space-hungrier round bottles once his stock wears out. Folded into the
  existing canonical home
  [operations/provisioning-and-water.md](/operations/provisioning-and-water.md#storage-individual-water-cubes-in-the-bilge)
  (cite [23], 23 total).

  **(3) "How heavy without ballast?" — answered, plus a build-time
  follow-up.** Adrian Dodd's bare-hull-weight question drew two independent
  guesses converging on the existing ~400 lb design figure — **Dave
  Bolduc**'s 450–500 lb, and **Glen Maxwell**'s 350–400 lb for "Zoë" before
  cabinets and ballast. Relieved, Adrian asked what part of the build tends
  to be most time-consuming; **Derek Clark** answered from his own "Enuf"
  build: no single stage stood out, but scarf joints were consistently
  irritating (he switched to a lapped butt joint for the floor) and chine
  logs were a pain (narrowed to simplify, with a warning against removing
  the vent box before the bulkheads are fitted); he also gave concrete
  hull-lifting data points at two build stages and vouched for hull-builder
  Alec Jordan's work quality on an unrelated commission. Folded into the
  existing canonical home
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#workspace)
  (cites [77]-[80], 80 total).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf",

## 2026-07-05 (batch 268)

* **Update (batch page_2671–page_2680, msgnum 2701-2702-2703-2704-2705-2706-2707-2708-2709-2710,
  continuing directly from batch 267's 2700, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  10–14 May 2005. No new files, 8 extended
  (boats/bris-paradox.md, people/kjell-karlsson.md, boats/zoe.md,
  references/mailing-list.md, references/off-topic-cross-posts.md,
  construction/build-effort.md, operations/provisioning-and-water.md,
  operations/head-and-sanitation.md — 8 files touched in total).

  **(1) Kjell Karlsson's "newer pics" thread.** The day after batch 267's
  roof/window update closed, **Kjell Karlsson** posted a brief follow-up
  showing the windows **test-fitted** in polycarbonate, describing the
  material's behavior under test-fitting only as "funny stuff." New section
  in
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#window-test-fitting-in-polycarbonate)
  (cite [75], 75 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link bullet on
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md#contributions)
  (cite [53], 53 total).

  **(2) Zoë hatch-skylight condensation.** A new correspondent, **Bob
  Sutton**, asked **Glen Maxwell** directly whether "Zoë"'s hatch skylight
  produced any condensation of its own; Glen answered in three words —
  "None at all." — closing a question the archive's earlier
  window-condensation discussion had left open. Folded into the existing
  canonical home
  [boats/zoe.md](/boats/zoe.md#condensation-ceclr-and-a-third-weight-estimate)
  (cite [41], 41 total) rather than
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`, since Zoë-specific hardware facts
  live on the boat's own page.

  **(3) The photo-storage saga, one year on.** **Paul** (`kayaker37`) asked
  whether the group's files section — again nearing capacity — warranted a
  `paradoxbuilders2`-style overflow group; **Nels** pointed out that
  `paradoxbuilders2` already existed, its files section still empty,
  confirming a year on that DC's May 2004 overflow group had seen little
  real use. Paul thanked him and told Kjell to keep the "ParaPlastic" photos
  coming. Folded into the existing canonical home
  [references/mailing-list.md](/references/mailing-list.md#the-photo-storage-limit-and-the-founding-of-paradoxbuilders2)
  (cites [33]-[35], 35 total).

  **(4) Off-topic: a "Fear of Sailing" group promo.** A one-off
  correspondent posting as "giddiap" ("Robert") shared a bare pointer to an
  unrelated new Yahoo group, "Fear of Sailing," pitched at the anxiety and
  inertia that can keep a would-be sailor ashore. New subsection in
  references/off-topic-cross-posts.md,
  following that file's existing per-section citation convention.

  **(5) Two unanswered newcomer questions, answered from existing archive
  facts.** **Adrian Dodd**, weighing whether to build at home and move the
  boat later, asked roughly how heavy the bare hull is during building —
  answered by cross-linking the archive's existing ~400 lb bare-hull-weight
  figure in construction/build-effort.md's "Workspace" section (cite [76],
  76 total). **Daved Ferrell** (`jackefroste`, a standing below-threshold
  correspondent) asked Dave Bolduc what size/brand water containers the
  Bolducs use (answered from their own pre-existing trip-inventory figures
  in
  [operations/provisioning-and-water.md](/operations/provisioning-and-water.md#storage-individual-water-cubes-in-the-bilge),
  cite [22], 22 total) and, separately, what to use for toilet facilities
  while cruising (folded into
  [operations/head-and-sanitation.md](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md),
  cite [12], 12 total, alongside the page's existing sanitation-question
  material).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 267)

* **Update (batch page_2661–page_2670, msgnum 2691-2692-2693-2694-2695-2696-2697-2698-2699-2700,
  continuing directly from batch 266's 2690, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  7–9 May 2005. No new files, 5 extended
  (design/rig.md, design/rudder.md, people/jack-gardiner.md,
  boats/bris-paradox.md, people/kjell-karlsson.md — 5 files touched in total).

  **(1) The "Mainsheet and outboard" thread, concluded.** The long-running
  thread carried over from batch 266 wound down. **Alastair** agreed proper
  jibing technique can take in slack in time, but recalled his own couple of
  unexpected jibes while inattentive ("it would only need one"). **Derek
  Clark**, having studied a photo of **Jack Gardiner**'s "Jill" on Bill
  Serjeant's website and realized how long the boom is, asked whether the
  mainsheet's attachment point could move further down the boom (doubled
  pulleys to recover the leverage) and whether side-mounting the outboard
  might keep it clear of the transom. **Jack Gardiner** answered from his own
  finished boat: moving the sheet down the boom would gain only a few inches
  at a real cost in leverage and sail geometry; he had already fixed the
  separate rudder/motor fouling problem with his own starboard motor-mount
  offset, though the sheet still snagged when he was distracted, prompting a
  half-serious hula-hoop-guide idea; he also described his own Mark II sail's
  too-far-forward yard attachment (boom rides low, occasionally drags,
  crease at the sail's top) and Glen Maxwell's modified topping lift as a
  possible fix, plus salvaging ~60 old fir rafters as future chine-log stock.
  Derek thanked him, confirmed his own Mark II sail was made but no
  mast/boom yet built, and found nothing he would change about the rig.
  Folded into the canonical home
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#a-horse-fitted-to-clear-the-outboard-and-the-mainsheet-angle-physics-behind-it)
  (extension, cites [170]-[173], 173 total).

  **(2) Stern-reentry safety, elaborated.** Pressed further on his stability
  objection from batch 266, **Jack Gardiner** clarified he was not doubting
  the boat's stability, which he knew firsthand — his concern was narrower:
  an unplanned reboarding in rough conditions ("Sudden High Intensity
  Training"), where the boat and swimmer both move independently regardless
  of hull stability. Drawing on his own free-diving/SCUBA experience, where
  conditions at the stern often change by the time a diver returns, he
  judged the area around his shins and knees (also where he stages his gear
  before diving) genuinely hazardous at the wrong moment — a matter of
  personal seamanship, not a fault in the boat. Extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill)
  (cite [128], 128 total; the file's pre-existing [53] gap, documented in
  batch 266, is unaffected).

  **(3) New topic: tiller construction.** **Derek Clark** opened a new
  "Tiller" thread describing his own laminated-ash tiller (epoxy hot enough
  to melt its mixing pot, straightened back out after 24 hours on a forming
  jig) and a faster alternative made from free scrapyard galvanized
  electrical conduit, bent using a drilled-block technique. **Jack
  Gardiner**, a former electrician like Derek, endorsed ash for its
  bend-and-return properties (steam-bend, dry, then epoxy in small batches
  to avoid overheating) and offered a second tube-bending method — pack the
  tube with sand to prevent kinking, with old tire rims as bender heads.
  Derek expected the sand method would also suit thin-walled tube, and
  more free galvanized conduit to turn up on "Enuf." New section in
  [design/rudder.md](/design/rudder.md#tiller-construction-laminated-wood-versus-bent-metal-tube)
  (cites [42]-[44], 44 total).

  **(4) Kjell Karlsson's "new pics" thread, closed out.** Two brief replies
  closed the "ParaPlastic" roof/window photo thread opened in batch 266:
  **Glen Maxwell** praised the workmanship, and **Paul** (`kayaker37`),
  checking the group's site after a time away, called the photos
  "inspirational" and mentioned he and his household were buying a house
  with a garage, so he might finally start his own build. Folded
  citation-only into the canonical home
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-further-round-of-roof-and-window-progress)
  (cites [73]-[74], 74 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link addition on
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md#contributions)
  (cite [52], 52 total).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf",
  STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate) appeared as author four times this
  batch (page_2662/msgnum2692, page_2663/msgnum2693, page_2667/msgnum2697,
  page_2668/msgnum2698) — still NOT promoted per standing instruction, no new
  trigger beyond ordinary topical contributions, not linked to

## 2026-07-05 (batch 266)

* **Update (batch page_2651–page_2660, msgnum 2681-2682-2683-2684-2685-2686-2687-2688-2689-2690,
  continuing directly from batch 265's 2680, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  3–7 May 2005. No new files, 5 extended
  (design/rig.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md, boats/bris-paradox.md,
  people/kjell-karlsson.md, people/bolduc/dave.md, people/alastair.md,
  people/jack-gardiner.md — 7 files touched in total).

  **(1) A horse fitted to clear the outboard, continued.** The "Mainsheet
  and outboard" thread carried over from batch 265 continued in full.
  **Alastair** answered **Jeff UK**'s fuel-line question on Dave Bolduc's
  behalf (Little Cruiser's old 4 hp Evinrude needed no pumping; the current
  3.3 hp Mercury needs pumping roughly hourly, with extra forward ballast
  compensating for the motor's weight), then separately explained why
  Paradox's longer boom forces the mainsheet to a further-aft angle than
  Little Cruiser's, ruling out a deck-mounted sheet (it would foul the
  boom-rolling swivel and the motor housing). **Derek Clark** floated
  bringing the sheet in over the rear deck regardless, citing his own
  150-mile-inland, canal-served cruising grounds as the reason an outboard
  is non-negotiable for him. Alastair judged **Jeff UK**'s tilted-motor
  storage sketch unsafe in a serious jibe (the slack sheet could catch the
  motor's leg and tear it off) and set aside his own metal-hoop-cage idea as
  not worth building, preferring an electric outboard stowed inside instead.
  **Jeff UK** explained proper jibing technique (taking in slack before the
  boom crosses) and proposed a shock-corded rubber teething-ring as a
  cushioning detail for his horse design. **Dave Bolduc** posted Little
  Cruiser's mainsheet-arrangement photos (already cited in batch 265) and
  answered the fuel-line question above. Folded into the canonical home
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#a-horse-fitted-to-clear-the-outboard-and-the-mainsheet-angle-physics-behind-it)
  (extension, cites [165]-[169], 169 total), with narrate-once-cross-link
  additions on
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#rig-electrics-and-furling)
  (cites [237]-[238], 240 total) and
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md#operations-and-cruising-practice)
  (cite [100], 100 total).

  **(2) Stern-deck stability, settled.** A safety objection inside the same
  thread — **Jack Gardiner** (posting as "John Gardiner") worried a raised
  hoop cage around the outboard would force an awkward stern-reentry hop,
  raising a sailor's center of gravity — drew **Alastair**'s direct answer:
  stability is not a practical problem for a Paradox, having personally had
  three adults standing on the aft deck at once. Pressed on whether that
  held true underway rather than at rest, Alastair confirmed the test was
  indeed conducted under sail, though — by his own account — without being
  tied on and without the sail fully hoisted at the time, a lapse he
  resolved to correct going forward, pointing again to his own website's
  "Stability & Balance" page as further evidence. Folded into the existing
  "Self-righting range, and bailing without opening the hatch" section at
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#self-righting-range-and-bailing-without-opening-the-hatch)
  (extension, cites [97]-[100], 100 total), with narrate-once-cross-link
  additions on
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill)
  (cites [126]-[127], 127 total; pre-existing [53] gap in that file
  untouched) and
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#rig-electrics-and-furling)
  (cites [239]-[240], see above).

  **(3) A "ParaPlastic" roof-and-window update.** **Kjell Karlsson** posted a
  further four-photo round to the files section's "paraplastic" folder,
  documented in full for the first time: the roof's own support pools built
  first for strength, the roof fitted over an already-painted interior, and
  the divinycell core ground away and filled with epoxy/fiberglass filler
  along each free deck edge to give the window openings something solid to
  screw into; windows were being cut as the update was posted. Folded into
  the canonical home
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-further-round-of-roof-and-window-progress)
  (new subsection, cite [72], 72 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link
  bullet on
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md)
  (cite [51], 51 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 265)

* **Update (batch page_2641–page_2650, msgnum 2671-2672-2673-2674-2675-2676-2677-2678-2679-2680,
  continuing directly from batch 264's 2670, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  30 April–3 May 2005. No new files, 3 extended
  (design/rig.md, construction/build-effort.md, people/bolduc/dave.md).

  **(1) Moving a finished hull without a helper.** Continuing Derek Clark's
  batch-264 trolley-mishap story for "Enuf," **Dave Bolduc** described "the
  big wheel" he built to turn Little Cruiser over solo, using it with a block
  and tackle and a jack rather than depend on a neighbor or his wife — a
  helper-dependent method he found unpredictable and always feared would
  damage the boat. **Derek Clark** adopted a simpler alternative in reply: a
  spare set of dinghy-trailer wheels and an axle strapped to a wooden baulk
  under the hull's point of balance, which slid "Enuf" easily along on timber
  and concrete. Folded into
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#workspace)
  (new bullet, cites [74]-[75], 75 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link
  bullet on
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md#operations-and-cruising-practice)
  (cite [98], 99 total).

  **(2) A horse fitted to clear the outboard, and the mainsheet-angle
  physics behind it.** **Derek Clark** revived his own unanswered
  September-2004 "mainsheet and outboard" question (see
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md), citation
  [55]), asking again whether the mainsheet should be led over the rear deck
  to clear a fitted outboard. **Jeff UK** (`roverticket`) sketched a **horse**
  — a small arch sized to the mounted motor's clearance, buildable in scrap
  copper tube or timber before being reproduced in stainless — and explained
  the **mainsheet angle**: flattening it makes the boom harder to haul to
  centerline when close-hauled. Weighing an outboard against a **yuloh**,
  Jeff put a motor, bracket, and a gallon of fuel at some **55 lb** on the
  stern against a yuloh's **$15** cost and **5 lb**, the latter the choice
  Matt Layden used for several thousand miles himself. **Derek Clark**,
  diabetic and unable to rely on extended sculling stamina, chose to keep
  both his outboard and a yuloh. **Dave Bolduc** posted photos of Little
  Cruiser's own mainsheet arrangement, workable with her outboard though the
  tiller handle sometimes catches the line; comparing the two boats, Jeff UK
  worked out why their mainsheets lean opposite ways — Little Cruiser's
  centerboard-set center of effort sits forward of amidships, Paradox's
  (no centerboard) sits aft — and separately asked, unanswered, how fuel
  reaches Little Cruiser's outboard from her on-deck tank. Folded into the
  canonical home
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#a-horse-fitted-to-clear-the-outboard-and-the-mainsheet-angle-physics-behind-it)
  (new subsection, cites [158]-[164], 164 total), with a
  narrate-once-cross-link bullet on
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md#operations-and-cruising-practice)
  (cite [99], see above).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack
  Gardiner's "Jill" boat-page-split remain open 3000-review items; neither
  surfaced this batch (Jack Gardiner did not appear). One genuinely new
  below-threshold correspondent: **jackefroste**/Daved Ferrell (Puget Sound,
  asking to see a built or in-progress Paradox nearby before starting his
  own; unanswered, no boat/build of his own) — added to the standing list,
  no page created. **Attribution-trap check:** David Beard, David LeBlanc, DC,
  and Glen Maxwell did not appear this batch; Derek Clark (four appearances
  as author) stayed correctly unconflated with DC; the two distinct UK
  Jeffs stayed correctly separate — "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`) authored three
  messages this batch, the other standing Jeff (`doryman_uk`) did not appear.
  Three-distinct-Larrys, two-distinct-Roberts, and two-distinct-Rons watches
  were not triggered. Derek Clark (boat "Enuf") remains a **strengthened
  promote-later candidate**, still not promoted per standing instruction.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 264)

* **Update (batch page_2631–page_2640, msgnum 2661-2662-2663-2664-2665-2666-2667-2668-2669-2670,
  continuing directly from batch 263's 2660, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  14–29 April 2005. No new files, 4 extended
  (operations/trailering.md, matt-layden/little-cruiser.md,
  people/bolduc/dave.md, construction/build-effort.md), plus
  2 cross-link-only additions with new citations (people/david-beard.md,
  people/alastair.md).

  **(1) The "trailer for paradox" thread closes out.** Continuing the thread
  opened in batch 263, **Dave Bolduc** described his own practice: a regular
  power-boat trailer works fine for both Little Cruiser and Swamp Thing,
  needing only custom 2x6 treated-lumber bunks shaped to each hull's rocker,
  with the boat centered slightly ahead of the axle to get the right
  **100-150 lb tongue weight** for his Honda Accord. **David Beard**
  separately reported modifying his own stock trailer to sit lower for
  low-tide launching, ahead of that year's Cedar Key gathering, quoting
  Derek Clark's original post in full. Folded into
  [operations/trailering.md](/operations/trailering.md#a-stock-power-boat-trailer-adapted-for-tongue-weight)
  (new section, cites [28]-[29], 29 total), with narrate-once-cross-link
  additions to
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md#operations-and-cruising-practice)
  (cite [96], 97 total) and
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md#contributions-and-stories)
  (cite [238], 238 total).

  **(2) Removable back window: design rationale and hinge ideas.** **Dave
  Bolduc** wrote a full essay on why Little Cruiser's back window is fully
  removable while Paradox's is permanent — Matt Layden's likely structural
  and water-ingress reasons for a fixed window, weighed against thousands of
  pooping/knockdown-free cruising miles with Little Cruiser's removable one,
  plus its ventilation, crew-access, and outboard-control benefits. A new
  correspondent, **John Pertalion**, praised the write-up. Three Paradox
  builders picked up the idea for their own boats: **William Serjeant**
  (citing his own "Folksong") proposed a hinged window for instant sealing;
  **Alastair** raised a safety concern that a top-hinged, inward-swinging
  window could strike a sailor's head in an unexpected wake, favoring a
  bottom hinge instead; Serjeant then detailed a bottom-hinged, door-like
  design swinging back over the aft deck; and **Derek Clark** proposed his
  own bottom-hinge variant for "Enuf" with a shrunk Lexan pane and enlarged
  wood frame. Folded into the canonical home
  [matt-layden/little-cruiser.md](/matt-layden/little-cruiser.md#the-removable-back-window-design-rationale-and-paradox-builders-adaptation-ideas)
  (new section, cites [28]-[34], 34 total), with narrate-once-cross-link
  additions to
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md#operations-and-cruising-practice)
  (cite [97], see above) and
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#construction-contributions)
  (cite [236], 236 total).

  **(3) "A day's work": a trolley, and a workshop mishap.** **Derek Clark**
  recounted building a trolley to move "Enuf" between his workshop and
  driveway — recycled, welded-on wheels freed with an angle grinder, bolted
  to a new wooden frame, with the hull raised on levers, blocks, trolley
  jacks, and axle stands to slide the trolley underneath. It worked well
  until, pushing the boat back into the workshop, the front wheels caught
  the half-inch door-sill lip, tore loose from the frame, and the hull
  skidded back onto its original resting blocks — prompting a "heated
  exchange" with his son. Folded into the existing
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#workspace)
  "Workspace" section (cite [73], 73 total).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf",

## 2026-07-05 (batch 263)

* **Update (batch page_2621–page_2630, msgnum 2651-2652-2653-2654-2655-2656-2657-2658-2659-2660,
  continuing directly from batch 262's 2650, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  12–13 April 2005. No new files, 4 extended
  (operations/ground-tackle.md, operations/crew-size-and-fit.md,
  chine-runners/how-they-work.md, operations/trailering.md), plus
  1 cross-link-only addition with no new citation (people/alastair.md).

  **(1) Anchor rode material, and a new SARCA anchor question.** John Hall
  opened a new "SARCA combination anchors" thread, asking about the
  Australian-made SARCA (galvanized/stainless, `anchorright.com.au`) —
  unanswered — and, in the same message, questioned **Dave Bolduc**'s
  earlier (batch 262) recommendation of dacron anchor rode. **Ray/rhaldridge**
  independently asked the identical dacron-versus-nylon question in a
  parallel reply. Dave corrected himself: he had meant 3-strand **nylon**
  line for its stretch and shock-absorbing qualities, not dacron. Ray
  thanked him and reflected on Paradox's iconoclastic design teaching him
  not to take convention for granted. Folded into
  [operations/ground-tackle.md](/operations/ground-tackle.md#rode-material-nylon-not-dacron-for-stretch)
  (new section, cites [41]-[44], 44 total).

  **(2) "Seating two?" resolved both ways.** Continuing the thread from
  batch 262, **Glen Maxwell** answered newcomer Alan's second-seat question
  with an alternative already in practice: his wife sits comfortably
  athwartships on the forward berth cushions, with good airflow through the
  vent box and no claustrophobia. **Alastair** gave his own considered
  answer to the same question — a full-length soft-top cabin roof paired
  with a second bench seat forward of bulkhead 3 would have been the better
  fix, but he had already built and fitted his as-designed solid roof by the
  time he worked this out. Folded into
  [operations/crew-size-and-fit.md](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md#sitting-two-side-by-side-on-the-seat-hatch-open)
  (extension, cites [52]-[53], 53 total).

  **(3) Box-keel chine-runner placement answered.** **Alastair** answered
  Richard Peeters's batch-262 question about where to place chine runners on
  a non-Paradox water-ballasted box-keel hull: the answer depends on which
  competing chine-runner theory is correct (some favor keeping the windward
  runner clear of the water), and by his own two working theories of the
  leeward-drag mechanism, either placement would be immaterial or neither
  would work well. Folded into
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#placement-on-a-water-ballasted-box-keel-hull-an-outside-question)
  (extension, cite [52], 52 total).

  **(4) A new "trailer for paradox" thread (Derek Clark).** **Derek Clark**
  opened a new thread asking whether trailer brakes are necessary for a
  laden "Enuf," having a candidate dinghy trailer with uncertain-capacity
  leaf-spring suspension and unbraked wheels, and asked for comparison
  details from Whisper's and Zoë's trailers. **Alastair** answered with the
  UK legal unbraked-trailer limit (750 kg gross, or half the tow vehicle's
  kerb weight, whichever is less) but cautioned that manhandling a loaded
  unbraked trailer is still difficult for one or two people. Derek thanked
  him, weighed towing options (an aging Land Rover vs. a modern Vauxhall),
  and related his motorway-patrolman contact's reassurance that private
  trailers are rarely stopped absent a flagrant violation. Folded into
  [operations/trailering.md](/operations/trailering.md#uk-trailer-weight-limits-and-whether-brakes-are-necessary)
  (new section, cites [25]-[27], 27 total, `[21a]` letter-suffix preserved
  untouched), with a cross-link bullet on
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#construction-contributions)
  (unchanged at 235 total).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf",
  STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate) appeared twice this batch as author
  (page_2628/msgnum2658 opening the trailer thread, page_2630/msgnum2660
  thanks/tow-vehicle anecdote) — still NOT promoted per standing
  instruction, no new trigger, and not linked to any person page (careful
  not to conflate with the distinct standing identity `people/dc.md`).
  Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill" boat-page-split
  remain OPEN 3000-review items; neither surfaced this batch (Jack Gardiner
  did not appear in pages 2621-2630). No genuinely new below-threshold
  correspondents this batch — John Hall (`jdmhall`), Ray/rhaldridge
  (`knobmaker`), and Derek Clark (`dclark52001`) are all already-standing
  identities, correctly not re-flagged as new.

  **Attribution-trap check:** David Beard (`dlb@`/`buster38801`) and David
  LeBlanc (`whisper@`/`meerkatoz`) both did not appear this batch, no
  confusion possible; DC (`kd7ixk@`/`betelgeuserdude`) did not appear,
  correctly not conflated with Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`,
  appeared twice, see above); two-distinct-UK-Jeffs, three-distinct-Larrys,
  two-distinct-Roberts, two-distinct-Rons watches all not triggered (no
  traffic from any of those identities this batch). Glen Maxwell appeared
  once this batch (page_2622/msgnum2652, "Seating two?" athwartships
  answer), correctly routed to a topical page
  (operations/crew-size-and-fit.md) rather than any
  people/glen-maxwell/ sub-file, since the content is neither Zoë-build,
  list-admin, nor Matt-Layden-eyewitness material.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 262)

* **Update (batch page_2611–page_2620, msgnum 2641-2642-2643-2644-2645-2646-2647-2648-2649-2650,
  continuing directly from batch 261's 2640, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  9–11 April 2005. No new files, 4 extended
  (design/interior-bins-and-floor.md, operations/ground-tackle.md,
  operations/crew-size-and-fit.md, chine-runners/how-they-work.md), plus
  2 cross-link-only additions with no new citations (people/alastair.md,
  people/bolduc/dave.md).

  **(1) "Construction Questions" thread continues — bulkhead 1 and anchor
  wells, the centerpiece.** Continuing the thread John Hall opened and Glen
  Maxwell answered in batch 261, **Derek Clark** described his own bulkhead-1
  practice on "Enuf": a hole cut to store spars inside, the rest of the
  forward compartment filled with expanded polystyrene foam plus silicone-glued
  empty soda bottles and an ice-cream tub for buoyancy, and a split floor aft
  of bulkhead 3 letting an anchor be lifted, bent onto its warp, and deployed
  without leaving the cabin. **Alastair** then gave his own answer to John
  Hall's original two questions: his through-bolted deck fittings need
  bulkhead-1 access from below, a sealed "watertight" compartment tends to
  fill with water it then cannot drain, and a flush anchor well was declined
  on the same weight-forward/exposed-bow grounds as his established
  stern-worked anchoring routine. Folded into
  [design/interior-bins-and-floor.md](/design/interior-bins-and-floor.md#design-intent-watertight-bulkhead-opening-is-optional)
  (extension, cites [24]-[26], 26 total) and
  [operations/ground-tackle.md](/operations/ground-tackle.md#a-flush-anchor-well-considered-and-declined-glen-maxwell)
  (cites [36]-[37], cross-referenced), with a cross-link bullet on
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#construction-contributions)
  (unchanged at 250 total).

  **(2) Anchor weights for a Paradox.** Derek Clark's separate anchor-weight
  question (15 lb Danforth-type and 14 lb fisherman, chosen per Matt Layden's
  own *Beachcruising and Coastal Cruising*, noting Little Cruiser uses much
  smaller ground tackle) drew **Dave Bolduc**'s fullest present-day
  recommendation on record: a Danforth-style anchor paired with a plow anchor
  for trickier bottoms, each on 10-14 ft of 3/16"-1/4" galvanized chain and
  100 ft of 3/8" dacron line — restating Matt's and Little Cruiser's own
  anchor weights already on file. Derek Clark thanked him and gave his own
  rode lengths (3 m and 2 m of 8 mm chain, ~30 m of 8 mm three-strand nylon
  each). Folded into
  [operations/ground-tackle.md](/operations/ground-tackle.md#anchors-builders-favor)
  (extension, cites [38]-[39], 39 total; John Hall's separate thanks to Glen
  Maxwell for the anchoring answer folded in as citation-only, cite [40], 40
  total), with a cross-link sentence on
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md#operations-and-cruising-practice)
  (unchanged at 111 total).

  **(3) "Seating two?" — a new correspondent's fit question.** New
  correspondent **Alan** (`logicaidnz`), working from the plans only and
  never having seen a Paradox in person, asked whether two smallish people
  can sit side by side on the seat with the hatch open. **Glen Maxwell**
  confirmed yes, "but the smaller they are the more comfortable they will
  be"; **Alastair** added that it is "a bit of a squeeze" and one occupant
  is squashed against the other as the boat heels underway. Alan's follow-up
  question — whether a second, lower seat below the forward end of the hatch
  might work instead — went unanswered. Folded into
  [operations/crew-size-and-fit.md](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md#sitting-two-side-by-side-on-the-seat-hatch-open)
  (new section, cites [48]-[51], 51 total).

  **(4) Chine-runner placement on a box-keel hull — an outside question.**
  New correspondent **Richard Peeters**, designing his own water-ballasted
  boat with a box keel (unrelated to Paradox's sharpie hull), asked whether
  chine runners are best fitted at the hull's chines or at the sides of the
  box keel; no reply is recorded. Folded into
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#placement-on-a-water-ballasted-box-keel-hull-an-outside-question)
  (new section, cite [51], 51 total).

  No new person or boat pages; no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link

## 2026-07-05 (batch 261)

* **Update (batch page_2601–page_2610, msgnum 2631-2632-2633-2634-2635-2636-2637-2638-2639-2640,
  continuing directly from batch 260's 2630, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  28 March – 9 April 2005. No new files, 5 extended
  (references/materials-sourcing-links.md, design/interior-bins-and-floor.md,
  operations/ground-tackle.md, chine-runners/windward-performance.md,
  operations/crew-size-and-fit.md).

  **(1) Sourcing cheap plexiglass/Lexan — four more replies, the centerpiece.**
  Continuing batch 260's plexiglass/Lexan thread, **Ray** (`rhaldridge`)
  replied that Paradox's overall design philosophy — its ingenuity and
  unapologetic single-mindedness — was as much a draw as any one detail, and
  that he intended to borrow the inside steering station for his own small
  catamaran design, reasoning an enclosed helm makes even more sense on a
  faster, wetter multihull. **Derek Clark** gave a UK sourcing tip (a local
  plastics firm's offcuts, discounted further by fitting parts to available
  sizes, with a wry aside that sending his wife or son in to ask gets better
  discounts — illustrated by a free aluminum cooking pot). **David Beard**
  suggested a local glass supplier for scrap or full-sheet stock, still
  cheaper than a chandlery. **Dave Bolduc** described buying six pieces of
  1/4 in scratch-resistant Lexan from an eBay seller to reglaze all of
  "Little Cruiser"'s windows in one combined-shipping order, arriving larger
  than advertised and in good condition, but cautioned shipping cost is the
  one real downside to buying online. Folded into
  [references/materials-sourcing-links.md](/references/materials-sourcing-links.md#inexpensive-plexiglass-and-lexan-for-glazing)
  (extension, cites [12]-[15], 15 total).

  **(2) "Upwind sailing" — a new correspondent's chine-runner/leeway question.**
  New correspondent **Doug Roller** asked whether Paradox suffers more leeway
  than a centerboard catboat upwind, or whether "the chine runners [are]
  magic." **Glen C. Maxwell** answered in kind — "They're magic!" — pointing
  to his own "Zoë" wake photographs as an illustration. Folded into
  [chine-runners/windward-performance.md](/chine-runners/windward-performance.md#pointing-angles-owners-report)
  (extension, cites [10]-[11], 11 total).

  **(3) A more open cockpit? Alastair's convertible-car analogy, restated.**
  Convinced on windward performance, Doug Roller's follow-up shifted to the
  boat's enclosed feel — could Paradox be built with a more open cockpit, or
  do other chine-runner designs exist? **Alastair** answered with the same
  convertible-car analogy he gave a year earlier for a claustrophobia
  question (top up: cozy and sheltered; top down: out in the wind and sun;
  an illusion either way, "but... a pretty good illusion"). No alternative
  more-open chine-runner layout is on record. Folded into
  [operations/crew-size-and-fit.md](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md#why-the-cabin-is-enclosed-a-convertible-car-analogy)
  (new section, cites [46]-[47], 47 total).

  **(4) Bulkhead #1 and an anchor well — a lurker's construction questions.**
  New below-threshold correspondent **John Hall** (a longtime lurker who had
  already built a 1:5 scale model, Tasmania) asked whether to seal bulkhead
  #1 watertight and foam the forward compartment solid instead of using the
  plans' optional opening, and whether anyone had tried a small self-draining
  anchor well over that compartment (John Welsford-style). **Glen C.
  Maxwell** answered both: he built "Zoë" with the bulkhead-1 opening but has
  never needed it, so the choice is a builder's own judgment call; and he
  considered an anchor well but was glad he skipped it, since going forward
  to the foredeck "isn't a lot of fun" — describing his own canvas-ice-bag
  anchor stowage and cockpit-worked retrieval routine instead. Folded into
  [design/interior-bins-and-floor.md](/design/interior-bins-and-floor.md#design-intent-watertight-bulkhead-opening-is-optional)
  (extension, cites [22]-[23], 23 total) and
  [operations/ground-tackle.md](/operations/ground-tackle.md#a-flush-anchor-well-considered-and-declined-glen-maxwell)
  (new section, cites [34]-[35], 35 total).

  No new person or boat pages; no prunings. One genuinely new below-threshold
  correspondent (Doug Roller/`drollerindy`) added to the standing list; Ray/
  `rhaldridge` and John Hall/`jdmhall` reconfirmed as already-standing
  identities. Derek Clark (boat "Enuf") appeared once more as author, still
  not promoted per standing instruction — his new citation carries no link to
  any person page (he has none), taking care not to conflate with the
  distinct standing identity DC/`kd7ixk` (`people/dc.md`). Jason Stancil

## 2026-07-05 (batch 260)

* **Update (batch page_2591–page_2600, msgnum 2621-2622-2623-2624-2625-2626-2627-2628-2629-2630,
  continuing directly from batch 259's 2620, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  20-28 March 2005. No new files, 6 extended (construction/water-tank-lining.md,
  people/bolduc/dave.md, people/alastair.md, people/chris-morejohn.md,
  chine-runners/windward-performance.md, references/materials-sourcing-links.md).

  **(1) Water tanks — not using them as designed, the centerpiece.**
  Continuing **Derek Clark**'s water-tank epoxy question from batch 259,
  **Dave Bolduc** described how "Little Cruiser" instead uses its built-in
  tank compartments to hold water and canned goods in individual containers
  — helping redistribute ballast as water is consumed and safer against a
  single contamination event — with tops built removable and gasket-sealed
  rather than epoxy-bonded. Derek fitted dividers to stop containers
  rattling, noting he is rarely far from good drinking water cruising the
  UK. **Alastair** proposed fitting bladders inside otherwise-watertight
  tanks so sea water could replace drawn-down fresh water and keep ballast
  constant; Derek considered it and declined. **Robert** (`cheepwings`)
  relayed a secondhand report that System Three had told Don Elliott its
  epoxy could work for fresh-water tanks given specific instructions, but
  said he would still prefer portable containers himself. Folded into
  [construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md#not-using-the-built-in-tanks-at-all-individual-containers-instead)
  (new section plus an epoxy-outlier paragraph, cites [22]-[26], 26 total),
  with cross-link-only additions to
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (cites [94]-[95], 95 total)
  and [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (no new citation, unchanged
  at 235 total).

  **(2) A new member's chine-runner windward-performance question.** New
  member **Michael Audette** called the bilge/chine runners "a radical
  concept" and asked how well the boat sails to windward and whether the
  storage "gizmos" make the boat harder to live with than it looks — the
  same skeptical first question the archive has recorded from newcomers for
  years (echoing "Jake"'s 2003 question). No reply on record. Folded into
  [chine-runners/windward-performance.md](/chine-runners/windward-performance.md)
  (new closing note, cite [9], 9 total).

  **(3) Cheaper plexiglass/Lexan glazing.** New correspondent **rhaldridge**
  ("Ray"), admiring the visibility Paradox's steering-station glazing gives
  but working on his own design, asked where to find cheaper plexiglass or
  Lexan than retail chandlery prices. **Glen C. Maxwell** advised checking
  the yellow pages for local plastics dealers' discounted drops or damaged
  sheets; **Gene T.** pointed to `harborsales.net` for the central East
  Coast and remarked that Paradox itself uses only a minimal amount of
  glazing material compared with some other designs. Folded into
  [references/materials-sourcing-links.md](/references/materials-sourcing-links.md#inexpensive-plexiglass-and-lexan-for-glazing)
  (new section, cites [9]-[11], 11 total).

  **(4) A Chris Morejohn "Hogfish" sharpie for sale.** Dave Bolduc flagged a
  "for sale" listing for a 40 ft version of Chris Morejohn's "Hogfish"
  sharpie, noting its "Paradox"-style chine runners visible in two of the
  listing photos. Folded into
  [people/chris-morejohn.md](/people/chris-morejohn.md) (cite [7], 7 total),
  with a cross-link-only bullet on
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (part of the [94]-[95]
  addition above).

  No new person or boat pages; no prunings. Two genuinely new below-threshold
  correspondents (Michael Audette/`col_sartoris`, rhaldridge/Ray/`knobmaker`)
  added to the standing list; Robert/`cheepwings` and Gene T./`tehansky`
  reconfirmed as already-standing identities. Derek Clark (boat "Enuf")
  appeared twice more as author, still not promoted per standing instruction.
  Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill" boat-page-split
  remain OPEN 3000-review items, neither surfaced this batch. Attribution-trap
  and identity watches all clear (see batches.log for full detail). Cursor
  advanced to 2600.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 259)

* **Update (batch page_2581–page_2590, msgnum 2609-2610-2612-2613-2615-2616-2617-2618-2619-2620,
  continuing directly from batch 258's 2608; two new internal archive gaps at
  msgnum 2611 and 2614 — no page_NNNN.eml exists for either)**: 7-18 March
  2005. No new files, 4 extended (matt-layden/swamp-thing.md,
  matt-layden/biography.md, boats/whisper.md, construction/water-tank-lining.md),
  plus 3 cross-link-only additions with no new citations
  (people/david-beard.md, people/alastair.md) and one extended person page
  with one new citation (people/ron-eike.md, cite [12], 12 total).

  **(1) Swamp Thing's rudder rebuild — the centerpiece.** Answering **Derek
  Clark**'s "Anybody there?" check-in, **Dave Bolduc** reported he had been
  busy rebuilding **Swamp Thing**'s bottom (133 old ring nails pulled, ground
  down to the plywood/mahogany chine-log glue line) and making her a new
  rudder blade — beveling the lead-weight mounting hole on both sides to lock
  the lead in, rather than the plans' wood-screw method, a technique Matt
  Layden himself used on both Swamp Thing and Little Cruiser. New
  correspondent **David Finch** praised the technique and asked about
  blueprints for the boat (no reply on record; added to the below-threshold
  list). **Alastair** added a real safety warning: melting down old lead pipe
  risks trapped moisture flashing to steam and firing molten lead out like a
  gun (his own 6 in pipe threw lead about 10 ft). Folded into
  [matt-layden/swamp-thing.md](/matt-layden/swamp-thing.md#rudder-rebuild-technique-beveled-lead-versus-screwed-lead-and-a-lead-melting-caution)
  (new section, cites [16]-[18], 18 total), with a cross-link-only addition to
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (no new citation, unchanged at 235
  total).

  **(2) Matt Layden's small racing canoe.** **Ron** (`sailman58`), a
  long-standing below-threshold correspondent, asked Dave Bolduc — "the
  official source for Paradox" — whether plans existed for the canoe Matt had
  raced in the last two Watertribe Challenges. Dave had no plans and had not
  seen the boat in person, but relayed what Matt told him directly: a 14 ft
  Rob Roy-based design with just a 28 in beam, aboard which Matt can sleep
  and cook; originally steered by shifting body weight, now fitted with a
  trim tab; and, per that year's Everglades Challenge progress reports, past
  Checkpoint 3 (mile 273) in 3 days 16 hours 12 minutes at a 3.1 knot average
  including rest stops. Folded into
  [matt-layden/biography.md](/matt-layden/biography.md#the-14-ft-rob-roy-based-canoe-and-the-2005-everglades-challenge)
  (new section, cites [54]-[55], 55 total), with
  [people/ron-eike.md](/people/ron-eike.md) extended (new bullet, cite [12],
  12 total).

  **(3) A 2005 hatch rebuild on "Whisper."** In the same "Anybody there?"
  thread, **David Beard** mentioned rebuilding "Whisper"'s hatch ahead of the
  new season; answering Derek Clark's question about what exactly the work
  involved, David explained he was fixing the hatch's sliding action and
  adding a skylight copied from Glen Maxwell's on "Zoë" — "nothing major."
  Folded into
  [boats/whisper.md](/boats/whisper.md#a-2005-hatch-rebuild-sliding-action-and-a-skylight-copied-from-zoë)
  (new section, cites [85]-[87], 87 total), with a cross-link-only addition
  to [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (no new citation,
  unchanged at 237 total).

  **(4) Water tanks: a blunt safety warning against structural epoxy.**
  **Derek Clark**, reconsidering whether to fit built-in water tanks or stick
  with bladders, called SP Systems directly to ask about using their epoxy to
  seal a potable tank; the technician's reply was blunt — "For Christ's sake
  don't do that; at the least you'll be very sick and at the worst you'll go
  blind." The only safe method is a dedicated potable coating over the
  sealant; Blakes sells one, but only in expensive 9-litre cans, leaving
  Derek leaning back toward bladders. Folded into
  [construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md)
  (extension to the existing "Epoxy: mixed opinions" section, cite [21], 21
  total).

  No new person or boat pages; no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Standing-open-items check: Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf," STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate) appeared

  quoted twice) — still NOT promoted per standing instruction, no new
  trigger. Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill"
  boat-page-split remain OPEN 3000-review items, neither surfaced this batch.
  Attribution-trap check: David Beard (`dlb@`/`buster38801`) appeared twice as
  author (page_2586, page_2589) correctly kept separate from David LeBlanc
  (`whisper@`/`meerkatoz`, did not appear); DC (`kd7ixk@`/`betelgeuserdude`)
  did not appear; Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`) appeared four times as
  author, confirmed via headers each time, no confusion with DC;
  two-distinct-UK-Jeffs, three-distinct-Larrys, two-distinct-Roberts,
  two-distinct-Rons watches all not triggered (no traffic from any of those
  identities this batch). One genuinely new below-threshold correspondent:
  **David Finch** (`salutlemec`, page_2583/msgnum2612), added to the standing
  list. Byte sizes cross-checked via `wc -c` against dispatch instructions

## 2026-07-05 (batch 258)

* **Update (batch page_2571–page_2580, msgnum 2599-2608, continuing directly
  from batch 257's 2598; no new gaps)**: 20-23 February 2005. No new files, 2
  extended (boats/bris-paradox.md, boats/little-jim.md), plus 2 cross-link-only
  additions with no new citations (people/alastair.md, people/kjell-karlsson.md).

  **(1) The "new pics" thread's tail.** Following Glen Maxwell's praise for
  Kjell Karlsson's latest "ParaPlastic" photos (recorded in batch 257), two
  more replies arrived: **Derek Clark**, comparing the photos to his own
  build's documentation, joked he was relieved his own pictures weren't
  detailed enough to invite comparison; **Dave Bolduc** judged the pace fast
  enough that Kjell would likely be sailing "ParaPlastic" by spring or
  summer, and — as he routinely did for builders posting to the group's
  files section — brightened and resized the new photos in Photoshop before
  re-uploading them alongside Kjell's untouched originals. Folded into
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#layup-materials-and-chine-runner-attachment-for-a-foam-core-hull-revisited)
  (cites [70]-[71], 71 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (no new citation,
  unchanged at 50 total).

  **(2) "Little Jim web site moving" — the centerpiece.** Going over to
  broadband service, **Alastair** closed his old Claranet e-mail account and,
  with it, the `asjg.clara.co.uk` address that had hosted his "little Jim"
  construction photos and cruise write-ups since 2002. He moved the site to
  `www.as4jg.freeuk.com` and asked the list to update any bookmarks. **Derek
  Clark** initially couldn't get past the new host's front page and asked if
  the address was right; Alastair confirmed it was Freeuk, not the
  similarly-named Freenet, and suspected a copy-paste slip. **Paul**
  (`kayaker37`) reported reaching the new site without trouble. **James
  Greene** — a genuinely new correspondent — then diagnosed the likely fault:
  the URL had gone out with backslashes rather than forward slashes, an
  artifact of Alastair's Pegasus Mail client, and supplied the corrected
  address. Derek Clark confirmed he'd since gotten through; Alastair agreed
  James was "of course" right, noting his own browser (Opera) had silently
  corrected the typo all along. Folded into
  [boats/little-jim.md](/boats/little-jim.md#the-construction-photo-site-moves-to-freeuk-february-2005)
  (new section, cites [96]-[102], 102 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link
  addition to [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (no new citation,
  unchanged at 235 total).

  **(3) An off-topic promotional post, recorded for completeness.** One page
  (page_2580, msgnum 2608) was a one-off spam-style introduction from
  **"thirstydragon2001,"** promoting his own external `fancyboats.com`
  custom-boat-catalog website, with a passing compliment on "the small boats
  featured on Paradox Builders" — no Paradox content, no reply on record. Per
  the established off-topic-promotional-spam precedent (see the batch
  covering page_1873), it is recorded here for completeness but not folded
  into any bundle body file.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 257)

* **Update (batch page_2561–page_2570, msgnum 2589-2598, continuing directly
  from batch 256's 2588; no new gaps)**: 16-20 February 2005. No new files, 4
  extended (construction/bottom-painting.md, operations/first-boat-suitability.md,
  boats/bris-paradox.md, people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md),
  plus 3 cross-link-only additions with no new citations
  (people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md, people/kjell-karlsson.md,
  construction/build-effort.md).

  **(1) The "not past it??" age-suitability thread closes out.** **Terry**
  (`celtikman59`), the 63-year-old newcomer whose age question ran through
  batches 255-256, thanked the list for its encouragement. **Kjell
  Karlsson** — the Swedish [BRIS-Paradox](/boats/bris-paradox.md) builder,
  56 himself, expecting to sail that summer with his wife (by then 60)
  aboard — added his own "senior testimony," pointing to his "Paraplastic"
  photo folder for the accommodations he judged fit for two. **Robert**
  (`carolfo`/"cheepwings") replied with praise for the boat and its bronze
  bottom, and a dry aside about Kjell having "outed" his wife's age online.
  Folded into
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#age-and-the-case-for-going-smaller-rather-than-larger)
  (cites [31]-[33], 33 total).

  **(2) Layup materials and chine-runner attachment, restated for a
  newcomer — the centerpiece.** Reading the age-suitability thread, **"Jeff
  UK"** (`roverticket`) asked Kjell Karlsson what material and resin he was
  using for his composite hull's layup and how he planned to fasten the
  chine runners, recalling his own 15-years-earlier 16 ft airboat built from
  Airex/Herex foam core in polyester resin — "bullet proof" even driven flat
  out over a shingle bank. Kjell answered with his settled BRIS method
  (fiberglass/epoxy inside, polyester/epoxy outside, divinycell core) and
  his bronze-sheet-plus-fiberglass-putty chine runners, then posted a
  further "ParaPlastic" photo round (painting, decking, laminated deck —
  "working like a polecat, still s-l-o-w progress"), drawing praise from
  **Glen Maxwell**. Folded into
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#layup-materials-and-chine-runner-attachment-for-a-foam-core-hull-revisited)
  (new section, cites [66]-[69], 69 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link
  addition to [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (no new
  citation, existing facts).

  **(3) A Yahoo Files folder cannot be renamed, only replaced.** **Derek
  Clark**, wanting his "So-So" photo folder relabeled to match his boat's
  settled name, "Enuf," asked **Glen Maxwell** whether a Files-section
  folder could simply be renamed. Glen had found no way to do so directly —
  only to delete the folder and upload a new one — which Derek accepted,
  the older photos being of limited use and taking up space regardless.
  Folded into
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#diagnosing-yahoos-photo-resolution-limit)
  (cites [83]-[84], 84 total), with a one-line cross-link addition to the
  existing Derek Clark paragraph on
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md) (no new
  citation, unchanged at 72).

  **(4) A terse antifouling answer.** Answering Dave Bolduc's still-open
  question from batch 256 about a hard antifouling paint for a trailered
  boat, **Glen Maxwell** reported using West Marine's **CPS** with very good
  results generally, but no better luck than anyone else keeping paint on
  the spots that rub the sea floor. Folded into
  [construction/bottom-painting.md](/construction/bottom-painting.md#a-hard-antifouling-paint-for-a-trailered-boat)
  (cite [38], 38 total), with a cross-link bullet on
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (no new citation).
