## 2026-07-05 (batch 256)

* **Update (batch page_2551–page_2560, msgnum 2579-2588, continuing directly
  from batch 255's 2578; no new gaps)**: 11-17 February 2005. No new files, 8
  extended (design/rig.md, people/alastair.md, construction/build-effort.md,
  construction/topside-painting.md, construction/bottom-painting.md,
  people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md, operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md,
  people/david-beard.md), plus 2 cross-link-only additions
  (operations/first-boat-suitability.md, references/paradox-uk-website.md).

  **(1) Glen Maxwell's full Awlgrip roll-and-tip recipe, and why not to spray
  it — the centerpiece.** **Dave Bolduc**, weighing whether to spray
  "Swamp Thing" and "Little Cruiser" with an HVLP outfit during their refits,
  asked **Glen Maxwell** to restate in full how he had painted "Zoë." Glen's
  answer: a single **1-quart Awlgrip kit** (paint, H3002 converter, T0031
  reducer) yields about **53 oz** of finished paint, enough for three thin
  coats with some left over, rolled vertically in sections no larger than
  about 6 sq ft and tipped horizontally with a foam brush, always brushing
  **into** the last section just finished, with about **four spare foam
  brushes** kept on hand; Awlgrip is, in his view, the cheapest paint to buy
  in practice since its gloss lasts roughly **ten years** without fading. He
  loves HVLP spray outfits but does **not** recommend spraying Awlgrip or any
  other two-part polyurethane, since atomized it is highly poisonous and
  calls for a full-face mask fed with an oxygen-rich air supply. **David
  Beard**, prompted by that hazard, urged builders to request a **Material
  Safety Data Sheet (MSDS)** for every product used on a boat — free of
  charge and required on request under US federal law. Folded into
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md#glen-maxwells-full-awlgrip-roll-and-tip-recipe-and-why-not-to-spray-it)
  (new section, cites [38]-[41], 41 total), with narrate-once-cross-link
  additions to
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (cites [234]-[235], 235 total) and
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cites [236]-[237], 237
  total).

  **(2) A still-open question: hard antifouling for a trailered boat.** In
  the same thread, Dave Bolduc separately asked for a **hard antifouling
  paint** recommendation suited to a trailered boat, since his usual Pettit
  Trinidad rubs off bouncing over shallows or drying out at low tide, and
  wondered whether an epoxy/copper coating might be the answer. The question
  remained unanswered on the record. Folded into
  [construction/bottom-painting.md](/construction/bottom-painting.md#a-hard-antifouling-paint-for-a-trailered-boat)
  (new section, cite [37], 37 total).

  **(3) A dumb sheave versus a bearing sheave at the masthead.** **Derek
  Clark** asked for the case against making the masthead halyard sheave a
  plain "dumb sheave" (a groove cut in the wood) rather than a running
  bearing sheave. **Alastair**, who runs a Tufnol sheave on a stainless pin
  himself, judged that since hauling up the sail is the single most
  physically demanding task aboard, fitting a proper bearing is worthwhile;
  Derek agreed it was "a good con" for the bearing over the plain groove.
  Folded into
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#a-dumb-sheave-versus-a-bearing-sheave-at-the-masthead)
  (new section, cites [155]-[157], 157 total), with a one-line
  narrate-once-cross-link bullet on
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cite [235], 235 total).

  **(4) Boat-naming follow-up: "So-So" becomes "Enuf."** **Derek Clark**
  reported that, prompted by an enquiry in another group, he had checked his
  own build photos (still filed under "So-So") and found the build had moved
  on much further than he remembered over six weeks; he posted a new picture
  but withheld close-ups as too rough to show, and asked that the photo file
  be renamed to "Enuf" to match the boat's now-settled name (previously
  chosen on his wife's suggestion). Folded into
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md) (extension to
  the existing cardboard-mock-up paragraph, cite [72], 72 total). Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf") remains a STRENGTHENED promote-later
  candidate, still NOT promoted per standing instruction.

  **(5) A UK trailer-sailer case for an older builder, and a Honda 2 hp
  reaffirmed.** Continuing batch 255's "past it??" thread, **William
  Serjeant** (over 70 himself) made the trailer-sailer case for age
  directly — no super fitness or agility needed, and Paradox's many UK
  launching sites put a wide range of cruising grounds within reach by road
  and ferry (Inverness, Ullapool, Falmouth, Milford Haven, the Scilly Isles,
  the Norfolk Broads, southwest Ireland, the French Morbihan, the Dutch
  Ijsselmeer) — and repeated his Jordan Boats/Alec Jordan kit-built pointer.
  **David Beard** reaffirmed the small-and-light outboard principle: his own
  Honda 2 hp reaches hull speed at about half throttle, while larger
  outboards in the 50 lb range gave no luck getting the sailing trim right.
  Folded into
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#age-and-the-case-for-going-smaller-rather-than-larger)
  (extension, cite [30], 30 total) and
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md#weighing-a-2-hp-four-stroke-against-a-bigger-motor-and-whether-paradox-can-plane)
  (extension, cite [30], 30 total), with a one-line cross-link summary added
  to [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md)
  (no new citation).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack
  Gardiner's "Jill" boat-page-split remain OPEN 3000-review items, neither

## 2026-07-05 (batch 255)

* **Update (batch page_2541–page_2550, msgnum 2569-2578, continuing directly
  from batch 254's 2568; no new gaps)**: 28 January - 3 February 2005. No new
  files, 5 extended (operations/self-steering.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md,
  references/paradox-uk-website.md, matt-layden/biography.md,
  operations/first-boat-suitability.md).

  **(1) Glen Maxwell's own reasoning for a wind vane over an autopilot — the
  centerpiece.** **Dave Bolduc** asked why Glen was building a wind vane for
  "Zoë" at all given he already had a working electric autopilot. **Glen
  Maxwell** answered as a retired electronics technician who does not trust
  electronics: the brand-new **Autohelm** autopilot aboard "Hogfish Maximus"
  lasted only three days into the Atlantic crossing; on his own trimaran he
  normally carries two autopilots and would fit a vane too, but multihulls
  accelerate too fast for a wind vane to track the apparent wind; what draws
  him to a vane regardless is its simplicity and zero current draw. Folded
  into
  [operations/self-steering.md](/operations/self-steering.md#glen-maxwells-horizontal-axis-wind-vane-concept-for-zoë)
  (extension to "Glen Maxwell's horizontal-axis wind-vane concept for 'Zoë,'"
  cites [25]-[26], 26 total).

  **(2) A UK kit-built option: Jordan Boats.** **William Serjeant** announced
  that **Alec Jordan** of Jordan Boats (`www.jordanboats.co.uk`) had agreed to
  supply him with the first "kit" Paradox, working directly with **Matt
  Layden** on the project, and separately invited anyone interested in
  manufacturing Paradox kits under license (or kits of any other plywood
  design) to contact Alec. Folded into
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-kit-built-option-jordan-boats)
  (new section, cites [38]-[39], 39 total).

  **(3) A second, independent confirmation of the stability-curve location.**
  **Jack Gardiner**, posting under his standing "John Gardiner"/`tidybowlmann`
  alias, gave the same "Longyard folder" answer Glen Maxwell had already given
  the same day to Alan Davison's righting-moment-curve question (batch 254).
  Folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#self-righting-range-and-bailing-without-opening-the-hatch)
  (cite [96], 96 total).

  **(4) No written account of Matt's 2003 Everglades Challenge exists on
  record.** Alan Davison asked whether Matt Layden had written up his 2003
  Everglades Challenge race; no reply is recorded. Folded into
  [matt-layden/biography.md](/matt-layden/biography.md#the-2003-everglades-challenge-an-eyewitness-account)
  (new closing note, cite [53], 53 total).

  **(5) Age is no obstacle — a case for going smaller, not larger.** A
  63-year-old prospective builder (**Terry**, new correspondent, no boat/build
  of his own on record) asked whether he was "too old" for a Paradox and
  whether an outboard had ever been tried as auxiliary power. **Dave Bolduc**
  judged 63 the perfect age for the boat's easy motion, small sail, light
  anchors, and shallow 9 in draft, arguing sailors should move down in boat
  size as they age rather than up, and cited his friend Dave Gatan's own
  microcruiser-to-Rob-Roy-Yawl progression into his 70s. **John Hall** (60,
  Tasmania, already building) added that the enclosed cabin's UV/sun
  protection is a real benefit for older sailors. **Alastair** described
  Paradox as a very low human-energy boat to own, trailer permanently
  attached, launching effortless, sailing itself undemanding. Folded into
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#age-and-the-case-for-going-smaller-rather-than-larger)
  (new section "Age, and the case for going smaller rather than larger,"
  cites [26]-[29], 29 total).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf",
  STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate) did not appear this batch, still not
  promoted. Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill"
  boat-page-split remain OPEN 3000-review items, neither surfaced this batch.
  One genuinely new below-threshold correspondent (Terry/`celtikman59`),
  recorded in `backlog.md`. Alan Davison (`bty468633`), John Hall
  (`jdmhall`), and William Serjeant all reconfirmed standing identities.

  the two UK Jeffs, the three Larrys, and the two Robert/Ron pairs did not
  appear this batch — no confusion possible. Jack Gardiner's From: header

  correctly recognized as his standing alias and kept distinct from John

## 2026-07-05 (batch 254)

* **Update (batch page_2531–page_2540, msgnum 2559-2568, continuing directly
  from batch 253's 2558; no new gaps)**: 16-28 January 2005. No new files, 5
  extended (people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md,
  construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md, references/building-plans.md,
  operations/self-steering.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md).

  **(1) The "Hogfish Maximus" thread's true close: designer attribution.**
  **Nels** pointed newcomer Aaron Edelman to the group's Files section (the
  "Larger Paradox"/"comp" file and a dedicated "Hogfish Maximus" folder of
  photos) after his tinyurl link failed. Edelman then asked directly whether
  "Hogfish Maximus" was a Bolger design; **Glen Maxwell** answered plainly
  that Chris Morejohn designed and built her himself, closing out the
  multi-batch Jesse-Cooper/Hogfish-Maximus comparison thread. Folded into
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (extension to "An Atlantic crossing aboard 'Hogfish Maximus'," cites
  [36]-[38], 38 total).

  **(2) A terse thank-you closes the epoxy-brand thread.** **Felix Bors**
  thanked Gene T. for his USComposites review, closing the epoxy-brand
  question first raised in batch 253. Folded into
  [construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md](/construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md)
  (cite [15], 15 total).

  **(3) A new correspondent asks about Paradox builds in Europe — the
  centerpiece.** **Renzo Callebert**, a prospective builder in Belgium who had
  already ordered the plans and construction book, asked whether a Paradox
  had been built in Europe yet or sailed the North Sea. **Dave Bolduc**
  answered with a link to the standing `smallsailboats.co.uk/paradox/names.htm`
  list of built and in-progress Paradoxes, noting some are in Europe. Folded
  into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#source-and-cost)
  (new paragraph "European builds," cites [84]-[85], 85 total).

  **(4) Glen Maxwell's own wind-vane concept for "Zoë."** Answering a direct
  question about wind-vane work he had mentioned only in passing, **Glen
  Maxwell** laid out, still on paper, a horizontal-axis vane design specific
  to Paradox's boom-over-rudder geometry — the boom would simply deflect the
  vane clear during tacking and jibing rather than fouling a conventional
  vertical-axis vane, driving a servo tab mounted on standoffs on the
  rudder's left side so the folding rudder blade could still kick up and
  retract as designed. He planned a transom/rudder mockup to finalize the
  idea before building. Folded into
  [operations/self-steering.md](/operations/self-steering.md#glen-maxwells-horizontal-axis-wind-vane-concept-for-zoë)
  (new section, cites [23]-[24], 24 total; frontmatter description updated).

  **(5) A stability-curve pointer.** A newcomer who recalled but had lost
  track of a righting-moment-versus-heel-angle plot for Paradox (or possibly
  Little Cruiser) asked the list to help relocate it; **Glen Maxwell**
  confirmed it is on file in the group's Photo section, in the Longyard
  folder — the same curve already discussed via Don Elliott's published
  figure and Alastair's own field measurements. Folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#self-righting-range-and-bailing-without-opening-the-hatch)
  (cites [94]-[95], 95 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 253)

* **Update (batch page_2521–page_2530, msgnum 2549-2558, continuing directly
  from batch 252's 2548; no new gaps)**: 14-15 January 2005. No new files, 4
  extended (people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md,
  people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md, boats/whisper.md,
  construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md).

  **(1) The "Jesse Cooper" thread's actual substance, deepening batch 252's
  summary — the centerpiece.** Derek Clark's "Thanks Bruce" closed the
  "what is Jesse Cooper" exchange; **Nels** clarified his original mention
  referred to a comparison file Glen had posted, posted a tinyurl link, and
  mused that a yawl rig with a Little Cruiser–style mainsail treatment would
  amount to a simplified junk rig. **Glen Maxwell** then gave his fullest
  technical answer yet on "Hogfish Maximus": she carries a dagger board used
  only hard on the wind; her one real handling flaw was a lack of
  longitudinal stability off the wind, making her wander under hand steering
  (worsened by wind-vane and autopilot trouble), which Glen suspected a
  better-fitting board could cure but which Morejohn tolerated to avoid a
  clunking noise; Glen — who dislikes steering for its own sake — rated the
  complaint as possibly subjective, since Morejohn never called it a
  problem. Glen announced a planned 300-mile non-stop shakedown course for
  "Zoë" in the Gulf of Mexico, deliberately never more than 50 miles from
  port, to gather an objective comparison against "Hogfish Maximus." **Aaron
  Edelman** reported Nels's tinyurl link no longer resolved, left unanswered
  in the record. Narrated in full at
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (extension to "The full return report" section, cites [32]-[35], 35
  total).

  **(2) A "well known source of grief": Larry Whited's aside on Don
  Elliott.** Closing out the "Zoë interior shots" foam-flotation thread,
  **Larry Whited** wryly remarked that Don Elliott giving builders grief over
  their choices was itself "a well known source of grief," quoting Chris
  Ostlind's earlier aside in full. Folded into
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (cite [233], 233 total).

  **(3) The Cedar Key/Pirates Cove scheduling conflict, answered.** Replying
  to David Beard's standing question, **Larry Whited** ("a CK freak")
  confirmed he would be at Cedar Key again, and **Glen Maxwell** likewise
  planned to attend, possibly sailing down from Steinhatchee. Folded into
  [boats/whisper.md](/boats/whisper.md) (extension to "A recurring
  scheduling conflict" section, cites [83]-[84], 84 total).

  **(4) The USComposites epoxy question, answered.** **Dave Bolduc**
  answered Felix Bors's brand question with concrete testing methodology —
  buy a small amount of any candidate brand first and actually laminate
  glass, glue wood, and mix fairing compound with it before committing to a
  bulk order, checking gel time, stiffness, and sandability; he settled on
  West System this way and sources supplies from raka.com and
  johnrsweet.com. **Gene T.** gave USComposites itself a satisfied review —
  their slow-hardener epoxy suited every job with no complaints — and added
  that Dynel fabric (bought from Jamestown) drapes around corners far better
  than woven fiberglass cloth, though their own fiberglass tape also worked
  fine. Folded into
  [construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md](/construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md)
  (extensions to "Brands used in practice" and "Pricing and supplier watch,"
  cites [12]-[14], 14 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 252)

* **Update (batch page_2511–page_2520, msgnum 2539-2548, continuing directly
  from batch 251's 2538; no new gaps)**: 13-14 January 2005. No new files, 5
  extended (people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md,
  people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md, people/nels.md,
  construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md, chine-runners/how-they-work.md).

  **(1) Glen Maxwell's full "Atlantic trip" return report — the centerpiece.**
  Back on his newly purchased farm and mid-build on a cabin, barn, and
  workshop, **Glen Maxwell** posted his fuller verdict on the May–June 2004
  Atlantic crossing aboard **Chris Morejohn**'s "Hogfish Maximus": loaded to
  the waterline (not light ship) with Morejohn's tools, spares, and a year's
  food for a family of four, the boat behaved beautifully, with too small a
  cockpit the one real shortcoming and pounding no issue at all — comparing
  favorably, on a repeat point of sail, against a Hans Christian 38 he had
  once delivered from the Virgin Islands to Jacksonville. He relayed
  Morejohn's own request to record that "Hogfish Maximus," despite
  resembling an enlarged Paradox, is Morejohn's own design from a separate
  design process, and that his hulls are not traditional sharpies but a
  "**Modified Sharpie**." He also uploaded 2001 Bahamas under-sail photographs
  of "Hogfish Maximus" to the files section. **Nels** asked for photographs or
  a lines sketch of "Hogfish Maximus" against Little Cruiser, Paradox, and
  Bolger's "Jesse Cooper"; **Derek Clark**, new enough not to know the
  reference, asked what "Jesse Cooper" was, answered by **Bruce Hallman**
  (one of Bolger's "Square Boats," citing boatdesign.com and Bolger's *30 Odd
  Boats* ch. 12) — cross-linked to the fuller Jesse Cooper/Paradox hull
  comparison already on record at
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md). New
  correspondent **Aaron Edelman** also asked what "Hogfish Maximus" was.
  **Dave Bolduc** thanked Glen for the photographs and said he, like
  everyone, looked forward to the promised written trip article. Narrated in
  full at
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (new section "The full return report: cockpit size, pounding, and 'Modified
  Sharpie' versus sharpie," cites [25]-[31], 31 total), with
  narrate-once-cross-link additions to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [125], 125 total) and a supplementary
  citation at
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md) (cite
  [50], 50 total).

  **(2) The "Zoë interior shots" foam-flotation thread's true tail-close.**

  already been covered in an earlier archive message (numbered 134 in the
  group's own numbering); Glen thanked him and added that earlier message's
  full body to the files section for easier future retrieval. Folded into the
  existing canonical home
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (tail addition to "The choice to leave 'Zoë' without foam flotation," cites

  **(3) A supplier/pricing note.** **Dave Bolduc** flagged a 20%-off West
  System resin sale at Jamestown Distributors; new correspondent **Felix

  uscomposites.com epoxy and fiberglass, noting competitive pricing (~$27/gal

  (new section "Pricing and supplier watch: sales and a lower-cost source,"

## 2026-07-05 (batch 251)

* **Update (batch page_2501–page_2510, msgnum 2529-2538, continuing directly
  from batch 250's 2528; no new gaps)**: 1-13 January 2005. No new files, 6
  extended (people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md,
  construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md, boats/whisper.md,
  people/david-beard.md, people/nels.md, references/building-plans.md).

  **(1) "Zoe interior shots" — the fullest record yet of Glen Maxwell's
  decision to sail "Zoë" without foam flotation** (12-13 January 2005), the
  batch's centerpiece. A new correspondent, "Wes," asked whether "Zoë" was
  built without foam insulation. **Glen Maxwell** confirmed it directly and
  gave his fullest explanation on record: after 45 years of sailing —
  including a recent Atlantic crossing with **Chris Morejohn** aboard
  "Hogfish Maximus" — he had never owned an unsinkable boat and preferred
  being able to inspect behind any foam for dry rot or moisture; he suspected
  the plans' specified foam, concentrated in the deck head, served insulation
  more than unsinkability; and he did not want to give up interior volume,
  instead mounting fittings to blocks glued directly to the hull. He recalled
  **Don Elliott**'s criticism of the choice at the time. **Chris Ostlind**
  added a wry aside on Don Elliott's piety. **Nels** could not find Glen's
  promised foam-lining article in the files section and asked for the
  installation method in full (prompted by Bolger's own recent foam-lined
  "Birdwatcher" redesign) plus a full account of the Atlantic crossing —
  both left unanswered in this window. **David Beard** contributed a
  real-world counterpoint: "Whisper"'s original builder had glued foam
  directly to the hull and then neglected the boat, leaving rainwater
  standing in her; tearing the foam out for inspection, David found sound
  wood wherever the glue bond had been good and real problems (worst at the
  corners) wherever it had been poor, leading him to prefer screwed-in
  installation going forward. Narrated in full at
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (new section "The choice to leave 'Zoë' without foam flotation," cites
  [226]-[230], 230 total), with David Beard's data point given its own home at
  [construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md)
  (new section "A real-world data point: glued foam gone wrong on a neglected
  boat," cite [46], 46 total) and narrate-once-cross-link summaries added to
  [boats/whisper.md](/boats/whisper.md) (cite [82], 82 total),
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cite [235], 235 total), and
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [122]-[123], 123 total).

  **(2) The plans FAQ's first-week-of-2005 tail** (1 January 2005): a second
  reply to new correspondent johnbrianscothern's plans question (opened in
  batch 250) pointed to UK builder William Serjeant's own Paradox site as a
  second concrete source. Folded into the existing canonical home
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (cite [83],
  83 total).

  **(3) A minor unresolved thread.** ben_azo/"Puck III" resurfaced twice still
  seeking identification of a mystery boat photo ("PP2.jpg") uploaded in
  batch 250; Nels suggested trying the `smallsailboats`, microcruising, or a
  boat-design Yahoo group instead — no identification recorded in this
  window. One-line summary folded into
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [122]).

  New correspondent Felix Bors introduced himself with two genuine
  construction dimensional questions (chine-runner drawing-#11 offsets, stem
  foot offset) that went unanswered in this window — recorded in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md` as a below-threshold watch item, no topic-page
  fact to add. No new person pages; no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Cursor set to 2510.

  **(1) The true tail-close of "Colour Schemes"** (22-28 December 2004):
  **Dave Bolduc**, working in Photoshop, posted a hull-colour chart to the
  group's Files section, favoring a light olive shade ("H") as a pairing with
  a red sail. **Derek Clark** replied that he'd found a similar light green
  in PS Elements, planned to have a paint supplier colour-match it, and noted
  household paint should suffice (cheaper than "yacht" paint) since Alastair
  had used it on "Little Jim" — closing with Christmas wishes to "Dave and
  Mindy." Dave Bolduc's reply rounded out the practical picture: most paint
  stores can colour-match a printed swatch directly; he favors oil-based
  house paint over latex for hardness, though latex works fine for many
  builders; and "Little Cruiser" has always been finished in Red Devil
  one-part polyurethane semi-gloss enamel (since discontinued, bought up at a
  sale price beforehand), with any reasonable oil-based paint expected to
  serve well elsewhere. Folded into the existing canonical home
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md)

## 2026-07-05 (batch 249)

* **Update (batch page_2481–page_2490, msgnum 2509-2518, continuing directly
  from batch 248's 2508; no new gaps)**: 8-21 December 2004. No new files, 3
  extended (construction/topside-painting.md, construction/scarf-joints.md,
  construction/fastening-and-gluing.md), plus narrate-once-cross-link
  additions to people/nels.md and people/alastair.md. Three short threads,
  each folded into an existing canonical home.

  **(1) The "Colour Schemes" thread's true tail-close** (8-9 December 2004):
  **Chris Ostlind** replied to Derek Clark's "Admiral Clark CDM" grey-hull/
  air-gun joke with a tangential anecdote about a basketball-arena mascot's
  compressed-air T-shirt launcher. **Nels** mock-warned "Admiral Clark" to
  expect papers reassigning him to the Persian Gulf, or alternatively
  suggested Spitfire colours in case a "Zero" appeared — a pun on the
  grey-hulled Paradox "Zero" named earlier in the same thread — signing off
  with a wink about spelling "colours" the Queen's English way. **Derek
  Clark** closed the joke, declining the Persian Gulf posting ("did my time
  when I was young and silly"), joking that "Enuf" would be better equipped
  than many Royal Navy ships "except for the crew," needling Nels that Her
  Majesty likely has "a little man" do her spelling, and reporting his new
  red sail had arrived — "big and very red." Folded into the existing
  canonical home
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md)
  (cites [32]-[34], 34 total).

  **(2) "The Bottom of Enuf"** (16-17 December 2004): **Derek Clark** began
  fitting his bottom panel — locating an offcut on the upturned bow with
  wire nails (a method credited to Al Law), cutting a lap joint on the
  forward section with a router — only to discover, once the last full
  sheet was offered up for marking, a 6 in gap where the two pieces should
  have met, the result of having shifted the forward piece too far forward
  earlier. **Alastair** commiserated ("have a LARGE drink and a good night's
  sleep"). A stiff gin and tonic did not shrink the gap, so Derek reshaped
  the forward joint into a **dovetail-shaped lap joint**, using a spare
  offcut to fill most of the rear shortfall and closing any remainder as a
  plain butt joint forward of the transom — "moving on, regardless." Folded
  into the existing canonical home
  [construction/scarf-joints.md](/construction/scarf-joints.md) as a new
  section, "A dovetail-shaped lap joint after a bottom-panel measuring
  error" (cites [15]-[17], 17 total).

  **(3) "Sheerstrakes"** (20-21 December 2004): **Derek Clark** fitted his
  sheer strakes at 20mm rather than the plans' 25mm (a sapele offcut from
  the chine logs allowed cutting both at that size), then asked for advice
  on bronze ring nails bending over in their last third of length as though
  the point could not penetrate the glue line, even through a predrilled
  hole. **Nels** asked for more detail. **Alastair** had solved the same
  problem himself by drilling bigger pilot holes — even a noticeably larger
  hole still left the nails impossible to withdraw once driven. Derek then
  found pilot-hole sizes tabulated on the Classic Marine (UK chandlery)
  website and added one sixty-fourth of an inch, after which the nails went
  in perfectly. Folded into the existing canonical home
  [construction/fastening-and-gluing.md](/construction/fastening-and-gluing.md)
  as a new section, "Bronze ring nails bending over: pilot-hole sizing"
  (cites [25]-[28], 28 total).

  Narrate-once-cross-link additions: [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md)
  (cites [119]-[120], 120 total, new bullet covering the Colour-Schemes tail
  and the Sheerstrakes reply) and [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md)
  (cites [233]-[234], 234 total, new "Commiseration and a pilot-hole fix"
  bullet covering the Bottom-of-Enuf and Sheerstrakes replies).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 248)

* **Update (batch page_2471–page_2480, msgnum 2499-2508, continuing directly
  from batch 247's 2498; no new gaps)**: 7-8 December 2004. No new files, 3
  extended. A single thread: the tail and close of "Colour Schemes."

  The whole batch is the continuation and close of the "Colour Schemes"
  thread opened in batch 247. **Alastair** joked that UK nights rarely reach
  epoxy-softening heat ("just once would be nice!"). **Derek Clark** mused on
  a paler green to go with his new red sail. **Nels** admired **Zoë**'s
  finish, drawing a purely appreciative "Thank you Nels" reply from **Glen
  Maxwell** (quoting her message in full, no new content). **William
  Serjeant** pointed to the UK Paradox photo gallery
  (smallsailboats.co.uk/paradox/para_gallery.htm) for comparing hull
  colours directly. Derek then gave a fuller walkthrough of other builders'
  hull colours — "Petite Lady"'s yellow, Zoë, "Swash"'s white, "Zero"'s grey,
  "Little Jim"'s green — settling on a paler green just ahead of grey with
  red trim, and admitting only a workmanlike finish was realistic for a
  first-time painter. **Nels** reacted "Blech!" to flattish grey and
  suggested pale gold instead, since a lighter colour is easier to repaint
  over if disliked. **Alastair** advised Derek to simply match whatever
  paint colour he already had on hand rather than "wasting good sailing
  time," and warned that PVC floor tape makes a poor trim-line material.
  **Chris Ostlind** riffed on the idea as "urban camouflage." Derek closed
  the thread in good humor, joking that a grey hull with a mounted air rifle
  could pass for "one of our modern navy ships," signing off "Admiral Clark
  CDM." Folded into the existing canonical home
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md)
  (cites [22]-[31], 31 total), with narrate-once-cross-link additions to
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cites [231]-[232], 232 total)
  and [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [117]-[118], 118 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 247)

* **Update (batch page_2461–page_2470, msgnum 2489-2498, continuing directly
  from batch 246's 2488; no new gaps)**: 4-7 December 2004. No new files, 4
  extended. Two threads: a tail-coda to the lap-butt-joint discussion, and a
  new "Colour Schemes" thread.

  **Nels** and **Derek Clark** closed out the lap-butt-joint exchange begun
  at the end of batch 246: a units mix-up (Derek's "25 mm" nail was meant to
  be half an inch, not a full inch — a lifelong habit of thinking Imperial
  despite the UK having been metric for decades) was resolved, with Nels
  reassuring him the joint should be plenty strong on epoxy and glass alone,
  little faith needed in nails, and recommending a test joint; screws (not
  nails) as adjustable, removable clamps; and a shared glue-line-thickness
  answer — a routed recess left with a few proud ridges at the target depth,
  epoxy thickened to a pancake-syrup consistency, a doubled taped joint
  inside paired with a single flush seam outside, and poly sheet/wax paper
  plus temporary screws to hold the joint flat while it cures. Folded onto
  the existing canonical home
  [construction/scarf-joints.md](/construction/scarf-joints.md) (new section
  "Glue-line thickness and clamping for a lap butt joint," cites [12]-[14],
  14 total), with narrate-once-cross-link additions to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [115]-[116], 116 total).

  **Derek Clark** opened a new "Colour Schemes" thread, having ordered a red
  sail for visibility and unsure what hull colour to pick; his epoxy
  supplier's claim that a colour additive alone would protect the epoxy from
  UV degradation struck him as dubious enough to phone and confirm. **Chris
  Ostlind** gave the fuller technical answer: paint's opacity (not colour)
  is what actually shields epoxy from UV, a UV-inhibited varnish is needed
  if left uncoated, complementary hull/environment colour schemes aid both
  visibility and visual appeal, and darker colours should be kept close to
  the waterline since epoxy can soften above roughly 150°F. **Alastair**
  added a countervailing data point — a friend's plain-epoxy dinghy kit,
  built to UK conditions two years earlier with no added UV protection,
  showed no deterioration — plus light banter with Derek about a shared
  boyhood pond and the UK's ubiquitous black-and-rose-painted narrowboats.
  Folded into the existing canonical home
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md) (new
  section "Choosing a hull colour, and why the epoxy's UV protection matters
  more than the shade," cites [15]-[21], 21 total), with a
  narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cites [229]-[230], 230 total).

  No new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. Timeline reached 4-7 December 2004.
  Standing-open-items check: Derek Clark (STRENGTHENED promote-later
  candidate, boat "Enuf," appeared six times this batch across both
  threads, still NOT promoted per standing instruction), Chris Ostlind
  (`wedgesail`, standing below-threshold identity, appeared three times),
  Alastair (`openboat`/`alopenboat`, "Little Jim," standing identity,
  appeared three times) all checked, none newly promoted. Jason Stancil
  identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill" boat-page-split remain OPEN
  3000-review items; neither surfaced this batch (neither correspondent
  appeared in pages 2461-2470). Attribution-trap check: David Beard
  (`dlb@`/`buster38801`) and David LeBlanc (`whisper@`/`meerkatoz`) both did
  not appear — no confusion possible. DC (`kd7ixk@`/`betelgeuserdude`) did
  not appear; Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`) appeared six times

## 2026-07-05

* **Update (batch page_2451–page_2460, msgnum 2479-2488, continuing directly
  from batch 245's 2478; no new gaps)**: 28 November – 4 December 2004. No new
  files, 5 extended. Three threads: a chine-log-gluing/lap-butt-joint
  construction pair, and a new locker-divider/stove-support/chain-stowage
  interior thread.

  **Derek Clark**'s chine-log build continued directly from batch 245's
  "Bloody chine logs AGAIN!" thread: fitting the first 19 mm sapele log
  proved more fraught than planned — sprung back under tension and struck
  his hand hard enough to draw blood — and he chose to leave it clamped a
  few days, hoping the wood would "learn" its new shape before gluing. Both
  chine logs were fitted within the week (the second undocumented — "look at
  the other one and use the imagination"). Turning next to the bottom
  panels, he sought a way to avoid scarf-jointing 18 mm plywood sheets,
  having found a **lap butt joint** design by Nels elsewhere; **Nels**
  answered with fastening guidance (wire nails or clenchable fasteners, not
  ring nails, to avoid starving the joint of epoxy under clamping pressure)
  and a glass-tape reinforcement scheme strong enough to exceed the
  surrounding plywood's own strength, and confirmed the joint is
  self-aligning if the routed recess is cut to width carefully. Derek closed
  with concrete scantlings for his own 18 mm bottom (a 9 mm lap, 9 mm filler
  piece, 25 mm bronze ring nails) and confirmed he is fitting stores and
  lead ballast rather than water tanks. Folded onto the existing canonical
  homes
  [construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md](/construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md)
  (extends the chine-log-thickness section, cites [24]-[26], 26 total) and
  [construction/scarf-joints.md](/construction/scarf-joints.md) (new section
  "A lap butt joint as a scarf alternative," cites [9]-[11], 11 total), with
  a narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [114], 114 total).

  **Alastair** ("Little Jim") opened a new "Locker dividers" thread: his rear
  locker's floor curves up toward the transom, so his nominally-upright
  dividers met it at an acute angle, letting a bucket of heavy chain
  eventually punch out its own bottom (probably from road jolting rather
  than sea use) — the fix is fitting rear-locker dividers square to the
  (curved) floor rather than plumb, which also makes the divider easier to
  reach over. **William Serjeant** asked how the re-angled dividers would
  affect the locker's stove support; Alastair answered with a wedge-shaped
  block to keep the support's axle bolt horizontal. **Derek Clark** asked
  whether heavy chain might be better stowed under the floor amidships
  instead; Alastair explained the chain in question was his bungee-held
  second anchor, and that the locker rework should let it move somewhere
  less accessible. Folded onto
  [design/interior-bins-and-floor.md](/design/interior-bins-and-floor.md)
  (new section "Rear-locker divider geometry, and where to stow heavy ground
  tackle," cites [17]-[21], 21 total), with a narrate-once-cross-link
  addition to [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cites [227]-[228],
  228 total).

  No new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. Timeline reached 28 November – 4 December 2004.

* **Update (batch page_2441–page_2450, msgnum 2469-2478, continuing directly
  from batch 244's 2468; no new gaps)**: 20-28 November 2004. No new files, 5
  extended. Two threads, both continuations of standing UK-registry and
  chine-log material.

  **William Serjeant**'s fresh registry-amendment request (batch 244) drew a
  direct answer: **Steve (Kansas City)** reported his own boat still
  unnamed, being built per plan, progressing slowly behind other
  priorities — his first mention of Serjeant's UK build-count table, having
  only just noticed it linked from `paradoxbuilders` itself. Serjeant
  thanked him, restating that the registry's accuracy depends entirely on
  builders reporting in. **Derek Clark**, prompted by Serjeant's mention of
  an autumn-2005 build start, asked whether the new Paradox would replace or
  supplement Serjeant's existing boat, "Bumper" — Serjeant confirmed "as
  well as," explaining he seldom keeps a boat more than two seasons, and
  that a fresh lead-ballast casting would be needed since none remained from
  his last boat. Folded onto the existing canonical home
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md)
  (extends "A UK and worldwide build count," cites [34]-[37], 37 total),
  with a narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/steve-kc.md](/people/steve-kc.md) (cite [11], 11 total).

  **Derek Clark** reopened chine-log construction with "Bloody chine logs
  AGAIN!" — questioning why the plans specify 38 mm chine logs when his own
  19 mm sapele stock bent readily lengthwise, seeing little benefit beyond a
  nailing/gluing surface, and comparing Paradox unfavorably to Shane Acton's
  twice-circumnavigating 9 mm stitch-and-glue plywood hull; he judged
  Paradox "massively over engineered" for its era. **Jack Gardiner**
  answered with his own completed, sailing Paradox as evidence — stitch and
  glue, fir chine logs, filleted, no flex felt — and confirmed, when Derek
  asked, that his boat is the one known on the list as "Jill Baby" (i.e.
  "Jill," launched September 2004; per standing instruction, no boat-page
  split was made). **Nels** floated a triangular-cross-section, taped chine
  log as a stiffer, lighter alternative. Derek closed the thread relaying
  Alastair's point that the chine runners themselves add strength to the
  side/bottom joint, and Matt Layden's own rough-weather experience likely
  drove the design's robust scantlings; weighing that against his own
  planned single-handed circumnavigation of the UK (historical rivers and
  creeks of England and Wales, skipping Scotland/Cape Wrath), Derek judged a
  lighter 19 mm build acceptably safe and proceeded with it. Folded onto the
  existing canonical home
  [construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md](/construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md)
  (new section "Chine-log thickness: why 38 mm rather than 19 mm," cites
  [18]-[23], 23 total), with narrate-once-cross-link additions to
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites [124]-[125],
  125 total) and [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [113], 113 total).

* **Update (batch page_2431–page_2440, msgnum 2459-2468, continuing directly
  from batch 243's 2458; no new gaps)**: 13-19 November 2004. No new files, 6
  extended. Three small threads, all tail-ends or direct continuations of
  prior batch-243 material.

  **Derek Clark**'s Sapele purchase (batch 243) had a coda: cutting the
  chine logs burned out his circular saw halfway through the second cut —
  "one step forward and two back" — but the smoke, smell, and noise
  persuaded his wife a proper table saw was worth buying. **Chris Ostlind**
  (`wedgesail`, standing below-threshold identity) replied in sympathy,
  vowing never again to treat a dull blade or tired tool with disdain.
  Folded onto the existing canonical home
  [construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md](/construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md)
  (cites [16]-[17], 17 total).

  **Alastair** ("Little Jim") reported a **rudder-edge repair** — reinforcing
  the worn leading edge with split, annealed 15 mm copper water pipe,
  epoxy-riveted through countersunk holes — and, in the same post, confirmed
  to **Derek Clark** (met in person on a recent outing) that the same
  15 mm split-copper-pipe technique used between the furling spindle and the
  deck had held up under tension-only loading. An unplanned stress test
  followed the next day: an unexpected gybe sailing home (F5 following wind,
  then a broad reach, sail 50% reefed) badly twisted that fitting's split
  end, discovered only once trailered; repaired with hammered-out bends and
  soldered doublers. New content on
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cite [226], 226 total).

  A six-message **LED-lighting thread** ran the rest of the batch: Alastair,
  comparing notes years after wiring "Little Jim" with LEDs, found a cheap
  set of off-the-shelf bicycle LED lamps and judged the design could have
  been made for a boat tent. Derek Clark recalled trying the same idea a
  year earlier with a Pifco lamp, having e-mailed the manufacturer (in vain)
  to make a purpose-built all-round white LED lamp for sailors. **Kjell
  Karlsson** pointed to a Swedish supplier (`mitron.se`) offering a lamp
  closer to a genuinely boat-designed unit, which Alastair confirmed looked
  purpose-built, at nearly £44. **Dave Bolduc** described the Bolducs' own
  cheaper ~$18 DIY all-round LED light (Perko fresnel globe plus
  `whitelightled.com` LEDs, photos on microcruising.com), and Alastair noted
  he had already built a similar lensless light of his own in perspex tube,
  with a less sharp color cutover on a red-green-white version. Folded onto
  the existing canonical home
  [operations/running-lights.md](/operations/running-lights.md) (new section
  "Off-the-shelf LED sources for a boat-tent light," cites [28]-[33], 33
  total), with narrate-once-cross-link summaries added to
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (cite [93], 93 total) and
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [50], 50
  total).

  Separately, **William Serjeant** posted a fresh registry-amendment
  request for `www.smallsailboats.co.uk/paradox/names.htm`, judging "by now
  there must be at least 14 boats" on the water or under construction — a
  direct continuation of his own build-count tracking from batch ~232-233.
  Folded onto the existing canonical home
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md)
  (cite [33], 33 total).

## 2026-07-23

* **Update (batch page_2421–page_2430, msgnum 2449-2458, continuing directly
  from batch 242's 2448; no new gaps)**: 7-12 November 2004. No new files, 4
  extended. Two threads carried the batch, both tail-ends of prior threads.

  The **"Amelie"/"ultimate cruiser" steel-enlargement exchange** closed with
  **Jack Gardiner**'s one-word "agreed," seconding Chris Ostlind's reply from
  batch 242 in full — no new substantive content, folded onto
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [123], 123 total).
  A previously-uncited transition message from batch 242 (page_2418, msgnum
  2446 — Paul/`kayaker37` asking Nels whether he was near Radisson,
  Saskatchewan, hunting the builder of an old Bolger Birdwatcher) turned out
  to open a genuinely new, self-contained sub-thread rather than continuing
  the "Amelie" design-comparison narrative (already closed at cite [55] in
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md)).
  **Nels** and Paul traded leads across five further messages — a plywood-hull,
  aluminum-deck modified Birdwatcher, an e-mail address traced to one **Aeneas
  Precht** of Saskatchewan, a recalled *Common Sense Newsletter* article by
  Benie Wolfard, and Paul finally reaching Precht by phone (just back from a
  trip to China, promising answers in two weeks) — with no further follow-up
  recorded anywhere later in the archive. Purely off-topic, non-Paradox color;
  folded onto a new section on
  references/off-topic-cross-posts.md
  ("Tracking down a mystery Bolger Birdwatcher builder near Saskatchewan," own
  [1]-[6] citations per that file's per-section numbering convention).

  **Derek Clark** reopened his own chine-log/sheer-strake timber question with
  a concrete follow-up: American (Brazilian) mahogany versus **Sapele**
  (African mahogany, ~40% cheaper) for the four sheer-strake/chine-log pieces,
  comparing durability, working qualities, and strength against English oak.
  **Nels** restated his fastener-purchase-first rule of thumb for his own
  Micro's mahogany chine logs; Derek cross-checked Sapele against the UK
  **TRADA** (Timber Research and Development Association) website, confirming
  good screw/nail/glue performance, settled on Sapele, and reported the final
  purchase — 4.45 m of 250x40mm stock for £105 including VAT, plus ~£30 of
  turning offcuts. Folded onto the existing canonical home
  [construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md](/construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md)
  (new section "A follow-up choice: Brazilian mahogany versus Sapele," cites
  [12]-[15], 15 total), cross-linked on [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md)
  (existing cite-linked bullet extended, no new citation number needed there).

  No new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. Standing-open-items and attribution-trap checks clean (David
  Beard and David LeBlanc both absent; DC absent, Derek Clark appeared three
  times, all confirmed via headers, no confusion; both UK Jeffs, all three
  Larrys, both Roberts, both Rons — none appeared, no conflicts). Jack
  Gardiner's message carried his standing "John Gardiner"/`alex29`/
  `tidybowlmann` From-name variant, confirmed same person via headers per
  precedent. No genuinely new below-threshold correspondents this batch — all
  four participants (Jack/John Gardiner, Nels, Paul/`kayaker37`, Derek Clark)
  were already-established standing identities. Derek Clark's STRENGTHENED
  promote-later status (boat "Enuf") reconfirmed, not promoted inline; Jason
  Stancil's OPEN 3000-review identity-reconcile item and Jack Gardiner's
  "Jill"-boat-page-split item both carried forward unresolved, neither
  surfacing this batch. Cursor set to 2430.


* **Update (batch page_2411–page_2420, msgnum 2439-2448, continuing directly
  from batch 241's 2438; no new gaps)**: 5-7 November 2004. No new files, 6
  extended. Two threads carried the batch. The **"Paint Blisters ???"**
  thread (opened batch 241) drew three further explanations for the same
  underlying mechanism — trapped heat/moisture with nowhere to escape:
  **Nels** asked what kind of tarp had been used, noting some tarps (poly
  among them) are known to cause this kind of damage since a cover should
  breathe, the same problem reported with car covers, and floated an
  incomplete cure as a contributing cause; **Larry** (`LJGeib`/
  `lorenzojose2000`, standing second-Larry identity) confirmed the
  incomplete-cure/outgassing theory from his own experience, noting the
  blisters may settle and re-adhere over time or the job may need
  redoing; and new below-threshold correspondent **Chris Ostlind**
  (`wedgesail`) added a sealed-air-gap/focused-heat theory, drawing a
  vivid parallel to a plastic kayak left on a lawn killing the grass
  beneath within an hour. Folded onto
  [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md)
  (cites [12]-[14], 14 total, extending "Paint blisters from a tarp left on
  too long"), cross-linked on [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [110],
  112 total) and briefly touched on
  [boats/whisper.md](/boats/whisper.md) (no new citation, existing cite
  [80] summary broadened).

  The **"Amelie"** thread ran the fuller course: **Emile Thimot**'s design
  question (opened batch 241) turned out to be a typo for bateau.com's
  **"Adelie,"** which Nels — who had discussed Adelie before — critiqued for
  her backrest-less cockpit and CGI-only, not-yet-built status, judging his
  own Bolger Micro superior and renewing his standing call: **"We need
  someone to come forward and enlarge the Paradox by 20%!"** That call
  prompted **Jack Gardiner** to float a next, larger "ultimate cruiser" of
  his own — undecided between 16 ft and 19 ft, still weighing steel,
  consistent with his standing steel-construction interest — which drew a
  reply from new below-threshold correspondent **Chris Ostlind**
  (`wedgesail`) endorsing the enlargement idea (carrying, in his view,
  Matt Layden's own "apparent, tacit blessing") while arguing against steel
  specifically at larger sizes, on weight-distribution and stability
  grounds. Folded onto
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md)
  (cites [52]-[55], 55 total, extending "A 2004 revisiting: Paradox against
  Sven Yrvind's 'Sofia'"; citation [50]'s stale "no reply survives" note
  corrected now that replies exist), cross-linked on
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [112], 112 total),

## 2026-07-21

* **Update (batch page_2401–page_2410, msgnum 2429-2438, continuing directly
  from batch 240's 2428; no new gaps)**: 29 October – 5 November 2004. No new
  files, 5 extended. The batch's throughline continued the "Paradoxes
  afloat...warped micro" chine-runners-on-a-Micro thread from the end of
  batch 240: **Paul** (`kayaker37`) asked **Jason Stancil** (`smithriverranger`,
  OPEN 3000-review identity-reconcile item, standing below-threshold) how he
  had concluded chine runners wouldn't work on his own Bolger Micro, beyond
  the previously-recorded basement-flood-warped-keel story. Jason answered
  with the underlying engineering reasoning: a Micro's flatter, less-rockered
  bottom would need chine runners long enough to cause unacceptable drag, and
  he had no way to predict where the resulting center of lateral resistance
  would fall. **Nels** separately answered Paul's beachability doubt about
  the Micro, pointing to a "long Micro" photo at the `microcruising` group
  showing a Bolger Long Micro, a regular Micro, and an Oldshoe beached side
  by side — Micros beach better than their 18 in draft suggests, since the
  first foot or so of the bow rises clear of the water. A related design
  aside came from new below-threshold correspondent **Emile Thimot**, who
  asked the list's opinion of bateau.com's "Amelie," a near-cousin
  Paradox-like design without chine runners (no reply survives). Folded onto
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md)
  (cites [48]-[51], 51 total, extending "A 2004 revisiting: Paradox against
  Sven Yrvind's 'Sofia'"), cross-linked on
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [109], 109 total).

  The "Paradoxes afloat" census thread got a further tail: **Dave Blow**
  (`smallshelters`/`smallshelters1954`, standing below-threshold identity)
  replied quoting Derek Clark's original census list and Al Law's reply,
  signing "Dave B./Colorado USA" and adding himself as a ninth builder/
  location missing from both prior lists. Folded onto
  [operations/southern-california-and-catalina-crossing.md](/operations/southern-california-and-catalina-crossing.md)
  (cite [11], 11 total). Separately, within the ongoing building-progress
  thread, **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`, STRENGTHENED promote-later
  candidate, not promoted per standing instruction) reported his transom and
  stem fitted at **4.21 m** and named his boat **"So-So,"** then, on his
  wife's suggestion, **"Enuf"** instead; Dave Blow answered with a
  flu-delayed but real progress update (starboard window sealed, sliding-hatch
  bottom guide fitted). Folded onto
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (cites
  [79]-[80], 80 total, extending "Why Paradox resists easy enlargement").

  A third thread, "Paint Blisters ???": **David Beard** reported small
  blisters under Interlux Brightsides topside paint wherever a tarp had
  touched "Whisper" during a six-week cover, and **William Serjeant**
  (posting this time as `william@`/`williamserjeant`, the Yahoo-profile name
  he had earlier announced switching to from `barnacletoo` — same standing
  person, no new identity) gave the residual-moisture-expansion explanation.
  Folded onto [construction/topside-painting.md](/construction/topside-painting.md)
  (cites [10]-[11], 11 total, new "Paint blisters from a tarp left on too
  long" section), narrate-once-cross-linked on
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cite [234], 234 total) and
  [boats/whisper.md](/boats/whisper.md) (cite [80], 80 total).

## 2026-07-20

* **Update (batch page_2391–page_2400, msgnum 2419-2428, continuing directly
  from batch 239's 2418; no new gaps)**: 26–29 October 2004. No new files, 4
  extended. The batch's throughline was **Jack Gardiner**'s Paradox "Jill"
  continuing to prove herself: **Paul** (`kayaker37`) posted photos from his
  visit the previous month, prompting Jack's full account of a deliberate
  **windward performance trial** with the friend who helped build the
  boat — good speed and pointing on the way out, holding their own racing
  other small sailboats home, until the tide's turn brought the expected fall
  weather shift and a freshening, circling wind; unable to beat back into the
  cove, Jack beached Jill and walked/paddled her home, drawing the plain
  lesson "check the tides." He had by then fitted a 30 lb-thrust electric
  trolling motor, conceded he would eventually build the yuloh he had never
  gotten to, and planned a topping lift like Glen Maxwell's. **Dave Bolduc**,
  seeing Jack's photos, recalled the Bolducs' own 1994 Bimini crossing with
  Matt Layden in similarly "mighty windy" conditions, linking two further
  microcruising.com photos from that day. All folded onto
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites [119]-[121], 121
  total) and [people/bolduc/matt-layden-authority.md](/people/bolduc/matt-layden-authority.md)
  (cite [18], 18 total).

  The batch's second thread was **Derek Clark**'s (`dclark52001`, STRENGTHENED
  promote-later candidate, not promoted per standing instruction) new
  "Paradoxes afloat" census, asking the list how many Paradoxes were then
  afloat and by whom — listing eight of his own (Matt Layden, Glen Maxwell's
  Zoë, Don Elliot, David's Whisper, Jack's boat, an unnamed New Zealand
  "Petite Lady," an unnamed Australian "Zero," and Al Law's Little Jim) and
  asking how many more were under construction. **Alastair** answered with
  three further named boats from his own notes (DC's "Swash," Ronald de Hon's
  "RHXII" in Amsterdam, Chris Becker's "Walden" in Australia) and a fuller
  builders'-in-progress list (Jeff Forrest, Paul/`kayaker37`, Jack Gardiner,
  Steve of Kansas City, Kjell Karlsson, and Derek Clark himself in
  Wolverhampton, UK) — extending the archive's running Paradox census (four
  boats in May 2002, six by December 2003) to its fullest count yet. Folded
  onto [operations/southern-california-and-catalina-crossing.md](/operations/southern-california-and-catalina-crossing.md)
  (cites [9]-[10], 10 total, new "Census: eight boats named, and a fuller
  builders' list (October 2004)" section).

  A third, smaller thread continued the "Paradox and Sofia" comparison
  (opened batch 236): **Glen Maxwell** asked whether Paul's (`kayaker37`)
  Sofia-model rendering came from real plans or sketches; Paul explained no
  formal Sofia plans exist (designer Rolf Eliasson drew only the hull and
  deck), his model came from a contact's 2D CAD hull files, and he mocked up
  the rudder/centerboard himself, borrowing an already-built stretched
  Paradox cabin for the model. He went on to clarify he is not currently
  building a Paradox — having seen Jack's boat in person and judged it too
  small (or himself too large), and finding Sofia too difficult — and was
  instead drawing up a Bolger Birdwatcher and Micro for comparison, noting
  Micro's 18 in draft ruled out beachability and that **someone else had once
  proposed chine runners on a Micro** but stuck to the stock plans. That
  someone else, **Jason Stancil** (`smithriverranger`, OPEN 3000-review
  identity-reconcile item, standing below-threshold), confirmed the story —
  abandoning the idea after a basement flood warped his keel, instead
  building an off-center centerboard case and a 425 lb lead-shot-and-epoxy
  ballast pour under the sole, with the rudder moved onto a skeg with an
  enlarged end-plate. Folded onto
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md)
  (cites [44]-[47], 47 total, new material extending "A 2004 revisiting:
  Paradox against Sven Yrvind's 'Sofia'"). No new person pages, no prunings,
  narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout. Standing-open-items and
  attribution-trap checks clean (confirmed only one Derek Clark exists,
  despite appearing both as the census-opener and among Alastair's named
  builders); msgnum run 2419-2428 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive
  from batch 239's 2418, no new gap (standing gaps 2122, 2190, 2229, 2385
  unaffected). See `.okf-progress/batches.log` for the full per-batch
  density record.

## 2026-07-19

* **Update (batch page_2381–page_2390, msgnum 2409-2418, continuing directly
  from batch 238's 2408; no new gaps)**: 22–26 October 2004. No new files, 6
  extended. The batch closed out the "Chine runner efficiency." thread with
  **Mat** (`mat_man22`) posting direct Duckworks Magazine links to Jeff
  Gilbert's "Gumboots" catamaran and his design column, a brief last-word
  compliment from new below-threshold correspondent **"boblq"** (his second
  appearance), and **Nels**'s further explanation of *why* chine runners work
  better as boat speed rises — a heeled hull's downward-deflected water gets
  pushed aft on meeting the runner, adding forward thrust rather than pure
  drag — plus a shallow-draft safety case for having no keel at all: a
  chine-runner boat caught off a lee shore can slide sideways rather than
  trip or get holed on a reef, in principle riding out a bad situation where a
  deeper-draft boat would be bashed apart offshore. All folded onto
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md) (cites
  [47]-[49], 49 total, closing "A dory-hulled catamaran design using chine
  runners" and adding a new "Why speed itself reduces leeway, and a
  shallow-draft safety case for no keel at all" section), with
  narrate-once-cross-link summaries added to
  [people/mat-man22.md](/people/mat-man22.md) (cite [14], 14 total) and
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [107], 108 total).

  The batch's other centerpiece was **Derek Clark**'s (`dclark52001`,
  STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate, not promoted per standing
  instruction) fullest build-progress post yet — having struggled with the
  stem's shape, he set it aside and assembled bulkheads 2, 3, and 4 into a
  square box out of sequence, confirming squareness within 2 mm on the
  diagonals, then completed bulkhead 1 the same way, working in a garage
  opening onto a 30 ft by 12 ft concrete area roomy enough to turn the hull
  alone at that stage. **Nels** answered with the archive's fullest
  **Spanish-windlass** technique for pulling a hull's sides together (a
  twisted rope loop, temporary uprights, and scab blocks at the bow); Derek
  had already tried one on bulkhead 2 and warned a slipping windlass's dowel
  can whip round fast enough to strike a hand or head, recommending ratchet
  straps instead. **Alastair** corroborated the two-person hull-turning
  finding from his own build, adding that he managed his own bottomed hull
  with roller skates and "some luck," and that the structure only becomes
  rigid once the stem is fitted. Derek also confirmed a decision to skip
  water tanks entirely (using the space for storage instead) and butt-joint
  the bottom panel in 18 mm plywood under 45-degree biaxial cloth. **Kjell
  Karlsson** and Derek closed with light banter (an office chair, Kjell's
  birdsong CDs, credit to Alastair and Don Elliott for helping Derek
  understand the drawings). All folded onto
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md)
  (cites [46]-[50], 50 total, new "Assembling bulkheads out of sequence, and
  squaring the resulting box" and "Pulling a hull's sides together with a
  Spanish windlass" sections, plus an addendum to "Turning the hull over"),
  with a narrate-once-cross-link bullet added to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [108], 108 total), the
  water-tanks-as-storage/bottom-butt-joint decision folded onto
  [construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md)
  (cite [20], 20 total), and a brief personal-detail bullet added to
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [49], 49
  total). No new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Standing-open-items and attribution-trap checks
  clean; msgnum run 2409-2418 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive from
  batch 238's 2408, no new gap (standing gaps 2122, 2190, 2229, 2385
  unaffected). See `.okf-progress/batches.log` for the full per-batch
  density record.

## 2026-07-18

* **Update (batch page_2371–page_2380, msgnum 2399-2408, continuing directly
  from batch 237's 2398; no new gaps)**: 20–22 October 2004. No new files, 3
  extended. The batch closed out the "Chine runner efficiency." thread
  (Colin/`sae140`'s runner-width question, opened batch 237) with **Dave
  Bolduc**'s fullest account on record of how the runners' size and shape
  changed over time — small and square on Swamp Thing, growing larger and
  more triangular through years of Matt Layden's model and full-size
  testing, and a later controlled trial in which Matt sailed months
  comparing one enlarged Paradox chine runner against the stock one before
  grinding it back down, having found the added drag and draft did not
  justify the change — and **Nels**'s fullest explanation on record of why
  chine runners suit a sharpie hull far better than a dory hull: a box hull's
  own inherent resistance to sideways slippage (traced to Phil Bolger's
  small box-shaped boats), and Paradox's deep, heavily rockered midships
  section letting her immerse far deeper before swamping than a flare-sided
  dory could risk. Nels illustrated the point with a **Bolger box-hull-versus-
  dory-hull design comparison** — Bolger's redesigned St. Pierre Dory against
  his box-hulled "Jesse Cooper" — judging a fin keel the dory hull's own best
  solution (citing Annie Hill and Jim Sale's "Badger"), which drew a thank-you
  from Dave Bolduc. A further tangent, prompted by **Mat** (`mat_man22`),
  surfaced designer **Jeff Gilbert**'s attempt at fitting chine runners to a
  dory-sided hull of his "Gumboots" catamaran ("Gumboot+Mono"), with Nels
  judging it a graphic illustration of the same sharpie-versus-dory principle
  and pointing out Gilbert's smaller "Hot Chili" camping-catamaran design as
  an aside. A new below-threshold correspondent, **"boblq"** (`boblq@`, no
  full name recorded), asked the single clarifying question that prompted
  Nels's full explanation. All folded onto
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md) (cites
  [37]-[46], 46 total, extending "Runner width, and no published
  width-versus-leeway trial" and adding two new sections, "A Bolger
  box-hull-versus-dory-hull design comparison" and "A dory-hulled catamaran
  design using chine runners"), with narrate-once-cross-link summaries added
  to [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (cite [92], 92 total),
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [102]-[106], 106 total), and
  [people/mat-man22.md](/people/mat-man22.md) (cites [12]-[13], 13 total). No
  new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. Standing-open-items and attribution-trap checks clean; msgnum
  run 2399-2408 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive from batch 237's
  2398, no new gap (standing gaps 2122, 2190, 2229, 2385 unaffected). See
  `.okf-progress/batches.log` for the full per-batch density record.

## 2026-07-17

* **Update (batch page_2361–page_2370, msgnum 2389-2398, continuing directly
  from batch 236's 2388; no new gaps)**: 18–20 October 2004. No new files, 4
  extended. The batch continued the "Epoxy & Fibreglass - Retry" thread
  (Dave Bolduc's hatch-glassing photos, opened batch 236) with **Nels**'s
  detailed technique follow-up — cloth weight, bare-wood sanding, a tacky
  sealer coat (the cedar-strip-canoe bonding technique), and whether 3M-77
  spray adhesive eases cloth handling — and **Dave Bolduc**'s full
  step-by-step answer: strip to bare wood, roll on a sealer coat, fill with
  phenolic microballoons, sand smooth, tack on 4 oz fiberglass with 3M-77
  (flat sides, then bias-cut strips for the curved "ribs," then the flat
  top), roll epoxy with a bubble roller and squeegee to clear trapped air,
  then a two-stage fill-and-sand finish — plus a further round of Swamp
  Thing core-void-repair photos. Folded onto
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
  (cites [58]-[59], [63], 63 total, extending "Why fiberglass the boat at
  all"). **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`, STRENGTHENED promote-later
  candidate, standing since batch 216, not promoted per standing
  instruction) opened a new UK epoxy-supplier question
  (`epoxy-resins.co.uk`, cheaper resin with 16 oz biaxial bottom/7 oz woven
  side-deck cloth recommendations), answered by **Alastair** confirming both
  `epoxy-resins.co.uk` and SP Systems (Isle of Wight) as trouble-free UK
  suppliers, in the same exchange giving a two-season "Little Jim" condition
  report (only two small bottom-repair spots from groundings) and confirming
  he had built to the plans' full "glass all over" scheme rather than any
  partial-coverage shortcut — folded onto the same file (cites [60]-[62]),
  with a narrate-once-cross-link summary added to
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cite [225], 225 total). A new
  below-threshold correspondent, **Colin** (`sae140`), considering fitting
  chine runners to a St Pierre dory, asked whether any documented trial
  quantifies runner width against leeway efficiency, surprised how much
  smaller/"stubbier" the photographed CR1/CR2 runners on "Zoë" looked than
  the 6+ in width he had assumed necessary — folded onto
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md) as a new
  "Runner width, and no published width-versus-leeway trial" section (cite
  [36], 36 total); no width-versus-efficiency trial is recorded. A second
  new below-threshold correspondent, **Steve** (`steve_williams45`), asked
  the standing plans FAQ, offering a Bolger "Micro" plans trade; **Dave

  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) as a new "A
  plans-swap offer (October 2004)" note (cites [77]-[78], 78 total). No new
  person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout.

## 2026-07-16

* **Update (batch page_2351–page_2360, msgnum 2378-2384/2386-2388, continuing
  directly from batch 235's 2377; new standing gap at 2385)**: 17–18 October
  2004. No new files, 2 extended. The batch centered on the "Bottom
  protection" and "Epoxy & Fibreglass" threads carried over from batch 235,
  drawing in two new below-threshold correspondents — **Tom Hathaway**
  (`tomhathaway1949`, El Cajon, CA) and **John Steadham** (`john_steadham`).
  On [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
  (cites [48]-[57], 57 total): John Steadham's naive "why cover the whole
  boat in glass?" question drew **Dave Bolduc**'s fullest published
  cost-and-damage-history case for full epoxy/fiberglass encapsulation —
  precise Kevlar and epoxy costings (Kevlar seconds at $1/sq ft, Raka 127
  kits at $129-$239), and the ~$400/boat decision justified by **Swamp
  Thing sinking at the dock** when a rock pierced her painted-only bottom,
  and both boats' plywood-checking, yearly crack-filling, and reef-holing
  history while unglassed — folded as a new "Why fiberglass the boat at
  all" section, with a narrate-once-cross-link summary added to
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (cite [91], 91 total).
  Dave also confirmed 3M-77 spray adhesive does not weaken the epoxy bond
  and gave his bias-cut Kevlar-tacking technique, prompting Tom Hathaway's
  closing endorsement (Kevlar that can stop a bullet should stop a coral
  reef). The existing "Bottom protection" UHMW/nylon thread continued: Tom
  Hathaway asked what UHMW thickness a regularly-beached boat would need;
  Larry (`LJGeib`, the standing "second Larry" identity) gave a hard price
  point ($220 for a 24x48x1/2 in sheet, Portland OR); Tom named a second
  UHMW source (Machinists Materials, Largo, FL) and weighed the cost
  against yearly bottom recoating or Kevlar. John Steadham separately
  proposed a **welded steel bottom plate with the chine runners welded
  directly on**, eliminating the water ballast tank for extra storage — a
  bottom-only variant of the standing whole-hull steel debate, folded onto

## 2026-07-15

* **Update (batch page_2341–page_2350, msgnum 2368-2377, continuing directly
  from batch 234's 2367, no new gap)**: 15–17 October 2004. No new files, 4
  extended. **Derek Clark closed the "Sheer strakes and Chine logs" thread**
  and, in the same run of messages, opened a new "Bottom protection"
  cost-versus-durability question. On
  [construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md](/construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md)
  (cite [11], 11 total), Derek accepted Nels's quality-wood-pays-for-itself
  conclusion, closing out the thread running since batch 233. On
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [48], 48
  total), Paul (`kayaker37`, standing below-threshold identity) sent a
  brief compliment on Kjell's "New pictures" update. The batch's centerpiece
  ran on [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
  (cites [41]-[47], 47 total): Dave Blow (`smallshelters1954`, standing
  below-threshold identity) confirmed his epoxy decision, detailing his
  strip-plank gluing/fastening method and a 300-400 lb starting ballast
  estimate; [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) answered Derek Clark's
  cloth-weight question in full — 4 oz glass on the topsides, several
  layers of 10 oz glass or woven roving on the bottom, Kevlar normally
  buried under fiberglass rather than left exposed — corroborated by Nels
  (plain E-glass and carbon-loaded epoxy already abrasion-resistant enough
  for a trailered, lake/river Paradox) and by Dave Bolduc's own home
  curb-abrasion test proving Kevlar's durability over plain glass, plus his
  bias-cut Kevlar/glass leading-edge lamination technique. A new **"Bottom
  protection"** thread then opened with Phil's (`topsail@`/`eliallegra`,
  standing below-threshold identity) UHMW/nylon bottom-skin idea (drawn
  from McKenzie River drift boats and Navy flight-deck padding), drawing
  Derek Clark's follow-up cost-driven question about aluminum or
  stainless-steel sheet as a cheaper alternative to further epoxy and
  glass (£600 spent so far, reluctant to spend another £400 on epoxy that
  then needs UV-protective paint), and David Beard's (`dlb@`/`buster38801`)
  corroborating reply that a friend's airboat uses the same UHMW material
  successfully, though it needs many screws to install — folded as a new
  subsection,
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#bottom-protection-uhmw-plastic-and-aluminum-or-stainless-steel-sheet-as-skin-alternatives](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#bottom-protection-uhmw-plastic-and-aluminum-or-stainless-steel-sheet-as-skin-alternatives),
  cross-linked to the existing whole-hull steel/aluminum debate at
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md).
  [construction/hull-planking.md](/construction/hull-planking.md)'s prior
  "unanswered on record" note on Derek Clark's UK-ply-grade follow-up (cite
  [32]) was updated to reflect Dave Bolduc's answer and cross-link the
  fuller exchange. One message this batch (page_2345, msgnum 2372) was a
  "Mail Delivery (failure)" auto-bounce with no substantive content — not
  cited. Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate)
  appeared twice this batch and remains below-threshold per standing
  instruction, not promoted inline.

## 2026-07-14

* **Update (batch page_2331–page_2340, msgnum 2358-2367, continuing directly
  from batch 233's 2357, no new gap)**: 12–15 October 2004. No new files, 9
  extended. **The list's fullest epoxy-versus-polyester debate to date.**
  Dave Blow (`smallshelters1954`, standing below-threshold identity)
  decided against polyester after his own research, wary of delamination,
  and ordered a West System catalog. Jack Gardiner answered emphatically
  for epoxy — 3 gallons into his own build, 12 oz biaxial cloth on the hull
  in place of the specified woven roving, two extra layers of 2 oz glass on
  the upper hull to stop checking, no nails or screws but the fittings —
  and warned specifically against polyester on a strip-planked deck, which
  needs epoxy's flex tolerance. [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) gave
  the fullest case: epoxy's superior waterproofing and bond strength over
  polyester; small-batch mixing/handling technique (test pieces first,
  exact ratios, bias-cut cloth on corners, discard any batch that gels
  early); and confirmed fiberglassing is not mandatory at all — Matt Layden
  painted his own boats for years, and Little Cruiser has sailed sound with
  no fiberglass sheathing whatsoever, though Little Cruiser and Swamp Thing
  are both now being fully glassed (4 oz topsides both boats; Little
  Cruiser's bottom getting 5 oz Kevlar under 10 oz glass). The same message
  confirmed **Matt Layden and the original Paradox survived Hurricanes
  Frances and Ivan (2004)** — both storms' eyes passed directly over Matt's
  reinforced Jensen Beach, FL home with little damage, and the boat was
  hauled out ahead of time, only a few branches falling on her — folded
  onto [boats/original-paradox.md](/boats/original-paradox.md#surviving-hurricanes-frances-and-ivan-2004)
  (cite [44], 44 total) — and gave a same-message Little
  Cruiser/Swamp Thing progress update (about to glass Little Cruiser's
  hull; still stripping Swamp Thing's), folded onto
  [matt-layden/little-cruiser.md](/matt-layden/little-cruiser.md#a-mid-october-2004-progress-update)
  (cite [27], 27 total) and
  [matt-layden/swamp-thing.md](/matt-layden/swamp-thing.md#from-dave-gatan-to-the-bolducs-the-2004-rescue-and-20052008-rebuild)
  (cite [15], 15 total) as a small refinement of the existing 2004-2008
  Swamp Thing rebuild timeline (the two refits ran in parallel, not fully
  sequentially). The core debate is narrated at
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#a-builders-own-polyester-to-epoxy-conversion-and-the-case-against-poly-on-a-strip-planked-deck)
  (cites [37]-[40], 40 total), cross-linked on
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [118], 118
  total; one pre-existing gap at [53] predating this batch, unrelated) and
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (summary bullet, no new
  citation). **Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, STRENGTHENED promote-later
  candidate, appeared three times this batch, not promoted per standing
  instruction)** opened the UK side of the thread: asked the best
  bottom-abrasion cloth weight, whether [Little Jim](/boats/little-jim.md)'s
  chine runners might protect the topsides from canal-wall abrasion closely
  enough to skip glassing the sides (folded as an open question onto
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#sven-yrvinds-seven-reasons),
  cite [35], 35 total), and whether an unreinforced epoxy coat alone would
  serve as waterproofing before painting. Dave Bolduc answered with the
  plans' own laminating schedule (3 layers of 24 oz woven roving on the
  bottom, 6-7.5 oz E-glass on the topsides) and his own heavier
  Little-Cruiser/Swamp-Thing layups, folded onto
  [construction/hull-planking.md](/construction/hull-planking.md#the-plans-bottom-and-topside-glass-weights-explained-to-a-uk-builder)
  (cites [30]-[33], 33 total); Derek's UK-specific follow-up (Far Eastern
  exterior-grade ply, not fir; whether a lighter topside cloth would still
  need Dave's fir-checking fix; Kevlar layer order) remains **unanswered on
  record** as of this batch. **Dave Blow's build continued** in parallel:
  a further compliment from new below-threshold correspondent Jason
