## 2026-07-13

* **Update (batch page_2321–page_2330, msgnum 2348-2357, continuing directly
  from batch 232's 2347, no new gap)**: 7–12 October 2004. No new files, 9
  extended. **A "Sheer strakes and Chine logs" thread** — Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`) asked what these parts actually do structurally, whether
  cheap epoxied pine could substitute for costly Douglas Fir, and whether
  ripped-and-laminated plywood strips would work instead. [Nels](/people/nels.md)
  answered across three replies: chine logs and sheer strakes carry real
  structural load (a well-built "box boat," glassed over, acts as an
  incredibly strong box beam) as well as fastener purchase; plywood is a poor
  substitute since ripping it into strips leaves alternating layers'
  cross-grain "useless," recommending instead laminated solid-wood strips;
  soft/flexible species (spruce, fir, cedar) are preferred per the plans'
  guidance, with reasonably straight-grained, tight-knotted local fir usable
  if oversized; relayed Matt Layden's species-to-avoid list (oak, teak,
  maple, birch, poplar); and reaffirmed that quality wood is the better
  economy over a 15-year hull life. Derek separately mentioned watching Al
  Law (Alastair) sailing "Little Jim" in person, "inspiring." Folded into
  [construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md](/construction/chine-log-vs-taped-seam.md#what-sheer-strakes-and-chine-logs-actually-do-and-why-plywood-is-a-poor-substitute)
  (cites [6]-[10], 10 total), cross-linked on
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [99]-[101], 101 total) and
  [boats/little-jim.md](/boats/little-jim.md#an-outside-sighting-october-2004)
  (cite [95], 95 total). **Derek Clark's unanswered "Boom" question** — asked
  whether revised boom dimensions exist for the Mark II sail, since the
  plans state boom taper is critical to reefing/furling — folded into
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#quantifying-the-new-sailplans-effect-on-boom-furling-geometry)
  (cite [154], 154 total). **Dave Blow's (`smallshelters1954`) build
  continued**, drawing a duplicate-quoted compliment from a new
  below-threshold correspondent, "Andy" (`servo_bot`), and posting a further
  photo update captioned as showing the boat's strip-planked deck. Folded
  into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#why-paradox-resists-easy-enlargement)
  (cites [73]-[74], 74 total). **"Jeff UK" reported a year lost to weather
  and scaffolding** on his own plywood build — a neighbor's roofing
  scaffolding blocked his basement door for months longer than planned, and
  the rain that followed once it came down left him only two panels and
  eight frames cut before the weather turned too cold for epoxy, deferring
  the build to the next year. Folded into
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#jeff-uks-own-plywood-build-a-year-lost-to-weather-and-scaffolding)
  (cite [117], 117 total). **The multi-batch chine-runner-curve question was
  resolved**: Kjell Karlsson (`kjellkkk`) reported simply cutting his BRIS
  Paradox's bronze bottom straight, excluding the drawn curvature entirely,
  with no apparent ill effect — the clearest real-world resolution on record
  of the September 2004 "chinerunners" debate. The same message also showed
  the aft baffle recut after mounting too steep, the tiller confirmed
  fiberglass/epoxy, and a stainless-steel stem tube fitted in place of the
  plans' fastening loop (Sven Yrvind's own suggestion), since a loop would
  not grip well in the Divinycell core. Folded into
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#the-chine-runner-curve-cosmetic-fabrication-choice-or-hydrodynamic-feature)
  (cite [34], 34 total) and
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-stainless-steel-stem-tube-in-place-of-a-fastening-loop)
  (cite [65], 65 total), cross-linked on
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [46], 46
  total). No new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout; Derek Clark's strengthened promote-later status
  reconfirmed, not promoted inline despite two appearances this batch (chine
  logs thread, boom question). Timeline reached 7-12 October 2004.

## 2026-07-12

* **Update (batch page_2311–page_2320, msgnum 2338-2347, continuing directly
  from batch 231's 2337, no new gap)**: 3–6 October 2004. No new files, 5
  extended. **Dave Blow's (`smallshelters1954`) build continued** on two
  fronts: he settled his strip-planking question by planking the bottom's
  **inside** face (roughly bulkhead #2 to #4) rather than the outside, hit a
  brief delay after his saw struck a buried nail, then posted further project
  photos that drew both a one-off compliment from a new below-threshold
  correspondent, Jason Stancil (`smithriverranger`), and a fastener question
  from [Nels](/people/nels.md); Dave clarified his actual fastening sequence —
  ring nails and "Gold" deck screws, with deck screws eventually running
  through the plywood into internal solid-wood strips once the hull is turned
  over, planking strips laid athwartship so they need not bend. Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#why-paradox-resists-easy-enlargement)
  (cites [66]-[72], 72 total), cross-linked on
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [97]-[98], 98 total). **A Swamp
  Thing photo/plans request from Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`) drew a reply
  from Dave Bolduc revealing the rescued boat's rebuild had **already begun**
  in early October 2004 — well ahead of the "next summer" timeline he had
  given in his original mid-September request — after finding rot in the
  hull, deck, stem, and stern; he had cut away the bad wood, removed the
  corroded original skeg, and made new rudder and oar blades. Folded into
  [matt-layden/swamp-thing.md](/matt-layden/swamp-thing.md) (cites [13]-[14],
  14 total). **A workshop-safety and epoxy-fume-mask thread**: the same
  message carrying Derek Clark's Swamp Thing request also reported a
  stem-fitting setback and a belt-sander finger injury, plus a request for a
  UK-available epoxy-fume mask; [Alastair](/people/alastair.md) answered with
  a Buck & Hickman ABEK-cartridge filter mask recommendation and restated his
  standing "leave bevels oversize until trial assembly" stem advice, while
  Dave Bolduc separately recounted his own belt-sander thumb injury and his
  own epoxy-mask practice. Folded into
  [construction/bottom-painting.md](/construction/bottom-painting.md#stripping-old-bottom-paint-sanding-and-respirator-advice)
  (cites [34]-[36], 36 total), cross-linked on
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cite [224], 224 total). No new
  person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout;
  Derek Clark's strengthened promote-later status reconfirmed, not promoted
  inline despite two appearances this batch. Timeline reached 3-6 October
  2004. Standing-open-items check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie
  Fossel, Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark/dclark52001 (appeared twice, see
  above), Fredrik, Jake, Lugger #2, kayaker37/Paul, paull01/Paul-Seattle,
  Paul V., Steve/Kansas City, Howard Welch, Jason Stancil (new
  below-threshold correspondent this batch, see above), John Hall,
  Todd/ktsrfer, Alf Sawatzky, Shane Hersey, Bengt Genell, Bill/wam_53,
  Craig/crag8, Ron/churo65, Ron/rondune508, Phil/eliallegra(topsail@),
  Shorty/shortypen, LJGeib/second-Larry, Ken Preston/kenpreston46,

## 2026-07-11

* **Update (batch page_2301–page_2310, msgnum 2328-2337, continuing directly
  from batch 230's 2327, no new gap)**: 24 September – 2 October 2004. No new
  files, 5 extended. **A follow-up to the Swamp Thing rescue**: a new
  below-threshold correspondent, Dan (`dstgean`), tried to send Dave Bolduc a
  scanned high-resolution copy of the Swamp Thing photo from Ida Little's
  *Beachcruising and Coastal Camping* but was blocked by his e-mail
  provider's attachment-size limit; before he could resend a smaller file,
  Dave confirmed another member had already supplied a usable copy, and
  linked two photos of the boat at his own house. Folded into
  [matt-layden/swamp-thing.md](/matt-layden/swamp-thing.md) (cite [11]-[12],
  12 total). **Dave Blow's (`smallshelters1954`) build continued** with
  routine project-photo updates (one URL correction) and confirmation he
  will trailer-sail rather than live aboard, following the prior batch's
  painted/unglassed-bottom approach; [Kjell
  Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) — building the far costlier BRIS
  composite Paradox — remarked it would be interesting to compare the two
  boats' functional differences later, since his own build was pursuing
  "the opposite" approach. Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#why-paradox-resists-easy-enlargement)
  (cites [62]-[65], 65 total), cross-linked on
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [45], 45
  total). **A "Paradox and Sofia" thread** reopened the 2002
  Paradox-vs-Sven-Yrvind-boats comparison: kayaker37/Paul, weighing which
  design to build next, posted side-by-side photos of Paradox against Rolf
  Eliasson and Christer Lundberg's "Sofia" (evolved from Yrvind's Bris
  boats); Dave Blow replied describing his own interests and his deck
  already half strip-planked; Paul answered admiring Dave's from-materials-on-hand
  approach; [Nels](/people/nels.md) suggested simply scaling up the existing
  metric Paradox plans rather than building an unfamiliar design, noting a
  builder could recoup costs by selling the enlarged plan set. Folded into a
  new "A 2004 revisiting: Paradox against Sven Yrvind's 'Sofia'" section on
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#a-2004-revisiting-paradox-against-sven-yrvinds-sofia)
  (cites [40]-[43], 43 total), cross-linked on
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [96], 96 total). No new person
  pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout; Derek
  Clark did not appear this batch, strengthened promote-later status
  unchanged.

## 2026-07-10

* **Update (batch page_2291–page_2300, msgnum 2318-2327, continuing directly
  from batch 229's 2317, no new gap)**: 19-23 September 2004. No new files,
  5 extended. **Jack Gardiner's "Jill" sailed again**, a week after her
  launch: a forecast 10-15 kt breeze built to an actual 30-40 mph once clear
  of the launch cove; a fouled reefing drum forced a brief beach stop before
  Jack pressed on half-reefed at about 6 mph on a broad reach to meet Paul
  (`kayaker37`) as arranged — Paul, seeing the real conditions firsthand,
  declined to try the boat himself. The day's real drama came ashore:
  recovering the trailer, the tow vehicle's tires sank into wet sand, and
  Jack freed it by wedging the boat's own rudder blade under the deepest-sunk
  tire for traction — the blade held undamaged, "it saved me!!" — while a
  passing tugboat deckhand recognized the chine runners on sight as the same
  "rolling blades" fitted to some tugs. The day's official forecast, checked
  afterward, had upgraded to a small craft advisory (20-25 kt, gusts to 30).
  Paul's own account confirmed the conditions and added a fresh safety note:
  a gust can jump the hull sideways enough, in even shallow water alongside
  a heeling Paradox, to crush a wading crew member's ankle against a chine
  runner. Folded into the existing "Launch: 'Jill'" section on
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill) (cites
  [116]-[117], 117 total, one pre-existing gap at [53] predating this batch);
  per batch 227's standing flag, still no dedicated `boats/` page created for
  "Jill," deferred to a future holistic review. **A materials/resin debate**
  prompted by Dave Blow's (`smallshelters1954`) early project photos ran
  across six of the ten messages: Mat (`mat_man22`) judged 3/8 in plywood
  adequate for the sides (citing Glen-L's 21 ft "Fancy Free" sharpie) against
  a stock 1/2 in unglassed bottom (or 9 oz. cloth for extra insurance),
  recommending epoxy over polyester; Dave weighed strip-planking the bottom
  but flagged a prior bad epoxy reaction (facial swelling, a rash) from an
  ultralight-airplane build, asking about non-allergenic formulations;
  [Nels](/people/nels.md) warned against epoxy given a developed sensitivity,
  suggested waterproof glue/ring nails with the glassing hired out or
  polyester as a fallback, and floated a tongue-and-groove hardwood floor
  laminated with a thin plywood skin as a strip-planking alternative; Dave
  explained his cost-driven table-saw-strip approach and reported no reaction
  at all to polyester on a prior Bolger Brick build; Nels questioned whether
  stripping saves real money once labor and materials are counted; Mat closed
  with George Buehler's plywood-skinned-2x-lumber method and, for a
  trailer-sailed boat, recommended paint over fiberglass entirely. Folded
  into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md#why-paradox-resists-easy-enlargement)
  (cites [56]-[61], 61 total), cross-linked with new bullets on
  [people/mat-man22.md](/people/mat-man22.md) (cites [10]-[11], 11 total) and
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [94]-[95], 95 total). **A new
  below-threshold correspondent, Kellan (`dagon_gsl`)**, asked the list
  whether anyone had heard from Glen Maxwell during the 2004 hurricane
  season — Glen lived in the storms' path, having made it through the first
  (missing the group's Lake Powell messabout) but gone quiet since Hurricane
  Ivan. Glen answered the next day: alive and well, delayed by storm cleanup
  and overseeing barn-building contractors. Folded into a new "A welfare
  check during the 2004 hurricane season" section on
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#a-welfare-check-during-the-2004-hurricane-season)
  (cites [81]-[82], 82 total). No new person pages, no prunings,
  narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout; Derek Clark did not appear
  this batch, strengthened promote-later status unchanged.

## 2026-07-09

* **Update (batch page_2281–page_2290, msgnum 2308-2317, continuing directly
  from batch 228's 2307, no new gap)**: 13-19 September 2004. No new files,
  8 extended. The first two messages closed out Jack Gardiner's "hand
  operated outboard" mystery from batch 228: Paul's guess that the pictured
  object was a yuloh was wrong — it was a genuine ratchet-drive propeller
  unit (small gearbox and ratchet clamped to an outboard mount, worked by a
  lifting/lowering handle, turning the prop on both strokes), a joke gift
  from a friend who also left behind a military-surplus 40 hp Evinrude for
  Jack's Navy CRRC; Jack confirmed he had not yet started his own yuloh. This
  correction is folded into
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill) (cites
  [114]-[115], 115 total, one pre-existing gap at [53] predating this
  batch), replacing the prior batch's inline text that had (per the source
  as read at the time) recorded Paul's guess as settled fact. **Alastair
  contributed a dense trio**: replying to Jack's own tenderness report, he
  recalled "Little Jim" once felt the same way and settled it empirically
  with a mast-lean-out heel test (photographed under "Stability & Balance"
  on his build website), confirming her standing ~20-degree close-hauled
  heel — folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#real-world-under-ballasted-handling)
  (cite [93], 93 total) alongside the existing Glen Maxwell/Jack Gardiner
  ballast data points, and cross-linked from
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md#sailing-performance-and-weight)
  (cite [221], 223 total); a **forward-weight-shift tacking technique** —
  duck into the cabin just before putting the helm over to reliably complete
  a stubborn tack — with a companion failure case sculling up a narrow
  river; and the **archive's fullest quantified tacking-angle GPS trial**
  (19 tacks, sail reefed ~50%, average 130°, range 98°-150° by chop, heel
  steady at 20°). Both new to
  [operations/pointing-and-tacking-angles.md](/operations/pointing-and-tacking-angles.md)
  (cites [3]-[4], 4 total; frontmatter description broadened), cross-linked
  from people/alastair.md (cites [222]-[223]). **Dave Bolduc** asked for a
  high-resolution scan of the Ida Little book's Swamp Thing photo, now that
  the boat was in his back yard with rot in both bottom and deck,
  restoration deferred to the next summer since he was mid-way through
  covering Little Cruiser in fiberglass — folded into
  [matt-layden/swamp-thing.md](/matt-layden/swamp-thing.md#from-dave-gatan-to-the-bolducs-the-2004-rescue-and-20052008-rebuild)
  (cite [10], 10 total) and
  [matt-layden/little-cruiser.md](/matt-layden/little-cruiser.md#construction-scantlings-and-the-2005-epoxyfiberglass-sheathing-refit)
  (cite [26], 26 total). **Dave Blow (`smallshelters`) and Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`)**, both standing below-threshold correspondents, traded
  parallel same-day "bulkheads made/glued" build-progress updates (Derek
  prompted by seeing Dave's post); Dave separately opened a project-photo
  website, and Derek separately asked, unanswered on record, whether leading
  the mainsheet in over the top of the rear deck would help prevent
  tangling with a fitted outboard — recorded on
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (cites
  [52]-[55], 55 total) with Derek's mainsheet question also cross-linked
  into
  [design/rig.md](/design/rig.md#mainsheet-fouling-on-the-rudder-and-rear-deck-with-an-outboard-fitted)
  (cite [153], 155 total counting inline body references). No new person
  pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout. Derek
  Clark's strengthened promote-later status is reconfirmed, not upgraded
  (per standing instruction, not promoted inline). Standing-open-items
  check: all standing below-threshold identities checked, none newly
  promoted this batch. Attribution-trap check: Jack Gardiner's From: header
  read "John Gardiner" on both his messages this batch (page_2281 msgnum
  2308, page_2282 msgnum 2309), each confirmed via matching X-Yahoo-Profile
  `tidybowlmann`/u=111173743 headers as the same established person, no new
  identity created; both messages carry client-clock header dates of
  31 Dec 2001/1 Jan 2002, confirmed as clock errors against the
  Received/list-relay headers showing the true date of 13 Sep 2004,
  consistent with a prior batch's similar finding; David Beard vs. David
  LeBlanc, DC vs. Derek Clark, two-distinct-UK-Jeffs, three-distinct-Larrys,
  two-distinct-Roberts, two-distinct-Rons watches all not triggered (no
  traffic from any of those identities this batch, aside from the

  2308-2309-2310-2311-2312-2313-2314-2315-2316-2317 confirmed distinct and

  no new gap (standing 2122, 2190, and 2229 gaps unaffected, not

  instructions exactly (6449/8134/2299/1800/3517/1538/1141/1237/1331/3314
  bytes for page_2281 through page_2290 respectively), no stale-read issues,

## 2026-07-08

* **Update (batch page_2271–page_2280, msgnum 2298-2307, continuing directly
  from batch 227's 2297, no new gap)**: 12-13 September 2004. No new files, 2
  extended. The entire batch is a single continuous thread — the direct
  continuation of Jack Gardiner's "Another Paradox hits the water" launch
  announcement (batch 227) — so, per narrate-once-cross-link, all ten
  messages were folded into the existing "Launch: 'Jill'" section on
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill), which has
  always been the canonical home for his whole build/launch narrative. Paul
  (`kayaker37`) got his viewing request answered ("Sure Paul"); Glen Maxwell
  added congratulations and asked for photos; Jack explained his only camera
  was an unreliable phone camera (having already dunked three proper cameras,
  with his wife vetoing a replacement), and that nobody had known in advance
  where he would launch, so no one was on hand with a real camera — a dead
  calm that day left him weighing his 1.5 hp/2.5 hp outboards, an electric
  trolling motor, or simply waiting for forecast wind. **Dave Bolduc gave his
  standing ballasting advice**: don't hesitate to load a Paradox all the way
  up to her full cruising displacement (9 in draft), since the boat "loves
  the weight" and keeping the chine runners submerged as deep as possible
  maximizes windward performance — folded as a new cross-link into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#real-world-under-ballasted-handling)
  (cites [91]-[92], 92 total) alongside Glen Maxwell's and Alastair's existing
  real-world ballast data points, and summarized in one line on
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (no new citation, per
  narrate-once-cross-link). Jack's own maiden-sail report confirmed the
  practical stakes directly: without enough ballast aboard (only a few
  sandbags, daylight running out after eight tacks to windward and a run back
  down), Jill sat on her waterline short of the full 9 in draft and felt a
  little tender, though she held her trim "without fuss for long periods" once
  settled and threw remarkably little wake for her short beam; a feared
  following-sea problem (the rear window not yet installed) proved unfounded,
  and he described a square (rather than the plans' curved) rubber tiller-hole
  boot with a cam cleat mounted on it, awkward to reach in practice. Jack then
  uploaded his first photos of the finished boat to a "Jill Baby" folder,
  including one showing what Paul mistook for "a hand operated outboard" —
  after a full night's sleep on the puzzle, Paul worked out for himself that
  it was a [yuloh](/design/yuloh-and-stowed-rig-retention.md), not a
  mechanical device at all. Extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill) (cites
  [104]-[113], 113 total, one pre-existing gap at [53] predating this batch)
  with the full ten-message continuation of the launch thread. No new person
  pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout (Dave
  Bolduc's advice cross-linked rather than re-narrated). Standing-open-items
  check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, Larry/newspipetr3,
  Derek Clark/dclark52001 (did NOT appear this batch, still not promoted),
  Fredrik, Jake, Lugger #2, kayaker37/Paul (resurfaced repeatedly this batch,
  already-known), paull01/Paul-Seattle, Paul V., Steve/Kansas City, Howard
  Welch, Jason Stancil, John Hall, Todd/ktsrfer, Alf Sawatzky, Shane Hersey,
  Bengt Genell, Bill/wam_53, Craig/crag8, Ron/churo65, Ron/rondune508, Phil/
  eliallegra(topsail@), Shorty/shortypen, LJGeib/second-Larry, Ken Preston/
  kenpreston46, James/jcbanik, Roger Nuffer/rogernuffer, Robert/boat142,
  Robert/carolfo(cheepwings), LEON/melodymann, William Serjeant, Hugh
  Miller(NZ), chadoora, roger chan/light369369, Matt Wagner/trisurfnturf,
  OzBill/bilbilau, Dave Blow/smallshelters, James Greene/james56greene, Lee
  Corbridge/allcritters all checked, none newly promoted; no genuinely new
  below-threshold correspondents this batch (all three participants — Jack/
  John Gardiner, Glen Maxwell, Dave Bolduc, Paul/kayaker37 — already
  established). Attribution-trap check: Jack Gardiner's From: header read

  two-distinct-UK-Jeffs, three-distinct-Larrys, two-distinct-Roberts, and

## 2026-07-07

* **Update (batch page_2261–page_2270, msgnum 2288-2297, continuing directly
  from batch 226's 2287, no new gap)**: 9-12 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 4 extended. The batch closes out the seven-batch "chinerunners"
  thread (opened batch 224) and its "polyester?" sub-thread, then reports a
  new build milestone. Jack Gardiner answered "Jeff UK"'s pinhole/corrosion
  concern about steel chine runners — coating a weld in epoxy is a known
  backyard steel-hull practice that fills any pinholes before priming and
  painting, with routine maintenance preventing the corrosion Jeff UK feared —
  and separately warned that beating or hammering bronze work-hardens it into
  a brittle state. Kjell Karlsson, working the hydrodynamic-curve question
  from his sheet-metal build, found Paul's kayak-chine analogy plausible but
  raised sharper, unresolved questions of his own about laminar/turbulent flow
  and whether the usual sideways-tripping mechanism even applies to a Paradox
  hull at all. On the practical side, Kjell worked through three fallback
  plans for bending his 2 mm bronze sheet's curve — hand-forming around a
  pattern (cast into doubt by Jack Gardiner's work-hardening warning),
  grinding and bonding on a curved strip ("sounds impossible"), and
  laminating offcut strips (ruled out since the divinycell core cannot take
  welding heat) — before accepting he would have to find out empirically
  whether the curve mattered; his own scrap-bronze tests suggested the metal
  was not brittle, the real obstacle being that 2 mm gauge is simply thick to
  bend by hand, and the sheet was already attached to the boat by the time
  the exchange closed. Separately, Bengt Genell (standing below-threshold
  correspondent) thanked the list for its earlier polyester-vs-epoxy replies
  and gave budget/location detail (central Sweden, ~2 hrs from the coast,
  trailerability priority, exterior-grade plywood); Kjell answered with a
  detailed lamination cautionary tale from his own 240 g/m² polyester cloth —
  "tricky round angles," his first attempt becoming "just a mess" until a
  professional painter rescued it — and practical advice (thinner cloth for
  a first attempt; an extra thin fiberglass layer over sanded polyester,
  which goes "fluffy, like velvet," to save on filler). Finally, Jack Gardiner
  **launched his Paradox on 11 September 2004**, naming her **"Jill"** after
  his wife, at Gaspee Point, Narragansett Bay, R.I. — deliberately chosen for
  the site's Revolutionary War history and the 9/11 date — reporting a calm
  first sail that "took to doing what she is hailed to do, beat to wind!,"
  an easy motion through a ship's wake, and an enthusiastic reception from
  onlookers at the ramp; Paul (`kayaker37`) sent congratulations and asked
  about visiting the boat in Connecticut. Extended
  [chine-runners/construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#bronze-versus-steel-if-a-builder-insists-on-a-shoe)
  (cites [42]-[47], 47 total) with the epoxy-over-welds/bronze-work-hardening
  rebuttal and the three-fallback-plans bronze-bending narrative, both folded
  into existing sections; extended
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#a-kayak-chine-analogy-for-the-deflector-view)
  (cite [33], 33 total) with Kjell's laminar/turbulent-flow follow-up;
  extended
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#polyester-resin-versus-epoxy)
  (cites [35]-[36], 36 total) with Bengt Genell's and Kjell's polyester
  follow-ups; extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill) (cites
  [101]-[103], 103 total) with a new "Launch: 'Jill'" section — his boat's
  name is now on record for the first time; and extended
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cites [40]-[44], 44
  total) with two new cross-link bullets (the bronze-bending saga, and the
  polyester-lamination cautionary tale). No new files created — per
  narrate-once-cross-link, Jack Gardiner's build and launch stayed on his
  existing person page rather than spinning up a new `boats/` page, since his
  whole build narrative has always lived there; flagged in the backlog for a
  future holistic review to reconsider if a substantial cruising narrative
  for "Jill" accumulates later. No prunings. Standing-open-items check: Jeff
  UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark/
  dclark52001 (did NOT appear, still not promoted), Fredrik, Jake, Lugger #2,
  kayaker37/Paul (resurfaced, already-known), paull01/Paul-Seattle, Paul V.,
  Steve/Kansas City, Howard Welch, Jason Stancil, John Hall, Todd/ktsrfer, Alf
  Sawatzky, Shane Hersey, Bengt Genell (resurfaced, already-known), Bill/
  wam_53, Craig/crag8, Ron/churo65, Ron/rondune508, Phil/eliallegra(topsail@),
  Shorty/shortypen, LJGeib/second-Larry, Ken Preston/kenpreston46, James/
  jcbanik, Roger Nuffer/rogernuffer, Robert/boat142, Robert/carolfo
  (cheepwings), LEON/melodymann, William Serjeant, Hugh Miller(NZ), chadoora,
  roger chan/light369369, Matt Wagner/trisurfnturf, OzBill/bilbilau, Dave
  Blow/smallshelters, James Greene/james56greene, Lee Corbridge/allcritters
  all checked, none newly promoted; no genuinely new below-threshold
  correspondents (all participants — John/Jack Gardiner, Kjell Karlsson,
  Bengt Genell, Paul/kayaker37 — already established). Attribution-trap

* **Update (batch page_2251–page_2260, msgnum 2278-2287, continuing directly
  from batch 225's 2277, no new gap)**: 9 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 6 extended. All ten messages continue the single **"chinerunners"**
  thread (opened batch 224, continued batch 225) across its full duration —
  every message in this batch falls on the same single day, 9 September 2004.
  Two strands ran through it: (1) Kjell Karlsson explained why he bonds metal
  with an elastic epoxy rather than fastening it — epoxy will not adhere to
  stainless steel, but a fellow Swedish builder of the related "Sofia" design
  bonds it anyway by heavily grinding the sheet and applying an elastic filler
  within seconds (the filler is markedly thixotropic), a bond that outlasted
  the polyester laminate beneath it when tested years later; bronze poses no
  comparable gluing problem, but he follows the same elastic-bond practice
  regardless. Paul (`kayaker37`) offered a kayak-chine analogy for the
  deflector mechanism (leaning into a curved hard chine steers the boat toward
  that side, similar to Phil Bolger's bottom-plated rudders), speculating a
  Paradox in trip-risk conditions should be sailed upright to present the
  runner's rounded radius rather than its sharper upper edge. (2) A new
  fabrication side-thread opened on whether chine runners could be built in
  **steel** on a steel-hulled Paradox: "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`, a below-
  threshold correspondent distinct from the established Jeff/"doryman_uk" —
  reconfirmed non-merged, see backlog) proposed bolting a hardwood runner onto
  a steel hull instead of fabricating the runner itself in steel; Jack
  Gardiner, drawing on his professional fabrication background and a long
  first-person account of shipyard-scale steel work (submarine steam-loop
  piping, confined-space boiler-tube welding), judged steel chine runners no
  real difficulty at any hull size, crediting modern affordable tools with
  putting the job within a backyard builder's reach; Glen Maxwell suggested
  welding a length of pipe along the runner's edge to form the curved radius,
  which Jack answered with a plasma-cutting alternative; Jeff UK countered
  that steel chine runners need at least two full-length welds, and even a
  skilled welder can leave a pinhole that lets water reach unprotected steel
  inside the runner and start corrosion from within — a failure he had seen
  firsthand in local boatyards with rusted-through steel boats — concluding a
  genuinely skilled welder is worth hiring for that joint specifically.
  Alastair proposed a lamination alternative to bending sheet metal to the
  curve at all: build up the edge's thickness from offcut strips laminated in
  layers (glued, riveted, brazed, or welded) until enough material
  accumulates to grind the final curve — a metal-specific analogue of his own
  plywood block-and-fill method. Separately, on a fully composite/metal hull,
  Glen Maxwell passed on a corrosion-control technique for bonding epoxy to
  **aluminum** (coat with epoxy first, then sand through the still-wet epoxy
  so the freshly abraded metal is never exposed to open air, applicable to
  bronze too) and recalled, from his own "Zoë" build, drilling and
  countersinking holes along a bonded ground-strip's edge so the cured epoxy
  in each hole acts like a rivet against peeling forces. No builder on record
  ever built or reported a steel-hulled Paradox with steel chine runners; the
  fabrication debate remained speculative, mirroring the 2003 "Steel?"
  thread's own outcome. Extended
  [chine-runners/construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#fabricating-chine-runners-in-steel-if-the-whole-hull-is-steel)
  (cites [35]-[41], 41 total) with a new "Fabricating chine runners in steel,
  if the whole hull is steel" section plus the epoxy/stainless bonding
  addition to "Bronze versus steel," and
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#a-kayak-chine-analogy-for-the-deflector-view)
  (cite [32], 32 total) with a new "A kayak-chine analogy for the deflector
  view" section, with cross-link summaries in
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [39], 39 total),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites [98]-[100], 100
  total), [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cite [220], 220 total),
  and [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md#building-zoë)
  (cite [225], 225 total). No new person pages (all six participants —
  Kjell Karlsson, "Jeff UK"/`roverticket`, Jack Gardiner, Paul/`kayaker37`,

## 2026-07-06

* **Update (batch page_2241–page_2250, msgnum 2268-2277, continuing directly
  from batch 224's 2267, no new gap)**: 8-9 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 6 extended. All ten messages continue the single **"chinerunners"**
  thread opened in batch 224, plus one short closing reply on the separate
  "new builder in Oz" thread. Centerpiece: the hydrodynamic-curve question
  widened but stayed unresolved. Paul (`kayaker37`) recalled Matt Layden's own
  *Beachcruising and Coastal Camping*, in which Warren Bailey described 1930s
  Key West sponging dinghies with a 4-inch-radius "knuckle-V" — a "non-trip
  chine" that let a boat surf sideways rather than trip — and noted Matt's own
  next boat after Swamp Thing's sideways-surfing capsize had gently radiused
  chines; unconvinced, Kjell Karlsson sharpened the question to whether the
  drawn "downward convexity" curve (roughly 15 mm radius) might make tripping
  *easier* than the opposite ("upward") curve would, while detailing his own
  sheet-metal build: a 2 mm, 70 kg bronze sheet (chosen over stainless steel
  for lightning-grounding), glued on with an elastic epoxy filler and no
  mechanical fasteners, with the 15 mm bend proving very hard to form in two
  directions even with a neighbor's mechanical-shop help. Alastair, restating
  his own plywood method (bottom ply proud of the sides, chine-runner wedge
  filled with wood blocks and epoxy, shaped with an angle grinder) and
  suggesting Kjell do the same in metal, offered his own guess that the curve
  really exists to protect the fiberglass laminate's edge rather than for any
  hydrodynamic reason; a separate correspondent, Robert (`carolfo`/
  `cheepwings`, standing below-threshold identity), asked the same metal-bottom
  question from the stainless-steel side and about the adhesive, which Kjell
  answered (NM/Nils Malmgren elastic epoxy, no mechanical fasteners) — folded
  into the existing bronze-vs-steel section as a settled bedding-method answer.
  No builder on record ever confirmed a hydrodynamic function for the curve one
  way or the other. Extended
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#the-chine-runner-curve-cosmetic-fabrication-choice-or-hydrodynamic-feature)
  (cites [23]-[31]),
  [chine-runners/construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#bronze-versus-steel-if-a-builder-insists-on-a-shoe)
  (cites [35]-[36]), with cross-link summaries in
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cites [35]-[38]) and
  [people/alastair.md](/people/alastair.md) (cites [218]-[219]). A minor
  aside in the same message — Kjell mentioned reworking rudder fittings after
  installing the main bronze rod askew, and building his own tiller from
  fiberglass and epoxy rather than wood — was folded into
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-rudder-fitting-rework-and-a-fiberglass-tiller)
  (cite [64]) as a routine build-progress note; a failed round-the-Baltic car
  trip (stopped by Finnish customs at the Tallinn ferry) was judged too thin
  to record. Separately, Glen Maxwell sent a brief closing reply on the "new
  builder in Oz" thread — "Congratulations Jack!!! Let us know how it goes,
  can't wait to see the pictures" — folded into
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#build-complete-sail-hoisted)
  (cite [97]) as the thread's final beat. No new person pages, no prunings,
  narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout. Standing-open-items check:

## 2026-07-05

* **Update (batch page_2231–page_2240, msgnum 2258-2267, continuing directly
  from batch 223's 2257, no new gap)**: 4-7 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 7 extended. Two centerpiece items plus a shorter build-progress
  note. (1) A new **"chinerunners" thread**: a Swedish builder laying up his
  bottom in sheet metal (rather than plywood) asked whether the chine
  runner's upward-curving outer edge served a genuine hydrodynamic function,
  since the curve was proving hard to reproduce in metal. Jack Gardiner
  suggested fabrication approaches (a hardwood pattern beaten or heat-formed
  around the sheet metal, or grinding thicker stock directly) and recalled a
  remark attributed to Matt Layden that the runner's outward dimension
  matters more than its exact shape; Alastair, initially confused about the
  bottom's fore-and-aft-only curvature, raised a gap-at-the-chine concern for
  an unbent sheet, which Jack Gardiner clarified was about the chine-runner
  profile's own radius; David Beard reported no upward curve at all on his
  own "Whisper," without a noticed difference; Paul (`kayaker37`) offered an
  uncorroborated guess (a sled effect when beaching, and/or protecting the
  fiberglass roving's edge). No definitive answer emerged. Extended
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#the-chine-runner-curve-cosmetic-fabrication-choice-or-hydrodynamic-feature)
  (cites [17]-[22]), with brief cross-link summaries added to
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [34]),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites [95]-[96]), and
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cite [233]). (2) Closing
  the **Australian plywood-sourcing sub-thread** opened in batch 223: John
  Hall (standing below-threshold identity since batch 190) gave his
  workaround for the local-vs-imported plywood sheet-size mismatch pinching
  bulkhead #3 — build it in two parts with a scarf on the bottom section,
  since a 2400 mm local sheet cannot supply the full 1214 mm width in one
  piece — and named two Australian timber alternatives to imported Gaboon:
  Hoop Pine (better-made veneers, in his view) or the cheaper but CCA-treated
  Proof Ply; Shane Hersey's sustainable-timber follow-up remained unanswered
  on record. Extended
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md#metric-vs-imperial-measuring)
  (cite [38]). (3) A **build-progress item**: Jack Gardiner reported his
  Paradox essentially complete and the sail hoisted, passing the news to
  William Serjeant (maintaining the UK build-count list) as another boat "to
  add to the list"; Serjeant thanked him and said he would follow up by
  e-mail for the boat's details. Extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [94]) and
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-and-worldwide-build-count)
  (cite [32]). Also closed the "roller reefing lug sail?" thread's true final
  beat: Glen Maxwell's brief good-wish reply to Jason Stancil's winter-boom
  plan, extending
  [design/furling-system.md](/design/furling-system.md#interest-from-outside-the-paradox-community)
  (cite [55]). No new person pages; no genuinely new correspondents (all
  participants are already-established identities); narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout.

* **Update (batch page_2221–page_2230, msgnum 2248-2257, continuing directly
  from batch 222's 2247, no new gap)**: 31 August - 4 September 2004. Two
  centerpiece items plus two smaller ones, no new files. (1)
  Closing out the **"roller reefing lug sail?" thread** left unanswered in
  `design/furling-system.md` since batch 220: a builder finishing a Bolger
  Micro Navigator with his own 200 sq ft lug sail had asked whether
  Paradox's furling gear was designed to avoid an awkward open-hatch
  reefing routine, whether the approach could be adapted to other lug rigs,
  and how the furling drum and swiveling tack fitting are built. Glen
  Maxwell answered in full over a week later — the fullest first-person
  description on record of the gear's mechanics (a bronze shaft riding in a
  bronze/copper pipe inside the boom end, a drum turned via a furling line
  led below deck, a tack fitting on a swiveling pipe fitting to clear the
  hatch when the rig is stowed) — judged it "about as simple a solution as
  I have come across" once built, declined to photocopy the plan sheets
  themselves out of respect for Matt Layden's authorship, and pointed to
  the photo section and a specific Files-section image (`TL07.jpg`) for
  visual reference. The builder, having had no other list reply, reported
  he had already built his own boom/yard traditionally to get sailing that
  season and planned a beefier tapered drum-and-thimble boom over the
  winter, crediting Matt Layden's "well thought out" design. Extended
  [design/furling-system.md](/design/furling-system.md#interest-from-outside-the-paradox-community)
  (cites [52]-[54], closing out [49]). (2) An **Australian/Tasmanian
  plywood-sourcing sub-thread**, continuing the "new builder in Oz" family
  of threads: Shane Hersey apologized for assuming the plans weren't
  metric and asked a new Australian builder about ply grade; John Hall
  (Launceston, Tasmania — a standing below-threshold identity since batch
  190's "Sheet Lead Ballast" thread) identified a genuine plywood-sourcing
  problem — Australian locally produced plywood runs 2400 x 1200 mm while
  imported Gaboon (Okoume) runs 2500 x 1220 mm, a closer fit to Matt
  Layden's own sheet layout, leaving bulkhead #3 (drawn 1214 mm wide) a
  tight fit against the local sheet size — and reported a 1:5 scale model
  well advanced toward CNC files for a local cutting service, offering to
  share the DXF files with Dave and Mindy Bolduc for distribution alongside
  the plans; Shane asked about a workaround and a preference for
  sustainably sourced local ply, left unanswered on record. Extended
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md#metric-vs-imperial-measuring)
  (cites [35]-[37]). Two smaller items: Nels and Jason Stancil both replied
  to batch 222's "New Member" thread confirming DIY scale-up is easy on the
  metric plans (15% by simple arithmetic, or Matt Layden's own "all clear"
  for a 20% enlargement on the same scantlings), extending
  [design/variants/stretched-paradox.md](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md#no-official-larger-plans-exist)
  (cites [61]-[62]), cross-linked from
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#storage-beachability-and-who-buys-the-beer);
  and William Serjeant, replying to Alastair's pointer to his own site,
  estimated "at least 3" more completed Paradoxes could be added to his
  existing list and invited builders to report new boats, extending
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-and-worldwide-build-count)
  (cite [31]); Dave Blow gave a routine building-manual progress update,
  extending [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md)
  (cite [51]). No new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout.

* **Update (batch page_2211–page_2220, msgnum 2238-2247, continuing directly
  from batch 221's 2237, no new gap)**: 28-31 August 2004. Three items, no
  new files. (1) Closing out the **"polyester?" thread** (opened batch 221):
  DC agreed with James Greene's verdict but floated a hybrid layup for the
  bottom specifically — epoxy for the first and last laminates, polyester
  for the layers between, provided both resins are properly dewaxed before
  the changeover — reasoning that the bottom glass functions mainly as a
  skidplate and secondary ballast rather than a structural bond. Nels
  pointed to designer Dynamite Payson, who built stitch-and-glue boats like
  Paradox using only polyester resin (ring nails for joinery/seam taping),
  many still afloat some 30 years on, though Payson himself now recommends
  epoxy where affordable; noted polyester cannot go over epoxy (though epoxy
  can go over polyester) and is a poor choice for a boat that stays wet most
  of the time. Alastair closed the thread with a one-line reminder that
  Little Cruiser has proved a Paradox-family hull can go to sea with nothing
  on the outside but paint — no fiberglass sheathing of either resin at all.
  Extended [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#polyester-resin-versus-epoxy)
  (cites [32]-[34]). (2) The **"new builder in Oz" thread's final coda**:
  Shane Hersey asked a new Australian builder how he was reconciling
  Imperial-measure US plans against Australian metric plywood; the builder
  confirmed his own plans arrived already in metric, apparently standard for
  the design, and shared a free unit-conversion utility. Derek Clark,
  reading along, double-checked his own bulkheads (already cut in
  millimetres) and confirmed his plans were metric too, then asked how many
  completed Paradoxes exist worldwide and how many are under construction in
  England — a question with no running total on record; Alastair pointed to
  Bill Serjeant's `smallsailboats.co.uk` site as holding "a fairly complete
  list," and could name only one interested-but-unconfirmed prospective
  Cornwall builder ("Neil") for the UK specifically. Extended
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md#metric-vs-imperial-measuring)
  (cites [32a]-[34]) and [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-and-worldwide-build-count)
  (cites [29]-[30]). (3) A new **"New Member" thread**: Lee Corbridge, DVM
  (Sebring, FL), introduced himself wanting a trailerable boat for Western
  US mountain-lake/coastal camp-cruising with an eventual Bahamas crossing,
  asking whether a bigger-than-14ft plan set exists, the build time and
  cost, and (in jest) who buys the beer — closing the loop first opened by
  the archive's original, long-unanswered newcomer-FAQ snapshot on
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#open-newcomer-questions-cabin-comfort-capacity-and-perceived-safety).
  Alastair answered directly this time: no larger-than-14ft plans exist,
  though one Australian builder had modified his own interior by removing
  the forward lockers to widen the cabin for two-person sleeping; Paradox
  carries far more storage than similarly sized boats; beaching itself is
  easy, but a falling tide strands the boat until it returns, and even with
  three helpers pushing a beached Paradox by hand proved impossible in
  practice; and by custom, whoever gets back last buys the drinks. Extended
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#storage-beachability-and-who-buys-the-beer)
  (new section, cites [24]-[25]), cross-linked from
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (cites
  [49]-[50]), [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#cost)
  (cites [70]-[71]), and a new cross-link section on
  [design/variants/stretched-paradox.md](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md#no-official-larger-plans-exist).
  Separately, Jack Gardiner, finishing his own build's mast/boom/yard, asked
  what size luff tape he needed and where to source it — left unanswered on
  record within this batch. Extended
  [construction/sailmaking.md](/construction/sailmaking.md#corner-patches-cut-tabling-and-the-luff-tape)
  (cite [16]), cross-linked from
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [93]). No new
  files, no renames, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. Standing-open-items check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie
